title: Lecture Guide — T04 · Decision Analysis
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_decision_analysis.xlsx, data/vungle_daily.csv · Lectures: 1 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: structure a business decision under uncertainty (alternatives × states → payoff table), pick the action that maximizes expected value, compute EVPI and EVSI to decide whether information is worth buying, revise odds with Bayes, and check the call’s robustness with sensitivity analysis — then write the Vungle rollout recommendation.


Goal of the day: the team makes the Vungle rollout call (and decides whether a bigger test is worth its cost), backed by EV / EVPI / EVSI.


Pose: “B looks better but we’re not sure. Roll out B to all traffic, keep A, or run a bigger test? Quantify it.” Stress the asymmetry: rolling out a wrong B degrades ALL revenue (not a 1/16 slice). Get each team to commit to a gut answer first — most will say “roll out B”; hold them to justifying it with a number by the end.



Teach in order: - Problem formulation: three lists — decision alternatives (roll out B / keep A / test more), states of nature (B truly better / not; mutually exclusive + exhaustive), payoffs. - Where payoffs come from: anchor in the data we already have — mean lift +$0.112/1000 × 236.5M impr/month ≈ $26.5K/mo ≈ $318K/yr. Roll-out payoff: +318 if B better, −160 if not (downside ≈ half the upside); keep A = 0/0. (The −160 is a managerial estimate — flag it as the number we’ll stress-test.) - Payoff table + decision tree: square = decision (you choose), circle = chance (nature chooses). - EV: \(EV(d_i)=\sum_j P(s_j)V_{ij}\); priors 0.60 / 0.40. Demo the textbook anchor Burger Prince first (Model C, EV = $14,000), then fold back the Vungle tree. - EVPI: best-per-state → EVwPI → EVPI; emphasize it’s the ceiling on any study’s value. - Sample info / Bayes / EVSI: the bigger test is imperfect info; conditional probs P(F|better)=0.80, P(F|not)=0.25; tabular Bayes (prior → joint → marginal → posterior); EVSI vs cost. - Sensitivity: indifference prior \(p^\*\).
Demo (Excel): open vungle_decision_analysis.xlsx. - Payoff table with priors in row 1; EV = =SUMPRODUCT($B$3:$C$3,B4:C4). - EVPI: =MAX(B4,B5) down each column, then =SUMPRODUCT(priors,best-per-state) minus best EV. - Bayes tables: Joint = prior*conditional; Marginal = SUM(joint); Posterior = joint/marginal. - EVSI: best EV after each signal, =SUMPRODUCT(P(signal),bestEV) − EVwoSI.
Quick checks (cold-call): - “Square or circle for ‘roll out B’?” (square — you choose). - “EV of roll-out is $126.8K. Will Vungle ever actually get $126.8K?” (No — it’s +318 or −160; EV is a long-run average / ranking device.) - “Can EVPI ever be negative?” (No — you can always ignore information.)



Task (mirror the deck): from vungle_decision_analysis.xlsx — 1. Payoff table + EV of roll-out vs. keep A (SUMPRODUCT, priors 0.60/0.40). 2. EVPI. 3. Posteriors from the Bayes tables → EVSI; compare to the $20K test cost. 4. Sensitivity: the tipping-point prior p*. Deliverable: 1 slide — the call · the EV · one caveat · and should they buy the bigger test?

Mentor as they work:

Make sure priors are in the SUMPRODUCT (a common slip is averaging the two payoffs → that’s the p=0.5 case, not 0.60).
Push them past “the bigger number wins” — ask why +126.8 beats 0 given the −160 downside.
For EVSI: have them notice that after an unfavorable report the best move is keep A (EV of roll-out goes negative), which is exactly why the test has any value.

Pitfalls:

Confusing EVwPI (190.8) with EVPI (64) — EVPI is the difference.
Using priors (0.60/0.40) instead of posteriors when computing the post-signal EVs.
Forgetting to subtract EVwoSI when getting EVSI; or comparing EVSI to EVPI instead of to cost.
Treating EV as a guaranteed payoff.

Answer key (instructor):

EV(roll out B) = 0.60(318) + 0.40(−160) = $126.8K; EV(keep A) = $0 → roll out B.
EVwPI = 0.60(318)+0.40(0) = $190.8K; EVPI = 190.8 − 126.8 = $64K.
Bayes (favorable): joint .48 / .10, P(F)=0.58, posteriors 0.828 / 0.172. Bayes (unfavorable): joint .12 / .30, P(U)=0.42, posteriors 0.286 / 0.714.
Post-signal EVs: favorable → roll out (EV $235.6K); unfavorable → keep A (roll-out EV = −$23.4K).
EVwSI = 0.58(235.6)+0.42(0) = $136.6K; EVSI = 136.6 − 126.8 = $9.84K (efficiency = 9.84/64 ≈ 15%). Test cost ≈ $20K → net −$10K → do NOT run the bigger test.
Sensitivity: $p^* = 160/(318+160) = $ 0.335; roll out B whenever P(B better) > 0.33. Our prior 0.60 clears it → robust.





Have 1–2 teams pitch. Land these points: - Roll out B — EV $126.8K > $0, robust (flips only below p* ≈ 0.33), and a further test isn’t worth its price (EVSI $9.84K < $20K cost). - EVPI is the ceiling on information value; a real test captures only a fraction (15% here). - Decision analysis vs. inference: the paired t said the lift is real; today turned that into what to do — inference and decision are partners, not substitutes. Surface the pitfall: EV is a long-run average that hides the −$160K downside — recommend monitoring eRPM post-rollout and being ready to revert. Acceptable for a team to argue “roll out to a larger slice first” if they justify it against the EVSI logic.



Homework (team client): frame one real decision in your case — alternatives, states of nature, payoff table; assign priors, compute EV and EVPI; give the recommendation and the tipping-point prior. Preview: back to the inference arc — discrete distributions for uncertain counts (installs tomorrow).





vungle_decision_analysis.xlsx (payoff table, priors, EVPI, Bayes favorable/unfavorable, EVSI, lift-from-data) and vungle_daily.csv in Excel. ToolPak installed (used elsewhere; today is mostly SUMPRODUCT + MAX formula cells, no ToolPak tool required).
Figures regenerate via Rscript _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic04_assets.R.
Numbers cross-checked against _adm_stuff/_redesign/ground_truth.md (lift +$0.112, CI 0.043–0.181, 236.5M A-impressions/month → ≈ $26.5K/month).





“EV is what we’ll earn.” (It’s a long-run average / ranking device; the realized payoff is +318 or −160.)
“EVPI = EVwPI.” (EVPI is the gain over deciding blind: EVwPI − EVwoPI.)
“A bigger test is always worth running.” (Only if its value (EVSI) beats its cost — here it doesn’t; the efficiency is just 15%.)
“Use the priors after the test.” (After a signal you decide on the posteriors.)
“Significant lift ⇒ roll out automatically.” (Inference is an input; the rollout is an EV decision that also weighs the downside and the cost of more information.)

title: Lecture Guide — T01 · Descriptive Statistics I (Course Intro)
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.xlsx · Lectures: 1 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: read a real dataset’s structure (elements / variables / type & scale), summarize its center (mean, median), and frame the Vungle roll-out decision — and say why a raw mean is not yet an answer.

Day-1 note: the first ~40 min is orientation (intro, logistics, instructor, textbook, survey). The studio loop below runs in the second half, on the descriptive content.



Goal of the day: turn “B’s average looks higher” into a precise data question.


Pose the Case Spotlight: “Vungle’s CEO must decide — roll out algorithm B or keep A? You have 30 days of data.” Show the funnel + the tempting “$3.46 vs $3.35 → roll out!” Get each team to commit to a gut call before any analysis.




What is this data? elements (60 algorithm-days), variables (7 columns), one observation (a row).
Types & scales: algorithm = categorical/nominal (count only); erpm = quantitative/ratio (mean valid). Land the rule: scale determines the toolbox.
Why an experiment? random assignment within each day ⟹ we may later say “B caused …”.
Measures of location: mean ( x̄ = Σxᵢ/n ), median, mode. Demo in Excel: Data → Data Analysis → Descriptive Statistics on the erpm column for A.
Quick checks: “Can we average algorithm?” (no) · “Mean vs median if there’s one huge outlier?”




Task (mirrors the deck): audit the data; with the ToolPak get mean & median eRPM for A and B; list two reasons B’s edge might not hold. Deliverable: 1 slide. - Mentor as they work: push past “B is bigger” — ask how big, how spread, how many days. Catch teams trying to average algorithm. - Pitfalls: averaging a categorical variable; reading a 30-day sample as the whole truth; ignoring spread. - Answer key (instructor): A → mean $3.347, median $3.327 · B → mean $3.459, median $3.434 · B’s edge ≈ +$0.11. Mean ≈ median ⇒ roughly symmetric. Good “what could go wrong” reasons: only 30 days (small sample / noise), B is more spread out, B serves ~15× less traffic, eRPM ≠ conversion (different metrics may disagree).



Take 1–2 teams’ calls. Land: we can now describe (B’s center is ~$0.11 higher over 30 designed days) but we cannot yet decide for all future traffic — that’s inference (later topics). Surface the pitfall: a higher sample mean is a hypothesis, not a verdict.



Homework (team client): write your client dataset’s data audit + compute its outcome’s mean & median. Preview: spread, shape, boxplots, association (T02).





vungle_daily.xlsx open; Analysis ToolPak installed (walk the install on screen — many students won’t have it yet). Have the Descriptive-Statistics output ready as a fallback.





“Average the algorithm column.” (categorical → count/proportion only)
“B’s mean is higher, so B wins.” (sample ≠ population; spread + sample size matter)
Mean and median are interchangeable. (only when roughly symmetric)

title: Lecture Guide — T08 · Hypothesis Testing I (One Population)
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv (worked solutions data/vungle_one_sample_tests.xlsx) · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: state \(H_0\)/\(H_a\) from a business decision, run a one-sample \(t\) (mean) and a one-sample \(z\) (proportion) in Excel, reach the same call by the critical-value and \(p\)-value roads, and separate statistical significance from practical importance.



Goal of the day: the team makes the CFO’s fund-B call by testing B’s mean eRPM against the $3.30 benchmark.


Pose: “The CFO funds B only if its mean eRPM clears the $3.30 benchmark. B’s 30-day average looks like $3.46 — real, or could 30 noisy days land there by luck? Test it.” Get every team to commit to a gut yes/no first. Frame the shift: Topic 7 estimated the mean (a range); today we test a specific claim (a decision).




Develop \(H_0\)/\(H_a\). \(H_0\) = the skeptic on trial (status quo, $3.30 line); \(H_a\) = what we want to prove (B beats it). Equality always in \(H_0\). The business decision picks the tail — Vungle is asymmetric (act only if better) → upper-tail. Walk the research-vs-challenge distinction (new algorithm = research → start with \(H_a\)).
Type I / II errors + \(\alpha\). Type I = fund a non-improvement (false alarm); Type II = miss a real B. You set \(\alpha\) before the test; \(\beta\) follows. Trade-off for fixed \(n\). Say “fail to reject,” never “accept.”
The \(t\) statistic (\(\sigma\) unknown): \(t = (\bar{x}-\mu_0)/(s/\sqrt{n})\), \(df = n-1\). Two roads: critical value (=T.INV(0.95,29)) and \(p\)-value (=T.DIST.RT(t,29)). Hammer the \(p\)-value meaning: \(P(\text{data this extreme} \mid H_0)\), not \(P(H_0)\). Show the four-level rubric.
Demo (Excel): anchor = Heathrow superior-service rating (book): \(\bar{x}=7.25\), \(s=1.052\), \(n=60\), \(\mu_0=7\) → \(t=1.84\), \(df=59\), \(t_{crit}=1.671\), \(p=0.035\) → reject (strong, not overwhelming). Then walk the Vungle eRPM result on the same five steps.
Quick checks: “Why upper-tail, not two-tailed?” (worse and same → same action). “Does a small \(p\) mean B is probably better, or that this data is unlikely under \(H_0\)?” (the latter).





Task: Descriptive Statistics on B’s erpm → \(\bar{x}, s, n\); formula cells for \(SE\), \(t\), upper-tail \(p\); build the 95% CI; decide at \(\alpha=0.05\). Deliver a 1-slide fund/don’t-fund verdict.
Mentor as they work: make sure they filter algorithm = B (not the whole column); \(\mu_0=3.30\) in the numerator (not \(\bar{x}\) of A); T.DIST.RT for the upper tail (not T.DIST which is left-tail). Push them past “reject” to the dollar translation.
Pitfalls: using A’s mean as the benchmark; two-tailed \(p\) when the test is one-tailed; reading the CI as “95% chance the mean is in here.”
Answer key (instructor): \(n=30\), \(\bar{x}=3.4590\), \(s=0.3444\), \(SE=0.0629\), \(t=2.529\), \(df=29\), one-tail \(p=0.0086\), \(t_{0.05,29}=1.699\) → reject \(H_0\). 95% CI for the mean = ($3.330, $3.588) — $3.30 falls just below it (duality). Even at \(\alpha=0.01\): \(t_{0.01,29}=2.462\), and \(2.529 > 2.462\) → still reject (use this as the threshold-matters moment).




1–2 teams pitch. Land: \(H_0\) is the conservative skeptic; the decision rests on \(t=2.53\), \(p=0.009\) → overwhelming evidence, fund B; the critical-value road, the \(p\)-value road, and the CI all agree. Surface the caveat: this says B beats a line ($3.30), not that B beats A — that two-population comparison is Topic 9 — and it assumes June represents future traffic.



Homework (team client): pick one mean with a real benchmark in your case; state \(H_0\)/\(H_a\), pick the tail from the decision, run the one-sample \(t\), write the call. Preview: same logic for a proportion next lecture.





Goal of the day: the team tests B’s conversion rate against the 0.35% viability target — and learns why “significant” isn’t the whole story at huge \(n\).


Pose: “Ops requires any algorithm to clear a 0.35% conversion rate (installs/impressions). Does B?” Note the parameter changed (mean → proportion) but the five-step recipe is identical.




Unit of observation flips: for eRPM the unit was the day (\(n=30\)); for conversion it’s the impression (\(n=15.8\)M, each a Bernoulli trial). Aggregate June B rows: installs ÷ impressions.
The \(z\) statistic: \(\sigma_{\bar p}=\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\) (computed under \(H_0\) → uses \(p_0\), not \(\bar p\)); \(z=(\bar p - p_0)/\sigma_{\bar p}\). Large-sample condition \(np_0\ge5\), \(n(1-p_0)\ge5\) (trivially met). Excel: =NORM.S.INV(0.95), =1-NORM.S.DIST(z,TRUE).
Demo (Excel): anchor = Pine Creek (book): 100/400 women, \(p_0=0.20\) → \(\sigma_{\bar p}=0.02\), \(z=2.50\), \(p=0.0062\) → reject. Then walk the Vungle conversion result.
The big idea: statistical vs. practical significance. At \(n\) in the millions the test is almost guaranteed to reject; the honest headline is the effect size, not the \(z\). Mirror with small-\(n\) low power (Type II). Use the “reading \(p\)-values like a manager” table (same data, different benchmark → different verdict).
Quick checks: “Is \(n\) 30 or 15.8 million here?” (impressions). “\(z=2.73\) is huge — does that mean the effect is large?” (No — it means it’s detectable; the gap is 0.0041 pts).





Task: =SUM B installs and impressions; \(\bar p=x/n\); formula cells for \(SE\), \(z\), upper-tail \(p\); decide at \(\alpha=0.05\); then state the effect size; 1 sentence each for call · number · caveat.
Mentor as they work: make sure \(SE\) uses \(p_0=0.0035\) (not \(\bar p\)); 1-NORM.S.DIST for the upper tail; push them to compute and name the effect size in percentage points.
Pitfalls: using daily proportions averaged (wrong \(n\)) instead of pooled totals; using \(\bar p\) in the SE; declaring “B is much better” off a big \(z\).
Answer key (instructor): installs \(x=56{,}031\), impressions \(n=15{,}825{,}376\), \(\bar p=0.3541\%\), \(\sigma_{\bar p}=1.485\times10^{-5}\), \(z=2.733\), one-tail \(p=0.0031\) → reject \(H_0\), B clears the target. Effect size = +0.0041 percentage points above 0.35% — tiny; significance is driven by the enormous \(n\). (\(np_0\approx55{,}000\gg5\).)




1–2 teams pitch. Land: same recipe as the mean test, only the SE and reference distribution changed; B is viable on conversion too; but the headline number is the effect size, not \(z=2.73\). Surface the pitfall: “large \(n\) ⇒ significant ⇒ important” is false — significance ≠ practical importance; and at small \(n\) real effects hide (power). Walk the one-page recipe table.



Homework (team client): pick one proportion with a target; run the one-sample \(z\) and report the effect size. Preview: Topic 9 — stop testing one population against a line and compare two (is B better than A?).






data/vungle_daily.csv open in Excel with the Analysis ToolPak installed (for Descriptive Statistics). One-sample \(t\) and one-proportion \(z\) have no ToolPak tool → both are formula-cell builds; pre-stage a blank template if teams are slow.
Worked solutions: data/vungle_one_sample_tests.xlsx (sheets: Raw_Data, One_Sample_t, One_Proportion_z, Heathrow_t, PineCreek_z).
Have the four-level \(p\)-value rubric and the threshold-matters (\(\alpha=0.01\) vs \(0.05\)) numbers ready to project.





“The \(p\)-value is the probability \(H_0\) is true.” No — it’s \(P(\text{data this extreme} \mid H_0)\). (\(p=0.009\) ≠ “0.9% chance B isn’t better.”)
“Fail to reject = accept \(H_0\) = the means are equal.” No — we just lacked evidence to overturn the skeptic (could be low power).
“Bigger \(z\)/\(t\) ⇒ bigger effect.” No — it means more detectable; with huge \(n\) a microscopic gap gives a huge \(z\). Always report the effect size.
“Pick the tail from the data.” No — the tail comes from the business decision, set before testing.
“Use A’s mean as the benchmark.” No — the benchmark is the fixed $3.30 line; this is a one-sample test (A isn’t the comparison yet — that’s Topic 9).

title: Lecture Guide — T08 · Hypothesis Testing I (One Population)
text:
Goal of the day: the team makes the CFO’s fund-B call by testing B’s mean eRPM against the $3.30 benchmark.


Pose: “The CFO funds B only if its mean eRPM clears the $3.30 benchmark. B’s 30-day average looks like $3.46 — real, or could 30 noisy days land there by luck? Test it.” Get every team to commit to a gut yes/no first. Frame the shift: Topic 7 estimated the mean (a range); today we test a specific claim (a decision).




Develop \(H_0\)/\(H_a\). \(H_0\) = the skeptic on trial (status quo, $3.30 line); \(H_a\) = what we want to prove (B beats it). Equality always in \(H_0\). The business decision picks the tail — Vungle is asymmetric (act only if better) → upper-tail. Walk the research-vs-challenge distinction (new algorithm = research → start with \(H_a\)).
Type I / II errors + \(\alpha\). Type I = fund a non-improvement (false alarm); Type II = miss a real B. You set \(\alpha\) before the test; \(\beta\) follows. Trade-off for fixed \(n\). Say “fail to reject,” never “accept.”
The \(t\) statistic (\(\sigma\) unknown): \(t = (\bar{x}-\mu_0)/(s/\sqrt{n})\), \(df = n-1\). Two roads: critical value (=T.INV(0.95,29)) and \(p\)-value (=T.DIST.RT(t,29)). Hammer the \(p\)-value meaning: \(P(\text{data this extreme} \mid H_0)\), not \(P(H_0)\). Show the four-level rubric.
Demo (Excel): anchor = Heathrow superior-service rating (book): \(\bar{x}=7.25\), \(s=1.052\), \(n=60\), \(\mu_0=7\) → \(t=1.84\), \(df=59\), \(t_{crit}=1.671\), \(p=0.035\) → reject (strong, not overwhelming). Then walk the Vungle eRPM result on the same five steps.
Quick checks: “Why upper-tail, not two-tailed?” (worse and same → same action). “Does a small \(p\) mean B is probably better, or that this data is unlikely under \(H_0\)?” (the latter).





Task: Descriptive Statistics on B’s erpm → \(\bar{x}, s, n\); formula cells for \(SE\), \(t\), upper-tail \(p\); build the 95% CI; decide at \(\alpha=0.05\). Deliver a 1-slide fund/don’t-fund verdict.
Mentor as they work: make sure they filter algorithm = B (not the whole column); \(\mu_0=3.30\) in the numerator (not \(\bar{x}\) of A); T.DIST.RT for the upper tail (not T.DIST which is left-tail). Push them past “reject” to the dollar translation.
Pitfalls: using A’s mean as the benchmark; two-tailed \(p\) when the test is one-tailed; reading the CI as “95% chance the mean is in here.”
Answer key (instructor): \(n=30\), \(\bar{x}=3.4590\), \(s=0.3444\), \(SE=0.0629\), \(t=2.529\), \(df=29\), one-tail \(p=0.0086\), \(t_{0.05,29}=1.699\) → reject \(H_0\). 95% CI for the mean = ($3.330, $3.588) — $3.30 falls just below it (duality). Even at \(\alpha=0.01\): \(t_{0.01,29}=2.462\), and \(2.529 > 2.462\) → still reject (use this as the threshold-matters moment).




1–2 teams pitch. Land: \(H_0\) is the conservative skeptic; the decision rests on \(t=2.53\), \(p=0.009\) → overwhelming evidence, fund B; the critical-value road, the \(p\)-value road, and the CI all agree. Surface the caveat: this says B beats a line ($3.30), not that B beats A — that two-population comparison is Topic 9 — and it assumes June represents future traffic.



Homework (team client): pick one mean with a real benchmark in your case; state \(H_0\)/\(H_a\), pick the tail from the decision, run the one-sample \(t\), write the call. Preview: same logic for a proportion next lecture.

title: Lecture Guide — T08 · Hypothesis Testing I (One Population)
text:
Goal of the day: the team tests B’s conversion rate against the 0.35% viability target — and learns why “significant” isn’t the whole story at huge \(n\).


Pose: “Ops requires any algorithm to clear a 0.35% conversion rate (installs/impressions). Does B?” Note the parameter changed (mean → proportion) but the five-step recipe is identical.




Unit of observation flips: for eRPM the unit was the day (\(n=30\)); for conversion it’s the impression (\(n=15.8\)M, each a Bernoulli trial). Aggregate June B rows: installs ÷ impressions.
The \(z\) statistic: \(\sigma_{\bar p}=\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}\) (computed under \(H_0\) → uses \(p_0\), not \(\bar p\)); \(z=(\bar p - p_0)/\sigma_{\bar p}\). Large-sample condition \(np_0\ge5\), \(n(1-p_0)\ge5\) (trivially met). Excel: =NORM.S.INV(0.95), =1-NORM.S.DIST(z,TRUE).
Demo (Excel): anchor = Pine Creek (book): 100/400 women, \(p_0=0.20\) → \(\sigma_{\bar p}=0.02\), \(z=2.50\), \(p=0.0062\) → reject. Then walk the Vungle conversion result.
The big idea: statistical vs. practical significance. At \(n\) in the millions the test is almost guaranteed to reject; the honest headline is the effect size, not the \(z\). Mirror with small-\(n\) low power (Type II). Use the “reading \(p\)-values like a manager” table (same data, different benchmark → different verdict).
Quick checks: “Is \(n\) 30 or 15.8 million here?” (impressions). “\(z=2.73\) is huge — does that mean the effect is large?” (No — it means it’s detectable; the gap is 0.0041 pts).





Task: =SUM B installs and impressions; \(\bar p=x/n\); formula cells for \(SE\), \(z\), upper-tail \(p\); decide at \(\alpha=0.05\); then state the effect size; 1 sentence each for call · number · caveat.
Mentor as they work: make sure \(SE\) uses \(p_0=0.0035\) (not \(\bar p\)); 1-NORM.S.DIST for the upper tail; push them to compute and name the effect size in percentage points.
Pitfalls: using daily proportions averaged (wrong \(n\)) instead of pooled totals; using \(\bar p\) in the SE; declaring “B is much better” off a big \(z\).
Answer key (instructor): installs \(x=56{,}031\), impressions \(n=15{,}825{,}376\), \(\bar p=0.3541\%\), \(\sigma_{\bar p}=1.485\times10^{-5}\), \(z=2.733\), one-tail \(p=0.0031\) → reject \(H_0\), B clears the target. Effect size = +0.0041 percentage points above 0.35% — tiny; significance is driven by the enormous \(n\). (\(np_0\approx55{,}000\gg5\).)




1–2 teams pitch. Land: same recipe as the mean test, only the SE and reference distribution changed; B is viable on conversion too; but the headline number is the effect size, not \(z=2.73\). Surface the pitfall: “large \(n\) ⇒ significant ⇒ important” is false — significance ≠ practical importance; and at small \(n\) real effects hide (power). Walk the one-page recipe table.



Homework (team client): pick one proportion with a target; run the one-sample \(z\) and report the effect size. Preview: Topic 9 — stop testing one population against a line and compare two (is B better than A?).

title: Lecture Guide — T02 · Descriptive Statistics II: Numerical Measures
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv, data/vungle_descriptive.xlsx · Lectures: 1 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: describe a variable beyond its center — quantify spread (range, IQR, variance, SD, CV), read shape (skewness) and relative location (z-scores, Empirical Rule, Chebyshev), flag outliers, build a five-number summary + boxplot, measure association (covariance, correlation), and assemble an honest A-vs-B dashboard that sets up the inference question “is B’s edge real or noise?”.


Goal of the day: the team builds a center+spread+boxplot dashboard for A vs. B and writes the two-sentence read a manager would act on — while learning that description alone cannot settle “real vs. noise.”


Pose: “B’s daily eRPM ran ~$0.11 above A’s. Before I brief the CEO — is that edge real, or just noise? How confident am I in that one number?” Get teams to commit to a gut answer. Then plant the hook with the spread_vs_center figure: two campaigns, same mean $3.46, opposite risk — the mean alone cannot tell them apart. Today we add the other half of describing data.




Variability first. Range → IQR (middle 50%, robust to extremes) → variance (avg squared deviation; why n−1: one df spent estimating x̄) → SD (back in original units) → CV (unit-free spread, =STDEV.S/AVERAGE).
Demo (Excel): Data → Data Analysis → Descriptive Statistics on the erpm column for Algorithm B — one click returns mean, SE, median, SD, variance, skewness, kurtosis, range, min, max. Anchor = two-suppliers table (same mean 15 hrs; Carrier A range 2 / CV 4.7% vs. Carrier B range 12 / CV 32.3%) → “the mean could not tell them apart, variability did.”
Shape & relative location. Skewness (and the quick mean-vs-median check); z-scores (=STANDARDIZE); Empirical Rule (bell-only 68/95/99.7) vs. Chebyshev (any shape, ≥75/89/94); two outlier rules (|z|>3 vs. 1.5×IQR) that can disagree.
Quick checks: (1) “Variance is in what units?” (dollars²) → motivates SD. (2) “When can you NOT use the Empirical Rule?” (when the data are not bell-shaped → fall back to Chebyshev).




Task / data / deliverable (mirror the deck’s sprint slide): with vungle_descriptive.xlsx (or vungle_daily.csv), teams compute center+spread for A and B, check skew + |z|>3 outliers, build the five-number summary and a Box & Whisker chart, and write two sentences (A-vs-B on center+spread; what it means for rollout). - Mentor as they work: push past “B’s mean is higher” — make them name the spread and read the CV. Probe: “If you were the CEO, does the extra volatility change the bet?” Watch they filter erpm by algorithm correctly (separate A and B columns) and use the .S (sample) functions, not .P. Make sure the boxplot has both A and B on one chart. - Pitfalls: (a) reporting only the mean; (b) using VAR.P/STDEV.P (population) on the 30-day sample; (c) calling June 2 an outlier “because the boxplot flags it” without noting |z| = 2.53 < 3; (d) concluding “B is better” — that is inference, not description. - Answer key (instructor):



Measure
Algorithm A
Algorithm B




Mean
3.347
3.459


Median
3.327
3.434


Range
0.887
1.486


IQR (Q3−Q1)
0.264
0.350


Variance s²
0.047
0.119


Std dev s
0.217
0.344


CV
6.5%
10.0%


Skewness
+0.24
−0.47



Five-number summaries — A: 2.943 / 3.214 / 3.327 / 3.478 / 3.830; B: 2.587 / 3.324 / 3.434 / 3.674 / 4.073 (Excel QUARTILE.INC = R type 7). Outliers: none under |z|>3 (B’s most extreme is June 2, $2.587, z = −2.53); the 1.5×IQR boxplot rule would flag June 2 ($2.587) and June 3 ($2.755) for B (lower fence ≈ $2.80) — a deliberate teaching disagreement. Empirical Rule on B (x̄ = 3.459, s = 0.344): observed 70% / 93% / 100% within ±1/±2/±3 SD vs. predicted 68/95/99.7 → close to bell-shaped. Model two-sentence read: “Over 30 days B averaged $0.11 more per 1,000 impressions than A but with ~50% more day-to-day volatility (CV 10% vs. 6.5%); whether that $0.11 edge is real signal or noise is a question description can’t settle — it needs a hypothesis test.”



Have 1–2 teams pitch their dashboard. Land these points: (1) the mean is half the story — B wins on center but loses on stability (higher SD/CV); higher reward, higher risk. (2) Outlier rules can disagree — a flag is an invitation to investigate, not a delete button. (3) The honest read stops at description: “is the $0.11 edge real or noise?” is an inference question (Topics 7–9), not something a summary table can answer. Surface the pitfall: teams that wrote “B is better, roll it out” — walk them back to “B’s sample mean is higher; we have not yet shown the edge generalizes.”



Homework (team client): build a descriptive dashboard of your case’s key outcome — center (mean/median), spread (SD, IQR, CV), a boxplot, and the correlation between the outcome and one plausible driver; write the two-sentence managerial read. Preview: Probability — the chance a random impression installs, and how that chance shifts once it clicks.





vungle_daily.csv and the worked workbook vungle_descriptive.xlsx in the topic data/ folder (regenerate with Rscript _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic02_assets.R). Analysis ToolPak installed; Excel 2016+ for the native Box & Whisker chart.
Workbook tabs: Vungle_Daily (raw), Descriptive_A, Descriptive_B, Five_Number_Summary, Association (covariance/correlation with formulas).
Association demo numbers (Algorithm A, clicks & installs in thousands): covariance +163.7, SD clicks 48.69, SD installs 3.67, r = +0.915; check 163.7/(48.69×3.67) = 0.915.





“B is better — its mean is higher.” (description ≠ inference; the edge may be sampling noise — that’s next block.)
“A bigger standard deviation is bad.” (it’s risk, not error; pair it with the mean via CV before judging.)
“Use the Empirical Rule on any data.” (it needs a bell shape; for arbitrary shapes use Chebyshev’s weaker bound.)
“The boxplot flagged it, so it’s an outlier — drop it.” (|z|>3 and 1.5×IQR can disagree; investigate the why first.)
“High correlation means clicks cause installs.” (correlation ≠ causation; both ride on daily traffic volume — only the experiment licenses causal talk.)
“Covariance of 163.7 is a strong relationship.” (covariance magnitude is unit-dependent and uninterpretable; use r for strength.)

title: Lecture Guide — T05 · Discrete Probability Distributions
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv, data/vungle_discrete.xlsx · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: put a probability distribution (Binomial or Poisson) on a random count of installs, compute its mean/variance, and read a tail probability \(P(X \geq N)\) to make a defensible capacity-planning recommendation.

Framing the whole topic: the spine shifts from “is B better?” (inference, T01–T09) to “can we handle the installs?” (modeling a random count). Installs fire a postback the ad server must absorb; engineering needs the distribution of installs per traffic burst, not just the average. The Brief for both lectures: “P(at least N installs)? Size the pipeline.” Conversion rates from the data: A \(\approx\) 0.40% (\(p \approx 0.0040\)), B \(\approx\) 0.354% (\(p \approx 0.0035\)). A “burst” = 1,000 impressions → \(\lambda_A = 4.0\), \(\lambda_B = 3.5\) (clean teaching anchors).




Goal of the day: the team models installs in a 1,000-impression burst as Binomial and computes the tail \(P(X \geq 8)\) that sizes a postback worker.


Pose: “For a typical burst of traffic, what’s the probability of at least N installs — so we can size the postback pipeline?” Get teams to commit to a gut answer (most will under-guess the tail). Tell them the plan: demo on textbook anchors → your team models Vungle (Sprint 1) → debrief the capacity call.




Random variables: discrete (counted) vs. continuous (measured); finite vs. countably infinite. Quick check: classify installs/burst (discrete-finite), eRPM (continuous), arrivals/min (discrete-∞).
Discrete distribution: \(f(x) = P(X=x)\); conditions \(f(x)\ge 0\) and \(\sum f(x)=1\). Anchor = JSL Appliances TV-sales table.
Expected value & variance: \(E(X)=\sum x f(x)\), \(\sigma^2 = \sum (x-\mu)^2 f(x)\). Demo the JSL worksheet (\(\mu = 1.20\), \(\sigma^2 = 1.660\), \(\sigma = 1.288\)). Show the Excel excel_ev_var.png panel (two formula columns — no ToolPak tool).
Binomial: the four assumptions; Bernoulli trial → count of successes. PMF \(f(x)=\binom{n}{x}p^x(1-p)^{n-x}\); \(\mu = np\), \(\sigma^2 = np(1-p)\). Anchor = Evans Electronics (\(n=3\), \(p=0.10\)): \(f(1)=0.243\), \(P(\ge 1)=0.271\). Then BINOM.DIST(x,n,p,FALSE/TRUE) and the complement trick for the tail.
Walk the Vungle burst: estimate \(p\) from installs/impressions; \(X_B \sim\) Binomial(1000, 0.0035).
Quick checks: “Why is installs/burst Binomial but eRPM not?” (count vs. measure). “Write \(P(X\ge 8)\) using a CDF.” (\(=1-P(X\le 7)\)).




Teams model Algorithm A bursts (\(n=1000\), \(p=0.0040\)) in vungle_discrete.xlsx: hand-compute \(E(X)\) and \(\sigma\), build the PMF/CDF with BINOM.DIST, compute \(P(X \ge 8)\), and state a capacity rec. - Mentor: make sure they use \(p = 0.0040\) for A (not B’s 0.0035); push them to read the tail off the cumulative column, not sum the PMF by hand; ask “what does 5% of bursts overflowing cost?” - Pitfalls: confusing \(P(X=8)\) with \(P(X\ge 8)\); using BINOM.DIST(8,...) instead of 7 in the complement; forgetting installs are a count so the model is discrete. - Answer key (Algorithm A, \(n=1000\), \(p=0.0040\)): - \(E(X) = np = 1000 \times 0.004 = \mathbf{4.0}\) installs; \(\sigma = \sqrt{1000(0.004)(0.996)} = \mathbf{1.996}\). - \(P(X = 8) = 0.0297\); \(P(X \le 7) = 0.9492\); \(P(X \ge 8) = 1 - 0.9492 = \mathbf{0.0508}\) (≈ 5.1%, ~1 burst in 20). - Capacity rec: a single worker (cap 7) overflows on ~5% of A’s bursts → provision a second worker / queue for the tail, or set capacity at \(N=10\) (\(P(X\ge 10)=0.0081 < 1\%\)).



1–2 teams pitch. Land: model the count → every capacity question is a tail probability; A’s tail (0.051) is double B’s (0.027) because A converts at a higher rate (higher \(\lambda\) = heavier tail). Surface the pitfall: the Binomial assumes constant, independent \(p\) — fine within a stable burst, broken by a viral spike.



Homework (team client): a yes/no count over fixed \(n\) (defects, conversions, churn) → Binomial; report \(E(X)\), \(\sigma\), one tail prob via BINOM.DIST. Preview: A’s \(p\) is tiny and \(n\) huge — the Poisson is the simpler model built for exactly that.





Goal of the day: the team re-models installs as Poisson(\(\lambda\)), confirms it matches the Binomial, and uses it to set a pipeline capacity with \(P(X \ge N) < 1\%\).


Pose: “Installs are rare — is there a model that needs only the average rate, not \(n\) and \(p\)? And does it give the same capacity answer?” Tie back to L1: A’s tail was 5%; today we get there from one number.




Poisson setup: count of rare arrivals over an interval; no fixed \(n\). Two assumptions (constant rate, independent intervals). One parameter \(\lambda\) = mean occurrences per interval.
PMF \(f(x) = \lambda^x e^{-\lambda}/x!\); key property \(E(X) = \text{Var}(X) = \lambda\). Anchor = Mercy Hospital: 6/hour → rescale to \(\lambda = 3\) per half-hour; \(f(4) = 0.1680\) (matches book). Stress the units/rescaling point.
POISSON.DIST(x, lambda, FALSE/TRUE); tail via complement. =POISSON.DIST(4,3,FALSE) → 0.1680.
Binomial ≈ Poisson: large \(n\), small \(p\) → Poisson(\(np\)). Rule of thumb \(n\ge 20\), \(p\le 0.05\). Show binom_vs_poisson.png and excel_binom_vs_poisson.png — bars/columns match to 3 decimals.
Walk the Vungle pipeline: \(X_A \sim\) Poisson(4.0), \(X_B \sim\) Poisson(3.5); rate scales with the interval (burst → day → second) — the Poisson superpower.
Quick checks: “What’s \(\lambda\) for installs per second if per-burst is 3.5?” (depends on burst duration — emphasize matching the interval). “Mean and variance of Poisson(4)?” (both 4).




Teams model Algorithm A as Poisson(\(\lambda = 4.0\)): build the PMF with POISSON.DIST, lay BINOM.DIST(x,1000,0.004,FALSE) beside it and confirm the match, compute \(P(X \ge 8)\), and pick the smallest capacity \(N\) with \(P(X \ge N) < 1\%\). - Mentor: confirm they see the Binomial and Poisson columns agree; push on “what interval is \(\lambda\) measured over?”; for the capacity \(N\), have them scan the CDF for the first \(1 - \text{CDF} < 0.01\). - Pitfalls: plugging \(\lambda = 0.004\) instead of 4 (forgetting \(\lambda = np\)); mixing intervals; reading \(P(X = N)\) instead of the tail. - Answer key (Algorithm A, \(\lambda = 4.0\)): - \(E(X) = \text{Var}(X) = \lambda = \mathbf{4.0}\); \(\sigma = 2.0\). - \(P(X = 8) = 0.0298\); \(P(X \le 7) = 0.9489\); \(P(X \ge 8) = \mathbf{0.0511}\) (Binomial gave 0.0508 — same call). - Capacity \(N\) for \(<1\%\) tail: \(P(X \ge 10) = 0.0081 < 0.01\) → \(N = 10\) (since \(P(X\ge 9)=0.0214\) is still >1%). - For B (\(\lambda = 3.5\)): \(P(X \ge 8) = 0.0267\); \(P(X \ge 9) = 0.0099 < 1\%\) → \(N = 9\).



1–2 teams pitch. Land: Poisson models a count from one rate; for Vungle’s tiny \(p\) it equals the Binomial answer; the rate rescales to any interval (provision for \(\lambda\) + a tail buffer). Walk the bridge table. Surface the caveat: Poisson assumes constant, independent arrivals — a viral spike clusters them, so the real tail is heavier than Poisson and you under-provision (over-dispersion: variance > mean).



Homework (team client): a rare-event count over an interval (defects/batch, complaints/week, arrivals/hour) → estimate \(\lambda\), build the PMF with POISSON.DIST, report a tail prob supporting a capacity/staffing call. Preview: continuous measurements → the Normal distribution and “P(eRPM > $3.50)?”






vungle_daily.csv and vungle_discrete.xlsx open in Excel; ToolPak installed (the binomial/Poisson functions are worksheet functions, not ToolPak tools — but keep ToolPak handy for the descriptive-stats refresh). Workbook tabs: Daily_Data, Binomial_burst, Binom_vs_Poisson, Expected_Value.
Figures regenerated by _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic05_assets.R (run before class if data changes). All anchor numbers cross-checked: JSL (\(\mu=1.20\), \(\sigma^2=1.660\)), Evans (\(f(1)=0.243\)), Mercy (\(f(4)=0.1680\)).





“\(P(X = N)\) is the capacity answer.” Capacity is a tail: \(P(X \ge N) = 1 - P(X \le N-1)\).
“Use \(p\) as \(\lambda\).” \(\lambda = np\) — the expected count, not the probability. (\(p=0.004 \to \lambda=4\).)
“Binomial and Poisson are different answers.” For large \(n\), small \(p\) they agree to 3+ decimals; Poisson is just the simpler shortcut.
“\(E(X)\) must be a possible value.” It need not be — JSL’s \(E(X)=1.2\) TVs; a burst’s \(E(X)=3.5\) installs.
“Same \(\lambda\) regardless of interval.” \(\lambda\) must match the interval; rescale rate when you change time/space windows (6/hr = 3/half-hr).
“Real traffic is exactly Poisson.” Viral spikes cause clustering/over-dispersion — the tail is heavier than Poisson; treat the model as a planning baseline, then add a buffer.

title: Lecture Guide — T05 · Discrete Probability Distributions
text:
Goal of the day: the team models installs in a 1,000-impression burst as Binomial and computes the tail \(P(X \geq 8)\) that sizes a postback worker.


Pose: “For a typical burst of traffic, what’s the probability of at least N installs — so we can size the postback pipeline?” Get teams to commit to a gut answer (most will under-guess the tail). Tell them the plan: demo on textbook anchors → your team models Vungle (Sprint 1) → debrief the capacity call.




Random variables: discrete (counted) vs. continuous (measured); finite vs. countably infinite. Quick check: classify installs/burst (discrete-finite), eRPM (continuous), arrivals/min (discrete-∞).
Discrete distribution: \(f(x) = P(X=x)\); conditions \(f(x)\ge 0\) and \(\sum f(x)=1\). Anchor = JSL Appliances TV-sales table.
Expected value & variance: \(E(X)=\sum x f(x)\), \(\sigma^2 = \sum (x-\mu)^2 f(x)\). Demo the JSL worksheet (\(\mu = 1.20\), \(\sigma^2 = 1.660\), \(\sigma = 1.288\)). Show the Excel excel_ev_var.png panel (two formula columns — no ToolPak tool).
Binomial: the four assumptions; Bernoulli trial → count of successes. PMF \(f(x)=\binom{n}{x}p^x(1-p)^{n-x}\); \(\mu = np\), \(\sigma^2 = np(1-p)\). Anchor = Evans Electronics (\(n=3\), \(p=0.10\)): \(f(1)=0.243\), \(P(\ge 1)=0.271\). Then BINOM.DIST(x,n,p,FALSE/TRUE) and the complement trick for the tail.
Walk the Vungle burst: estimate \(p\) from installs/impressions; \(X_B \sim\) Binomial(1000, 0.0035).
Quick checks: “Why is installs/burst Binomial but eRPM not?” (count vs. measure). “Write \(P(X\ge 8)\) using a CDF.” (\(=1-P(X\le 7)\)).




Teams model Algorithm A bursts (\(n=1000\), \(p=0.0040\)) in vungle_discrete.xlsx: hand-compute \(E(X)\) and \(\sigma\), build the PMF/CDF with BINOM.DIST, compute \(P(X \ge 8)\), and state a capacity rec. - Mentor: make sure they use \(p = 0.0040\) for A (not B’s 0.0035); push them to read the tail off the cumulative column, not sum the PMF by hand; ask “what does 5% of bursts overflowing cost?” - Pitfalls: confusing \(P(X=8)\) with \(P(X\ge 8)\); using BINOM.DIST(8,...) instead of 7 in the complement; forgetting installs are a count so the model is discrete. - Answer key (Algorithm A, \(n=1000\), \(p=0.0040\)): - \(E(X) = np = 1000 \times 0.004 = \mathbf{4.0}\) installs; \(\sigma = \sqrt{1000(0.004)(0.996)} = \mathbf{1.996}\). - \(P(X = 8) = 0.0297\); \(P(X \le 7) = 0.9492\); \(P(X \ge 8) = 1 - 0.9492 = \mathbf{0.0508}\) (≈ 5.1%, ~1 burst in 20). - Capacity rec: a single worker (cap 7) overflows on ~5% of A’s bursts → provision a second worker / queue for the tail, or set capacity at \(N=10\) (\(P(X\ge 10)=0.0081 < 1\%\)).



1–2 teams pitch. Land: model the count → every capacity question is a tail probability; A’s tail (0.051) is double B’s (0.027) because A converts at a higher rate (higher \(\lambda\) = heavier tail). Surface the pitfall: the Binomial assumes constant, independent \(p\) — fine within a stable burst, broken by a viral spike.



Homework (team client): a yes/no count over fixed \(n\) (defects, conversions, churn) → Binomial; report \(E(X)\), \(\sigma\), one tail prob via BINOM.DIST. Preview: A’s \(p\) is tiny and \(n\) huge — the Poisson is the simpler model built for exactly that.

text:
vungle_daily.csv and vungle_discrete.xlsx open in Excel; ToolPak installed (the binomial/Poisson functions are worksheet functions, not ToolPak tools — but keep ToolPak handy for the descriptive-stats refresh). Workbook tabs: Daily_Data, Binomial_burst, Binom_vs_Poisson, Expected_Value.

text:
“\(P(X = N)\) is the capacity answer.” Capacity is a tail: \(P(X \ge N) = 1 - P(X \le N-1)\).

title: Lecture Guide — T10 · Simple Linear Regression
text:
Spine case: Auto-parts retail · Data: data/autoparts.csv · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: fit sales ~ yelp_rating, interpret the slope in dollars-per-star and read \(R^2\) (L1); test the slope, validate the assumptions with residual plots, and forecast a new store with the right interval (L2) — then turn it into a site recommendation.

New client this topic. The Vungle arc is closed; we now drive everything off the 45-store auto-parts dataset. Anchor demo = Armand’s Pizza (textbook). Worked solutions live in data/autoparts_regression.xlsx.




Goal of the day: the team fits the line, says what one Yelp star is worth, and reports \(R^2\).


Pose: “Does a store’s Yelp rating predict its annual sales? If so, by how much?” Frame the new client (auto-parts retailer scoring sites). Get teams to gut-guess: will rating predict sales, and roughly how strongly? Park the guesses; revisit at debrief.




From comparing to predicting. We stop contrasting two groups and start modeling how \(y\) moves with \(x\). Define dependent (\(y\) = sales) vs. independent (\(x\) = Yelp rating); model \(y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon\); estimated line \(\hat y = b_0 + b_1 x\).
Just look (scatter, autoparts_scatter.png): upward cloud with spread — motivates a line + a fit number.
Least squares: residual \(e_i = y_i - \hat y_i\); criterion = minimize \(\sum e_i^2\). Slope/intercept formulas; in Excel use =SLOPE, =INTERCEPT, or the full Regression tool.
Demo (anchor): Armand’s Pizza — \(\hat y = 60 + 5x\); slope = “+$5K quarterly sales per 1,000 students.” Show armands_fit.png.
Demo (spine): run Data → Data Analysis → Regression on autoparts (Y = sales2012_k, X = yelp_rating, check Labels). Read \(\hat y = 332.8 + 757.5\,x\); slope = “+$757.5K per star”; \(R^2 = 0.834\). Decompose SST = SSR + SSE; tie \(r = \sqrt{R^2} = 0.913\).
Quick checks: “Interpret the intercept” (anchors the line; no 0-star store — don’t over-read). “What’s the other 17%?” (everything else — location, merchandising → motivates next topic).




Teams run Regression on autoparts.csv (Y = sales2012_k, X = yelp_rating), write the equation, interpret the slope in dollars-per-star, and read \(R^2\). - Mentor as they work: make sure Y and X aren’t swapped (slope should be ~+757, not the inverse); push them to say the slope in dollars (“each star ≈ +$757K”), not “0.83” or a bare coefficient. Ask the over-eager team to state the unexplained share out loud. - Pitfalls: interpreting the intercept as “sales of a 0-star store”; reporting \(R^2\) as a percentage of stores instead of variation; confusing \(r\) and \(R^2\). - Answer key (instructor): - \(\hat y = 332.77 + 757.51\,x\) · slope = +757.51 ($K per Yelp star) · intercept = +332.77 ($K). - R Square = 0.8344 (Yelp explains ~83% of the store-to-store sales variation; ~17% unexplained). - Multiple R (= \(r\)) = 0.9135; Standard Error \(s\) = 463.7; Observations = 45. - SST = 55,840,034; SSR = 46,594,785; SSE = 9,245,249.



1–2 teams pitch. Land: a strong, usable predictor — ~$757K/star, \(R^2 = 0.834\) — good enough to score sites. Surface the pitfalls: “explains” = association, not cause; 17% unexplained means one variable is rarely the whole story. We have a fitted line but have not yet asked if it’s statistically real or whether assumptions hold → that’s Lecture 2.



Homework (team client): pick a numeric predictor of your outcome; fit the simple regression; write the slope in business units; report \(R^2\). Preview: is the slope real, do the assumptions hold, and how do we forecast a new case?





Goal of the day: the team tests the slope, checks the residual plot, and forecasts a 4.5-star site with the correct interval — then makes the lease call.


Pose: “The fitted slope is +757.5. But 45 stores is a sample — is the relationship real, do the assumptions hold, and what should we forecast for a specific new site?” Distinguish describing the sample (L1) from inference to the population (L2).




Four assumptions on \(\varepsilon\): mean 0, constant variance (homoscedasticity), independence, normality. Flag up front; check with residuals later.
Standard error of the estimate \(s = \sqrt{\text{SSE}/(n-2)} \approx \$464K\) (typical vertical miss).
t-test on the slope: \(H_0:\beta_1 = 0\) vs. \(H_a:\beta_1 \ne 0\); \(t = b_1/s_{b_1}\), \(df = n-2\). Demo on Armand’s (\(t = 8.62\)) then spine. Read \(t\), \(p\), Lower/Upper 95% straight off the output.
CI for slope and the CI ⇔ test equivalence (zero outside CI ⇒ reject). Note \(F = t^2\) (same conclusion in simple regression; they diverge with >1 predictor next topic).
Significance ≠ causation: reverse causation, lurking variables; no extrapolation past the data.
Residual analysis: residual plot vs. \(x\) should be a horizontal band; curve → nonlinear (add \(x^2\)), fan → non-constant variance (transform \(y\)). Show autoparts_residual_plot.png. Standardized residuals; \(|z| > 2\) flags outliers (autoparts_std_residual_plot.png).
Prediction: point \(\hat y\) at \(x^* = 4.5\); CI (mean store, narrow) vs. PI (one new store, wide — the extra “1” under the root). Four width levers: confidence, \(s\), \(n\), distance of \(x^*\) from \(\bar x\). Show autoparts_pred_band.png.




Teams: (1) state \(t\), \(p\), and the 95% CI for the slope; (2) eyeball the residual plot for fan/curve; (3) forecast Yelp = 4.5 and decide CI vs. PI for one lease. - Mentor as they work: make sure they quote the slope’s \(t\)/CI (not the intercept’s); push the CI-vs-PI choice — one lease ⇒ PI. Have them notice the two 5-star outliers and ask “what could separate two 5-star stores?” (teaser: endcap, income → multiple regression). - Pitfalls: quoting the CI when forecasting a single new store; reading the intercept row instead of the slope row; calling a clean residual plot “bad” because of the two outliers (outliers ≠ a failed assumption); extrapolating beyond 5 stars. - Answer key (instructor): - Slope: \(b_1 = 757.51\), \(s_{b_1} = 51.46\), \(t = 14.72\), df = 43, \(p < 0.0001\) → reject \(H_0\). - 95% CI for slope = (653.7, 861.3) $K per star; \(F = 216.71 = 14.72^2\). - Residual plot: roughly horizontal band, no fan/curve → linearity + constant variance OK. - Standardized residuals: 2 stores |z| > 2 — store 26 (rating 5.0, std ≈ −2.79, under-performs) and store 12 (rating 5.0, std ≈ +2.21, over-performs). Not data errors. - Forecast at \(x^* = 4.5\): point \(\hat y = 3{,}742\) $K (≈ $3.74M); CI = (3,543, 3,940); PI = (2,786, 4,698). For one lease → quote the PI.



1–2 teams pitch the lease memo. Land: the slope is overwhelmingly real (\(t = 14.7\)); for one new store quote the PI, not the CI; the model assumes the new site behaves like the existing 45. Surface: significance ≠ causation; the two outliers + 17% unexplained ⇒ rating alone misses something → bridge to multiple regression. Walk the one-page engagement table.



Homework (team client): test the slope (report \(t\), \(p\), 95% CI), check the residual plot, and forecast one new case with the right interval — say whether you’d trust it. Preview: Multiple Regression — add population, income, endcap; learn “holding others constant” and why a bivariate winner can vanish.






autoparts.csv open in Excel; ToolPak (Data Analysis) enabled. Worked solutions in data/autoparts_regression.xlsx (Raw_Data, Regression_Output, Prediction, Residuals sheets).
For the live demo, run Regression with Residuals + Residual Plots checked so the residual plot appears in the same output the teams will produce.
Anchor figures: armands_fit.png, armands_reg_coef.png. Spine figures: autoparts_scatter.png, autoparts_fit.png, autoparts_residual_concept.png, autoparts_reg_stats.png, autoparts_reg_coef.png, autoparts_residual_plot.png, autoparts_std_residual_plot.png, autoparts_pred_band.png. Regenerate via _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic10_assets.R.





“R² = 0.83 means 83% of stores fit the line.” No — 83% of the variation in sales is explained.
“The intercept is sales for a 0-star store.” It only anchors the line; 0 is outside the data.
“Significant slope ⇒ better service causes more sales.” Association, not causation (reverse causation, lurking location effects).
“Use the CI to forecast a new store.” The CI is for the mean response; one new store needs the wider PI.
“Two outliers ⇒ the model is broken.” Off-trend points aren’t an assumption violation; they’re a prompt to ask what else (endcap, income) explains them.
“Extrapolate to a 6-star store.” The line is only validated over the observed range (1–5 stars).
