Quick checks: “Interpret the intercept” (anchors the line; no 0-star store — don’t over-read). “What’s the other 17%?” (everything else — location, merchandising → motivates next topic).




Teams run Regression on autoparts.csv (Y = sales2012_k, X = yelp_rating), write the equation, interpret the slope in dollars-per-star, and read \(R^2\). - Mentor as they work: make sure Y and X aren’t swapped (slope should be ~+757, not the inverse); push them to say the slope in dollars (“each star ≈ +$757K”), not “0.83” or a bare coefficient. Ask the over-eager team to state the unexplained share out loud. - Pitfalls: interpreting the intercept as “sales of a 0-star store”; reporting \(R^2\) as a percentage of stores instead of variation; confusing \(r\) and \(R^2\). - Answer key (instructor): - \(\hat y = 332.77 + 757.51\,x\) · slope = +757.51 ($K per Yelp star) · intercept = +332.77 ($K). - R Square = 0.8344 (Yelp explains ~83% of the store-to-store sales variation; ~17% unexplained). - Multiple R (= \(r\)) = 0.9135; Standard Error \(s\) = 463.7; Observations = 45. - SST = 55,840,034; SSR = 46,594,785; SSE = 9,245,249.



1–2 teams pitch. Land: a strong, usable predictor — ~$757K/star, \(R^2 = 0.834\) — good enough to score sites. Surface the pitfalls: “explains” = association, not cause; 17% unexplained means one variable is rarely the whole story. We have a fitted line but have not yet asked if it’s statistically real or whether assumptions hold → that’s Lecture 2.



Homework (team client): pick a numeric predictor of your outcome; fit the simple regression; write the slope in business units; report \(R^2\). Preview: is the slope real, do the assumptions hold, and how do we forecast a new case?

title: Lecture Guide — T10 · Simple Linear Regression
text:
Goal of the day: the team tests the slope, checks the residual plot, and forecasts a 4.5-star site with the correct interval — then makes the lease call.


Pose: “The fitted slope is +757.5. But 45 stores is a sample — is the relationship real, do the assumptions hold, and what should we forecast for a specific new site?” Distinguish describing the sample (L1) from inference to the population (L2).




Four assumptions on \(\varepsilon\): mean 0, constant variance (homoscedasticity), independence, normality. Flag up front; check with residuals later.
Standard error of the estimate \(s = \sqrt{\text{SSE}/(n-2)} \approx \$464K\) (typical vertical miss).
t-test on the slope: \(H_0:\beta_1 = 0\) vs. \(H_a:\beta_1 \ne 0\); \(t = b_1/s_{b_1}\), \(df = n-2\). Demo on Armand’s (\(t = 8.62\)) then spine. Read \(t\), \(p\), Lower/Upper 95% straight off the output.
CI for slope and the CI ⇔ test equivalence (zero outside CI ⇒ reject). Note \(F = t^2\) (same conclusion in simple regression; they diverge with >1 predictor next topic).
Significance ≠ causation: reverse causation, lurking variables; no extrapolation past the data.
Residual analysis: residual plot vs. \(x\) should be a horizontal band; curve → nonlinear (add \(x^2\)), fan → non-constant variance (transform \(y\)). Show autoparts_residual_plot.png. Standardized residuals; \(|z| > 2\) flags outliers (autoparts_std_residual_plot.png).
Prediction: point \(\hat y\) at \(x^* = 4.5\); CI (mean store, narrow) vs. PI (one new store, wide — the extra “1” under the root). Four width levers: confidence, \(s\), \(n\), distance of \(x^*\) from \(\bar x\). Show autoparts_pred_band.png.




Teams: (1) state \(t\), \(p\), and the 95% CI for the slope; (2) eyeball the residual plot for fan/curve; (3) forecast Yelp = 4.5 and decide CI vs. PI for one lease. - Mentor as they work: make sure they quote the slope’s \(t\)/CI (not the intercept’s); push the CI-vs-PI choice — one lease ⇒ PI. Have them notice the two 5-star outliers and ask “what could separate two 5-star stores?” (teaser: endcap, income → multiple regression). - Pitfalls: quoting the CI when forecasting a single new store; reading the intercept row instead of the slope row; calling a clean residual plot “bad” because of the two outliers (outliers ≠ a failed assumption); extrapolating beyond 5 stars. - Answer key (instructor): - Slope: \(b_1 = 757.51\), \(s_{b_1} = 51.46\), \(t = 14.72\), df = 43, \(p < 0.0001\) → reject \(H_0\). - 95% CI for slope = (653.7, 861.3) $K per star; \(F = 216.71 = 14.72^2\). - Residual plot: roughly horizontal band, no fan/curve → linearity + constant variance OK. - Standardized residuals: 2 stores |z| > 2 — store 26 (rating 5.0, std ≈ −2.79, under-performs) and store 12 (rating 5.0, std ≈ +2.21, over-performs). Not data errors. - Forecast at \(x^* = 4.5\): point \(\hat y = 3{,}742\) $K (≈ $3.74M); CI = (3,543, 3,940); PI = (2,786, 4,698). For one lease → quote the PI.



1–2 teams pitch the lease memo. Land: the slope is overwhelmingly real (\(t = 14.7\)); for one new store quote the PI, not the CI; the model assumes the new site behaves like the existing 45. Surface: significance ≠ causation; the two outliers + 17% unexplained ⇒ rating alone misses something → bridge to multiple regression. Walk the one-page engagement table.



Homework (team client): test the slope (report \(t\), \(p\), 95% CI), check the residual plot, and forecast one new case with the right interval — say whether you’d trust it. Preview: Multiple Regression — add population, income, endcap; learn “holding others constant” and why a bivariate winner can vanish.

title: Lecture Guide — T10 · Simple Linear Regression
text:
autoparts.csv open in Excel; ToolPak (Data Analysis) enabled. Worked solutions in data/autoparts_regression.xlsx (Raw_Data, Regression_Output, Prediction, Residuals sheets).
For the live demo, run Regression with Residuals + Residual Plots checked so the residual plot appears in the same output the teams will produce.
Anchor figures: armands_fit.png, armands_reg_coef.png. Spine figures: autoparts_scatter.png, autoparts_fit.png, autoparts_residual_concept.png, autoparts_reg_stats.png, autoparts_reg_coef.png, autoparts_residual_plot.png, autoparts_std_residual_plot.png, autoparts_pred_band.png. Regenerate via _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic10_assets.R.

title: Lecture Guide — T10 · Simple Linear Regression
text:
“R² = 0.83 means 83% of stores fit the line.” No — 83% of the variation in sales is explained.
“The intercept is sales for a 0-star store.” It only anchors the line; 0 is outside the data.
“Significant slope ⇒ better service causes more sales.” Association, not causation (reverse causation, lurking location effects).
“Use the CI to forecast a new store.” The CI is for the mean response; one new store needs the wider PI.
“Two outliers ⇒ the model is broken.” Off-trend points aren’t an assumption violation; they’re a prompt to ask what else (endcap, income) explains them.
“Extrapolate to a 6-star store.” The line is only validated over the observed range (1–5 stars).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
IMPORTANT
This document does not replace the official information in the course brightspace page



Course Description and Objectives
This course develops the quantitative and data-analysis skills managers need to make data-driven decisions. Coverage includes descriptive statistics, probability and probability distributions, decision analysis, sampling distributions and interval estimation, hypothesis testing, and applied regression analysis (simple and multiple). (Scope to be finalized during the course redesign.)
Our main goal is to instill the basic quantitative and data analysis skills needed by managers in modern business, where Business Analytics and Data Science have become essential. These skills will aid in data-driven decision-making, risk assessments, and policy formulation, and will enable managers to collaborate effectively with data analysis teams to enhance business performance.
Since modern data analysis is primarily performed using computers and various sophisticated software, this course will also emphasize software applications (such as Minitab, R, or Python). However, Microsoft Excel is the primary software tool demonstrated in this course.
Course Website: https://davi-moreira.github.io/2026F_business_analytics_qm670/


Instructor: Professor Davi Moreira

Email: dmoreira@purdue.edu
Virtual Office hours: Zoom link in your Course Brightspace Page
Individual Appointments: Book time with me through the link in the course syllabus on your Course Brightspace Page or by appointment.



Learning Outcomes
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

Understand the basic statistical principles and their applications in the area of management and business.

Understand fundamental issues in business and management.

Describe and use the commonly used statistical techniques for analyzing business data.

Be proficient in using Excel to carry out statistical and analytics methods covered in the course.



Prerequisite

Admission to the MS in Business Analytics & Information Management (MSBAIM), or an equivalent background in introductory statistics. (To be confirmed.)



Course Materials

Textbook (to be confirmed during the course redesign):
Anderson, Sweeney, Williams, Camm, and Cochran.
Statistics for Business and Economics, 14th Edition, Cengage Learning, Inc.
(Used in the previous edition.)
Handouts:
Lecture Slides and Supplementary Materials (available on Brightspace).
The E-Book and handouts are located in Brightspace → Content → Table of Contents → …
Computing:
Quizzes will be administered in-person, and you will need to work on practice exercises in class. You should have access to a PC, Mac, or tablet.
Software:
Microsoft Excel is used for in-class demonstrations. Ensure the “Data Analysis” ToolPak is enabled:

File -> Options -> Add-ins

Select Analysis ToolPak and Analysis ToolPak-VBA → Go

Data → Data Analysis (for Excel-based analyses)




Course Infra-structure
Brightspace: The Course Brightspace Page https://purdue.brightspace.com/ should be checked on a regular basis for announcements and course material.
Software: Microsoft Excel will be used for in-class demonstrations and instruction. The main tool is Data Analysis under Tools. If you don’t see this tool, follow the following steps to add in:

File -> Options -> Add-ins -> Select Analysis ToolPak and Analysis ToolPak-VBA (also select StatTools 7.5, if available) -> Go -> Data -> Data Analysis (to conduct analyses)

Course Website: This class website will be used throughout the course, but it does not replace the Course Brightspace Page.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Overview



Case Spotlight: the Vungle rollout decision
Structuring a decision: alternatives, states of nature, payoffs
Payoff tables and decision trees
Expected value (EV) — deciding under risk



Expected value of perfect information (EVPI)
Sample information, Bayes revision, and EVSI
Sensitivity: how robust is the call?
From inference to action: making the rollout recommendation

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Rewind: Before the A/B Test


Earlier in our work with Vungle — the mobile ad-tech startup that earns revenue when viewers install an advertised app — we tested whether their new ad-serving algorithm B beat the incumbent A.
The verdict: B’s daily eRPM (revenue per 1,000 impressions) ran about $0.11 higher than A’s, and a paired test said that lift was real (\(t = 3.33\)), worth roughly $26.5K/month at June’s traffic.
But statistics told us what is true and how sure we are. They did not tell management what to do.
Today we put on the manager’s hat: given that uncertainty, what is the right action?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Brief — Today’s Managerial Question


“Algorithm B looks better — but we’re not certain. Do we roll out B to all traffic now, keep A and play it safe, or run a bigger test before committing? Quantify it and make the call.”



Rolling out B and being wrong degrades all of Vungle’s revenue — not a 1/16 slice. Keeping A forgoes a real upside. Testing more costs time and money.
How today’s studio runs: I demo each tool on a textbook anchor → your team builds the Vungle rollout tree (one sprint) → we debrief the recommendation together.
By the end of class, your team makes the call — with the decision math to back it up.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
How Today’s Tools Answer It

Every tool today maps onto the Vungle rollout:







The managerial question
The tool




What are my options, and what could happen?
Payoff table (alternatives × states)


What’s the sequence of choice and chance?
Decision tree


Which option is best on average?
Expected value (EV)


What is it worth to remove the uncertainty?
EVPI / EVSI


Would the call change if I’m a bit off?
Sensitivity analysis





One decision, five tools — and they all build on the probability logic from earlier in the course.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Step Zero: Problem Formulation


Before any number, decision analysis forces three clean lists:

Decision alternatives — the strategies you control.
States of nature — the uncertain future events you do not control. They must be mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive.
Payoffs — the consequence (profit, cost, time…) of each alternative × state combination.

The discipline is in the structuring: most bad business decisions come from a missing alternative or an unstated state of nature, not from arithmetic.
The whole apparatus — payoff tables, trees, EV — is just bookkeeping on top of these three lists.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Vungle Decision, in Three Lists
text:
The Vungle Decision, in Three Lists

Decision alternatives (what Vungle controls):

\(d_1\): Roll out B now to all traffic
\(d_2\): Keep A (the safe baseline)
\(d_3\): Run a bigger test first, then decide

States of nature (the uncertainty management faces):

\(s_1\): B is truly better than A
\(s_2\): B is not better (no real lift, or worse)


These two states are mutually exclusive and exhaustive: exactly one is true, we just don’t know which.
Payoffs come next — and we’ll build them from the data we already have.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Where Do the Payoffs Come From? The Data
text:
Where Do the Payoffs Come From? The Data


We don’t invent payoffs — we anchor them in the eRPM evidence:

The mean lift was +$0.112 per 1,000 impressions; A served ≈ 236.5M impressions/month.
At that scale: \(\dfrac{236{,}459{,}402}{1{,}000} \times \$0.112 \approx \$26.5\text{K/month} \approx \$318\text{K/year}\).











If we roll out B and…
Annual payoff ($K)
Reasoning




\(s_1\): B is truly better
+318
full lift across all traffic


\(s_2\): B is not better
−160
degraded revenue vs. a working algorithm





Keep A is the baseline: $0 in both states (no gain, no loss).
(The −160 is management’s estimate of the downside of replacing a working algorithm with a worse one — about half the upside. We’ll stress-test it later.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Payoff Table

The payoff table is the whole problem on one page — rows are alternatives, columns are states of nature:




Decision alternative
\(s_1\): B truly better
\(s_2\): B not better




\(d_1\): Roll out B now
+$318K
−$160K


\(d_2\): Keep A
$0
$0






Read a cell as: “if I choose this row and that state occurs, this is what I get.”
A payoff table with no probabilities supports only crude rules (best-case, worst-case). To do better, we need the odds on the states — that is the next ingredient.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Decision Trees: Sequencing Choice and Chance
text:
Decision Trees: Sequencing Choice and Chance


Square node = a decision (you choose the branch). Round node = a chance event (nature chooses, with probabilities).
Branches from a square = alternatives; branches from a circle = states of nature. Payoffs sit at the tips.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Putting Odds on the States of Nature
text:
Putting Odds on the States of Nature


To choose, we need \(P(s_1)\) and \(P(s_2)\). Because exactly one state occurs, they must satisfy


\[
P(s_j) \geq 0 \quad\text{for all } j, \qquad \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(s_j) = 1
\]


Vungle’s data-science team, having seen the encouraging A/B signal, assigns a prior:

\(P(s_1) = 0.60\) (B truly better) and \(P(s_2) = 0.40\) (not better).

These can come from the classical, relative-frequency, or subjective method. Here they are an informed subjective judgment — and we will test how much they matter at the end.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Expected Value Approach


The expected value of a decision alternative is its probability-weighted average payoff:


\[
EV(d_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{N} P(s_j)\, V_{ij}
\]


where \(V_{ij}\) is the payoff for alternative \(d_i\) under state \(s_j\), and \(N\) is the number of states.
The decision rule: choose the alternative with the best EV (highest for profit, lowest for cost).
In Excel, one formula does it: =SUMPRODUCT(probabilities, payoff_row).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: Burger Prince

Burger Prince is choosing a restaurant layout. Demand could be 80, 100, or 120 customers/hour, with probabilities 0.4, 0.2, 0.4. Profit ($):










Model
\(s_1{=}80\) (0.4)
\(s_2{=}100\) (0.2)
\(s_3{=}120\) (0.4)
EV




A
10,000
15,000
14,000
$12,600


B
8,000
18,000
12,000
$11,600


C
6,000
16,000
21,000
$14,000 ✅



\[
EV(\text{C}) = 0.4(6{,}000) + 0.2(16{,}000) + 0.4(21{,}000) = \$14{,}000
\]
Decision: Model C has the highest EV → choose Model C. (We’ll reuse this example for EVPI and Bayes.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Vungle Decision Tree


The square is Vungle’s choice; the circle is nature deciding whether B is truly better. To pick a branch, we fold the tree back — replace each chance node by its EV.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Folding Back: EV of Each Vungle Alternative
text:
Folding Back: EV of Each Vungle Alternative

Using priors \(P(s_1)=0.60\), \(P(s_2)=0.40\):

\[
EV(\text{Roll out B}) = 0.60(318) + 0.40(-160) = 190.8 - 64.0 = \mathbf{\$126.8K}
\]


\[
EV(\text{Keep A}) = 0.60(0) + 0.40(0) = \mathbf{\$0}
\]



Decision rule: $126.8K > $0 → roll out B.
The expected payoff is positive because the upside (+318) is large and fairly likely (0.60), even though the downside (−160) is real.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Visualizing the Expected Values


EV ranks the alternatives, but it hides the risk: “roll out B” averages +$126.8K while still risking a −$160K year. EV is the right tool when this decision repeats; a one-shot, bet-the-company call would also weigh that downside.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A Quick Check on the Logic
text:
A Quick Check on the Logic


EV is a long-run average, not a guaranteed outcome. If Vungle faced this exact decision many times, “roll out B” would net +$126.8K per decision on average.
The actual result will be either +$318K or −$160K — never $126.8K. The expected value is a ranking device, not a forecast of what happens this once.
This is exactly why managers also ask: what would it be worth to know the true state before deciding? That question has a precise answer — EVPI.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)


Imagine a perfect oracle who tells you the true state before you decide. How much is that worth?
EVwPI (expected value with perfect information): for each state, take the best payoff you’d choose if you knew that state, then weight by the priors.
EVPI is the gain over deciding blind:


\[
\text{EVPI} = \lvert\, \text{EVwPI} - \text{EVwoPI} \,\rvert
\]


where EVwoPI is the best EV without information — what we just computed ($126.8K).
EVPI is an upper bound on the value of any study, survey, or test: no information can beat perfect information.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
EVPI — the Three Steps (Vungle)

Step 1 — best payoff in each state (look down each column of the payoff table):



State
Best choice if you knew
Best payoff




\(s_1\): B truly better (0.60)
Roll out B
+$318K


\(s_2\): B not better (0.40)
Keep A
$0



Step 2 — expected value of those best returns:
\[
\text{EVwPI} = 0.60(318) + 0.40(0) = \$190.8\text{K}
\]
Step 3 — subtract the best EV without information:
\[
\text{EVPI} = 190.8 - 126.8 = \mathbf{\$64K}
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
What EVPI Tells the Manager


$64K is the most Vungle should ever pay to remove all doubt about B before deciding. Any real test — always imperfect — is worth less than this ceiling.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
EVPI in Excel



Three labeled cells on top of the payoff table:

Best per state with =MAX(...) down each column
EVwPI with =SUMPRODUCT(priors, best-per-state)
EVPI = EVwPI − EVwoPI


EVPI is always ≥ 0: information can’t hurt you (you can ignore it).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Third Alternative: Run a Bigger Test
text:
The Third Alternative: Run a Bigger Test


Vungle’s real third option, \(d_3\), is to buy information — run a larger, longer A/B test before committing.
A test is imperfect (sample) information: it returns a signal, not the truth.

Favorable (F): the bigger test shows a significant lift.
Unfavorable (U): the test shows no significant lift.

The signal is reliable but not perfect. Suppose the test’s track record gives the conditional probabilities:


\[
P(F \mid s_1) = 0.80, \qquad P(F \mid s_2) = 0.25
\]


“When B really is better, the test flags it 80% of the time; when B is not better, it gives a false ‘favorable’ 25% of the time.”

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Prior, Conditional, Posterior


We have priors \(P(s_j)\) and conditionals \(P(\text{signal} \mid s_j)\). We want the posterior — the revised odds after seeing the signal:


\[
P(s_j \mid \text{signal}) = \frac{P(s_j)\,P(\text{signal}\mid s_j)}{\sum_k P(s_k)\,P(\text{signal}\mid s_k)}
\]


This is Bayes’ theorem — the same updating logic from earlier in the course, now feeding a decision tree.
A simple tabular layout does the arithmetic: prior → joint → marginal → posterior. The posteriors become the branch probabilities on the tree after the test.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Bayes Revision — Favorable Report


Joint = prior × conditional. Marginal \(P(F) = 0.48 + 0.10 = 0.58\). Posterior = joint ÷ marginal.
A favorable report lifts the odds B is truly better from 0.60 → 0.828.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Bayes Revision — Unfavorable Report


\(P(U) = 0.12 + 0.30 = 0.42\) (and \(0.58 + 0.42 = 1\) ✓). An unfavorable report drops the odds B is better from 0.60 → 0.286.
Same data, opposite signal → opposite revision. That is exactly what makes the test useful.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Signal Moves the Decision


After favorable: \(EV(\text{roll}) = 0.828(318) + 0.172(-160) = \$235.6K > 0\) → roll out B.
After unfavorable: \(EV(\text{roll}) = 0.286(318) + 0.714(-160) = -\$23.4K < 0\) → keep A.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)


The test is worth the extra expected profit it buys, before its cost:


\[
\text{EVSI} = \lvert\, \text{EVwSI} - \text{EVwoSI} \,\rvert
\]


EVwSI weights each signal’s best decision by how often that signal occurs:


\[
\text{EVwSI} = P(F)\,(235.6) + P(U)\,(0) = 0.58(235.6) + 0.42(0) = \$136.6\text{K}
\]


EVwoSI is the best EV with no test = $126.8K. So


\[
\text{EVSI} = 136.6 - 126.8 = \mathbf{\$9.84K}
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Is the Test Worth Buying?


Efficiency \(= \text{EVSI}/\text{EVPI} = 9.84/64 \approx 15\%\) — this imperfect test captures only 15% of perfect information’s value.
The test costs ≈ $20K to run, but is worth only $9.84K → net −$10K. Do not run the bigger test. (Compare: the textbook’s Burger Prince survey also failed this test — EVSI < its price.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
EVSI in Excel



Build it from the Bayes tables:

Best EV after each signal (using posteriors)
EVwSI = SUMPRODUCT(P(signal), best-EV)
EVSI = EVwSI − EVwoSI
Compare EVSI to the test cost


If EVSI < cost → skip the study and decide now.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Sensitivity Analysis


“Roll out B” wins as long as \(P(s_1)\) exceeds the indifference prior \(p^{*} \approx 0.33\). Our prior (0.60) clears it comfortably — the decision is robust.
Below one-third, keep A. The call only flips if we’re very pessimistic about B.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Finding the Tipping Point


The decision flips where the two EV lines cross — set \(EV(\text{roll}) = EV(\text{keep})\) and solve for the prior \(p = P(s_1)\):


\[
p(318) + (1-p)(-160) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; p^{*} = \frac{160}{318 + 160} \approx 0.335
\]


A robust decision is one that stays the same under small changes in the inputs. Here, B would have to be more likely wrong than right before we’d keep A.
This is the real payoff of structuring the decision: we now know not just what to do, but how sure we’d have to be to change our minds. In Excel: a What-If Data Table varying \(p\) traces the whole line.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint — Build the Rollout Tree, Make the Call
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint — Build the Rollout Tree, Make the Call

Decide: roll out B now, keep A, or run a bigger test? Recommend with the numbers.
Data: data/vungle_decision_analysis.xlsx (payoff table + priors + Bayes tables) · Time: ~20 min

Build the payoff table (roll out B: +318 / −160; keep A: 0/0) and compute EV of each with =SUMPRODUCT (priors 0.60 / 0.40).
Compute EVPI — the most a perfect oracle is worth.
Use the Bayes tables to get the posteriors, then EVSI; compare it to the $20K test cost.
Run a quick sensitivity check: at what prior \(p^{*}\) would the call flip?

Hand in (1 slide): your recommendation — the call · the EV · the one caveat (and: should Vungle buy the bigger test?).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Decision on One Page




Question
Tool
Answer




Best option on average?
EV
Roll out B ($126.8K vs. $0)


Worth knowing the truth for sure?
EVPI
$64K ceiling


Worth running a bigger test?
EVSI
$9.84K < $20K cost → no


Robust to our prior?
Sensitivity
flips only below \(p^{*} = 0.33\)





Recommendation to CEO Jaffer: roll out B now. Expected value is clearly positive (+$126.8K/yr), the decision holds unless we think B is more likely wrong than right, and a further test isn’t worth its price. Caveat: there is a real −$160K downside if B disappoints — so monitor eRPM closely post-rollout and be ready to revert.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: How Inference and Decision Analysis Fit Together
text:
How Inference and Decision Analysis Fit Together


This is the division of labor that runs through the whole course:

Inference (estimation, hypothesis tests): what is true, and how sure are we? → gave us the $0.11 lift, the CI, the \(p\)-value.
Decision analysis (today): given that uncertainty, what should we do? → turned those into alternatives, payoffs, and an EV-maximizing call.

The test result was an input to the decision, not the decision itself. The paired \(t\) said “the lift is real”; the EV said “and acting on it is worth +$126.8K.”
A good analyst can do both — and knows which question they’re answering.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Manager’s Takeaway


One sentence: Structuring the rollout as a decision — alternatives, states, payoffs — says roll out B, because its expected value ($126.8K/yr) beats keeping A, and a further test isn’t worth its cost.
One number to remember: EVPI = $64K — the ceiling on what any information is worth here; the actual test cleared only $9.84K of it.
One caveat: EV is a long-run average. “Roll out B” still risks a −$160K year — sensitivity shows the call is robust (flips only below \(p^{*} = 0.33\)), but the downside is real, so monitor and stay ready to revert.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Summary
Some key takeaways from this session:

Structure first: every decision under uncertainty reduces to alternatives × states of nature → payoffs; the payoff table and decision tree are just that on a page.
Decide by expected value: \(EV(d_i) = \sum_j P(s_j) V_{ij}\); pick the best EV — but remember it’s a long-run average that hides the downside.
Value information before buying it: EVPI is the ceiling (perfect info); EVSI is what a real, imperfect test is worth — buy a study only if its value beats its cost.
Bayes turns a signal into revised odds: prior → joint → marginal → posterior; the posteriors become the branch probabilities after the test.
Sensitivity tells you how robust the call is: find the prior \(p^{*}\) where the decision flips; ours holds comfortably.
Inference and decision analysis are partners: one says what’s true and how sure; the other says what to do about it.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward

Your client (homework): frame one real decision in your team’s case as a decision problem — list the alternatives, the states of nature, and a payoff table. Assign priors, compute EV, and find EVPI. State the recommendation and the tipping-point prior.
Next time: we return to the inference arc — modeling uncertain counts (how many installs tomorrow?) with discrete probability distributions, the raw material that priors and payoffs are built from.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Overview



New Case Spotlight: an auto-parts retailer picking store sites
From comparing groups to predicting an outcome
The simple linear regression model
Least squares: the best-fit line
Interpreting the slope and intercept
\(R^2\): how much does the line explain?



Is the relationship real? The \(t\)-test on the slope
Confidence interval for the slope
Residual analysis: checking the assumptions
Predicting sales for a brand-new store
Confidence vs. prediction intervals
From a fitted line to a site recommendation

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A New Client — Where Should the Next Store Go?
text:
A New Client — Where Should the Next Store Go?


We close the Vungle engagement and open a new one. Our client is a regional auto-parts retailer with 45 stores and capital to open more.
The real-estate team scores prospective sites on demographics — population, income — and on the store’s Yelp rating (a proxy for service quality once a store is open).
The question on the table: which sites will sell? The CFO wants every new lease backed by a sales forecast, not a hunch.
We have one dataset, data/autoparts.csv: for each of the 45 existing stores, its 2012 annual sales ($K), trade-area population and income, an end-cap placement flag, and its Yelp rating.
Until now we compared two groups. Now we predict a number — and that is what regression does.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Brief — Today’s Managerial Question


“Does a store’s Yelp rating predict its annual sales? If so, by how much — and how confident are we when we forecast a brand-new store?”



Lecture 1 builds and reads the model: fit sales ~ yelp_rating, interpret the slope (dollars per star), and measure fit with \(R^2\).
Lecture 2 asks whether the relationship is real (a \(t\)-test on the slope), checks the model’s assumptions, and turns the line into a site-selection forecast.
How the studio runs: I demo each step on a textbook anchor (Armand’s Pizza) and on the auto-parts data → your team fits the model and makes the site call.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
From Comparing to Predicting








What we did (Topics 7–9)
What we do now




Estimate / test one number (a mean, a proportion)
Model how one variable moves with another


“Is B’s eRPM above $3.30?”
“How many $ of sales per Yelp star?”


Output: a decision (reject / fail to reject)
Output: an equation you can forecast with





Regression develops an equation showing how a dependent variable \(y\) (what we predict — sales) relates to an independent variable \(x\) (the predictor — Yelp rating).
Simple linear regression uses one predictor; the relationship is approximated by a straight line. (More predictors → multiple regression, next topic.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Brief — Lecture 1


“Fit a straight line to sales ~ yelp_rating. What does the slope mean in dollars, and how much of the store-to-store sales gap does Yelp rating explain?”



By the end of Lecture 1 your team will have a fitted equation, a dollars-per-star interpretation, and an \(R^2\) — the raw material for a site score.
The decision it feeds: rank prospective sites by predicted sales.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Simple Linear Regression Model


The model describes how \(y\) relates to \(x\) and a random error term \(\varepsilon\) in the whole population of stores:


\[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon
\]


\(\beta_0\) (intercept) and \(\beta_1\) (slope) are unknown population parameters; \(\varepsilon\) captures everything about sales that Yelp rating does not explain.
We never see \(\beta_0, \beta_1\) — we estimate them from our 45-store sample. The estimated equation is


\[
\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x
\]


\(\hat{y}\) is the predicted mean sales at a given rating; \(b_0, b_1\) are the sample estimates of \(\beta_0, \beta_1\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: What a Line Can Look Like
text:
What a Line Can Look Like


The sign of \(\beta_1\) sets the story:

\(\beta_1 > 0\) — positive relationship: higher \(x\) goes with higher \(y\) (we expect this for Yelp → sales).
\(\beta_1 < 0\) — negative relationship: higher \(x\) goes with lower \(y\).
\(\beta_1 = 0\) — no linear relationship: \(x\) tells you nothing about \(y\).

Honest caveat — regression is not causation. A line that fits well says rating and sales move together; it does not prove better service causes sales. We will return to this when we test significance.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Data: Just Look First


45 stores. Higher-rated stores clearly tend to sell more — but the cloud has spread. We need a line to summarize the trend and a number to summarize how tight it is.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Least Squares: Define “Best Fit”


Infinitely many lines pass through a cloud of points. Which one is best? The one that makes the residuals smallest.
A residual is the vertical gap between an actual store and the line:


\[
e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i
\]


Least squares criterion: choose \(b_0, b_1\) to minimize the sum of squared residuals:


\[
\min \;\; \text{SSE} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
\]


Squaring penalizes big misses and keeps positives and negatives from cancelling.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Least-Squares Formulas



Slope — the workhorse:


\[
b_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}
\]


Intercept — forces the line through the point of averages \((\bar{x}, \bar{y})\):


\[
b_0 = \bar{y} - b_1 \bar{x}
\]


In Excel you rarely type these: =SLOPE(y, x), =INTERCEPT(y, x), or the full Regression tool do the arithmetic.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
What the Criterion Is Minimizing


Each grey segment is one residual \(e_i\). Least squares is the unique line that makes the sum of those squared lengths as small as possible.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: Armand’s Pizza Parlors

A classic: Armand’s Pizza has 10 restaurants near college campuses. Sales clearly depend on how many students live nearby. \(x\) = student population in the trade area (1,000s); \(y\) = quarterly sales ($1,000s).




Student population \(x\) (000s)
Quarterly sales \(y\) ($000s)




Restaurant 1
2
58


Restaurant 2
6
105


\(\vdots\)
\(\vdots\)
\(\vdots\)


Restaurant 10
26
202



Least-squares fit: \(\bar{x} = 14\), \(\bar{y} = 130\), and the slope/intercept formulas give
\[
b_1 = 5, \qquad b_0 = 60 \qquad\Rightarrow\qquad \hat{y} = 60 + 5x
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Armand’s — Reading the Line


Slope \(b_1 = 5\): each additional 1,000 students in the trade area is associated with $5,000 more quarterly sales.
Intercept \(b_0 = 60\): the line’s height at \(x = 0\) — outside the data range, so read it as a fitting constant, not “sales with zero students.”

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Auto-Parts Fit


\[
\widehat{\text{sales}} = 332.8 + 757.5 \times (\text{Yelp rating})
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Interpreting the Auto-Parts Slope and Intercept
text:
Interpreting the Auto-Parts Slope and Intercept


Slope \(b_1 = +757.5\): each additional Yelp star is associated with about $757.5K more in annual sales. This is the number the real-estate team can use to score sites.
Intercept \(b_0 = +332.8\): the line’s height at a rating of zero. No store has a zero rating, so do not interpret it as “sales of a zero-star store” — it just anchors the line.
Manager’s translation: Moving a store from a 3.0 to a 4.0 Yelp rating is worth roughly three-quarters of a million dollars in predicted annual sales.
Sanity check: the slope is positive and large — consistent with the upward cloud. Good.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: \(R^2\): How Much Does the Line Explain?
text:
\(R^2\): How Much Does the Line Explain?


Total variation in sales splits into what the line explains and what it leaves as error:


\[
\underbrace{\text{SST}}_{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{SSR}}_{\sum (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{SSE}}_{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}
\]


The coefficient of determination is the explained share:


\[
R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}}, \qquad 0 \le R^2 \le 1
\]


\(R^2 = 0\): the line explains nothing. \(R^2 = 1\): every point sits exactly on the line.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
\(R^2\) for the Auto-Parts Model


From the auto-parts fit:





Quantity
Value




SST (total variation)
55,840,034


SSR (explained by line)
46,594,785


SSE (left as error)
9,245,249


\(R^2 = \text{SSR}/\text{SST}\)
0.834





Interpretation: Yelp rating alone explains 83.4% of the store-to-store variation in annual sales — strong for a single predictor.
The remaining 16.6% is everything else (location, merchandising, competition). That gap is exactly what motivates adding more predictors next topic.
In Excel: =RSQ(sales, yelp) or read R Square off the Regression output.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: \(R^2\) and Correlation Are the Same Story
text:
\(R^2\) and Correlation Are the Same Story


The sample correlation \(r\) and \(R^2\) are tightly linked in simple regression:


\[
r = (\text{sign of } b_1)\sqrt{R^2}
\]


Here \(b_1 > 0\) and \(R^2 = 0.834\), so \(r = +\sqrt{0.834} = +0.913\) — a strong positive correlation, the same fact \(R^2\) reports.
Correlation measures the tightness and direction of the linear association; \(R^2\) measures the share of variation explained. Same coin, two faces.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Excel: One Click Gives You Everything
text:
Excel: One Click Gives You Everything



Data → Data Analysis → Regression

Input Y Range: sales2012_k
Input X Range: yelp_rating
Check Labels, Residuals, Residual Plots


The top block is Regression Statistics — Multiple R, R Square, Standard Error, Observations.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — Fit the line, read the slope
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — Fit the line, read the slope

Decide: does Yelp rating predict store sales — and what is one star worth?
Data: data/autoparts.csv · Time: ~12 min

In Excel: Data → Data Analysis → Regression (Y = sales2012_k, X = yelp_rating, check Labels).
Write the estimated equation \(\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x\) and interpret the slope in plain dollars-per-star.
Read R Square: what fraction of the sales gap does Yelp rating explain? State the unexplained share.

Hand in (1 slide): the equation, the dollars-per-star sentence, and your \(R^2\) read — would you use Yelp rating to score sites?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Debrief — Manager’s Takeaway (Lecture 1)


One sentence: Yelp rating is a strong predictor of store sales — each star is worth about $757K in predicted annual sales.
One number: \(R^2 = 0.834\) — Yelp rating alone explains 83% of the sales differences across stores.
One caveat: “explains” is association, not cause, and 17% is still unexplained — one variable is rarely the whole story. We have a fitted line, but we have not yet asked whether it is statistically real or whether the model’s assumptions hold. That is Lecture 2.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward (Lecture 1)

Your client (homework): in your team’s case, pick a plausible numeric predictor of your key outcome. Fit the simple regression, write the slope interpretation in business units, and report \(R^2\).
Next time: is the slope real or could it be noise? We test it, check the assumptions behind the line, and forecast a brand-new store.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Brief — Lecture 2


“The fitted slope is +757.5 $K per star. But our 45 stores are a sample. Is the Yelp–sales relationship real, do the model’s assumptions hold, and what sales should we forecast for a specific new site?”



Lecture 1 described the sample. Lecture 2 does inference: from these 45 stores to the population of all possible stores.
Three jobs: (1) test the slope, (2) validate the assumptions with residuals, (3) predict a new store with an honest margin of error.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Assumptions Behind the Line


Everything we are about to do (tests, intervals) rests on four assumptions about the error term \(\varepsilon\):

Mean zero: \(E(\varepsilon) = 0\) — the line is right on average.
Constant variance: \(\text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2\) is the same for all \(x\) (homoscedasticity).
Independence: the errors for different stores are independent.
Normality: \(\varepsilon\) is normally distributed.

If these are badly violated, the \(p\)-values and intervals can mislead. We will check them with residual plots after we test — but they are the foundation, so flag them first.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Estimating \(\sigma\): the Standard Error of the Estimate
text:
Estimating \(\sigma\): the Standard Error of the Estimate


The tests need an estimate of the error spread \(\sigma\). The mean square error estimates \(\sigma^2\):


\[
s^2 = \text{MSE} = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n - 2}
\]


Its square root, \(s = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}\), is the standard error of the estimate — the typical vertical miss of the line, in the units of \(y\):


\[
s = \sqrt{\frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{9{,}245{,}249}{43}} \approx \$464\text{K}
\]


We divide by \(n - 2\) because we spent two degrees of freedom estimating \(b_0\) and \(b_1\). In Excel: =STEYX(sales, yelp) or read Standard Error off the output.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Testing the Slope: Is the Relationship Real?
text:
Testing the Slope: Is the Relationship Real?


If \(\beta_1 = 0\), \(x\) has no linear effect on \(y\) — there is no relationship to forecast with. So we test:


\[
H_0: \beta_1 = 0 \qquad \text{vs.} \qquad H_a: \beta_1 \neq 0 \qquad (\alpha = 0.05)
\]


The test statistic divides the estimated slope by its standard error, with \(df = n - 2\):


\[
t = \frac{b_1 - 0}{s_{b_1}}, \qquad s_{b_1} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}
\]


Same logic as every test since Topic 8: a statistic, a \(t\) distribution, a \(p\)-value, a decision.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor: Armand’s Slope Is Significant



For Armand’s Pizza:
\[
t = \frac{5}{0.580} = 8.62, \quad df = 8
\]
\(p\text{-value} \approx 0.000 \le 0.05\) → reject \(H_0\).

Student population is a significant predictor of restaurant sales — the slope is not zero.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Auto-Parts Slope Test


From the Regression output (yelp_rating row):





Quantity
Value




\(b_1\) (slope)
757.5


\(s_{b_1}\) (std. error of slope)
51.46


\(t = b_1 / s_{b_1}\)
14.7


\(df = n - 2\)
43


\(p\)-value
\(< 0.0001\)





\(|t| = 14.7\) is enormous against \(t_{0.025, 43} = 2.02\) → reject \(H_0\) decisively.
Translation: the Yelp–sales relationship is not a fluke of these 45 stores. There is overwhelming evidence the true slope is positive.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Confidence Interval for the Slope


The test says the slope is nonzero; the confidence interval says how big it plausibly is:


\[
b_1 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s_{b_1} = 757.5 \pm 2.02 \times 51.46 = (\$653.7\text{K},\; \$861.3\text{K})
\]


We are 95% confident each Yelp star is worth between $654K and $861K in annual sales.
The interval is entirely above zero — the same conclusion as the test (reject \(H_0\)), now with a magnitude a manager can plan around.
Equivalence: reject \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) at \(\alpha = 0.05\) \(\iff\) zero is not in the 95% CI. Read directly off the Lower 95% / Upper 95% columns of the output.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A Note on the F Test
text:
A Note on the F Test


The Regression output also reports an F test (the ANOVA block): \(F = \text{MSR}/\text{MSE}\), testing the same \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\).
In simple regression (one predictor) the \(F\) and \(t\) tests are equivalent — in fact \(F = t^2\):


\[
F = 216.7 = (14.7)^2 = t^2
\]


Same conclusion, same \(p\)-value. (When we add predictors next topic, \(F\) tests the model as a whole while each \(t\) tests one variable — then they differ.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Significance Is Not Causation


Rejecting \(H_0: \beta_1 = 0\) tells us the relationship is statistically significant — Yelp and sales move together more than chance would explain.
It does not establish that better service causes higher sales. Reverse causation (busy, well-stocked stores earn better reviews) and lurking variables (a great location drives both) are live alternatives.
It also does not prove the relationship is truly linear outside the range we observed — extrapolating a 6-star store is meaningless.
Use regression to forecast and to quantify; use judgment and design to claim causation.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Residual Analysis: Validating the Assumptions


A significant slope is worthless if the model is the wrong shape. Residuals are our window onto the error term — they should look like random noise.
The core diagnostic: plot residuals against \(x\) (or against \(\hat{y}\)). We want a horizontal band of points centered at zero with no pattern.
Two warning signs to learn:

A curved (U-shaped) band → the relationship is nonlinear; a straight line is the wrong functional form (consider adding \(x^2\)).
A fan that widens → non-constant variance (heteroscedasticity); the constant-variance assumption fails (consider transforming \(y\)).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Auto-Parts Residual Plot


Roughly horizontal, centered at zero, no fan and no curve → the linearity and constant-variance assumptions look reasonable. The straight-line model is defensible.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Standardized Residuals and Outliers

Dividing each residual by its standard deviation gives a standardized residual — on a \(z\)-like scale.
Rule of thumb: a standardized residual with \(|z| > 2\) flags an outlier — a store that performs far off-trend for its rating. Investigate; don’t auto-delete.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Two Stores Worth a Conversation


Two stores sit just past \(\pm 2\) — both rated 5.0 stars:

One under-performs badly (sales far below the $4.1M its rating predicts).
One over-performs (sales well above the line).

These are not data errors — they are real stores the model can’t fully explain with rating alone.
What might separate two 5-star stores? End-cap placement, local income, population. That is the teaser for multiple regression — adding the variables that explain the residuals.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Forecasting a New Store: Point Prediction
text:
Forecasting a New Store: Point Prediction


The real payoff: a prospective site rates 4.5 stars on comparable stores. Predicted sales:


\[
\hat{y} = 332.8 + 757.5 \times 4.5 = \$3{,}742\text{K} \;\approx\; \$3.74\text{M}
\]


That single number is the point prediction — but a point with no margin of error is a trap. Two different questions need two different intervals:

Confidence interval — the mean sales of all stores rated 4.5.
Prediction interval — the sales of this one new store.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Confidence Interval vs. Prediction Interval



Confidence interval for the mean response at \(x^*\) — narrower:


\[
\hat{y} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x^* - \bar{x})^2}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}
\]


Prediction interval for one new store at \(x^*\) — wider (note the extra 1):


\[
\hat{y} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-2}\; s\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x^* - \bar{x})^2}{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}
\]


The PI carries the individual store’s own scatter (\(s\)) on top of the uncertainty in the line — that is the extra “1.”

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Two Intervals, in a Picture
text:
The Two Intervals, in a Picture


At Yelp = 4.5: point $3.74M; CI \((\$3{,}543\text{K},\; \$3{,}940\text{K})\) for the average such store; PI \((\$2{,}786\text{K},\; \$4{,}698\text{K})\) for one specific new store.
Both bands are narrowest near \(\bar{x}\) and flare out at the edges — forecasts far from the data are less certain.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: What Makes a Forecast Tighter or Looser
text:
What Makes a Forecast Tighter or Looser


Four levers set the width of these intervals:

Confidence level \((1-\alpha)\) — more confidence → wider.
Data spread \(s\) — noisier data → wider.
Sample size \(n\) — more stores → narrower.
Distance of \(x^*\) from \(\bar{x}\) — forecasting far from the average rating → wider.

Manager’s lesson: a confident forecast comes from a tight model (\(s\) small), lots of data (\(n\) large), and a site that looks like the stores we already have (\(x^*\) near \(\bar{x}\)). Forecasting an exotic site is a guess dressed as a number.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Test the slope, forecast a site
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Test the slope, forecast a site

Decide: is the Yelp–sales relationship real, and what should we forecast for a candidate 4.5-star site?
Data: data/autoparts.csv · Time: ~15 min

From the Regression output, state \(t\), the \(p\)-value, and the 95% CI for the slope — is \(\beta_1\) significantly nonzero?
Glance at the residual plot (check Residual Plots in the dialog): any fan or curve that breaks the assumptions?
Forecast a site at Yelp = 4.5: give the point prediction, and say whether the CFO should quote the CI or the PI for one new lease — and why.

Hand in (1 slide): the slope verdict (with CI), an assumptions one-liner, and your forecast + the interval you’d put in the lease memo.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Debrief — Manager’s Takeaway (Lecture 2)


One sentence: the Yelp–sales slope is overwhelmingly significant (\(t = 14.7\), \(p < 0.0001\)); a 4.5-star site is forecast at $3.74M, and for one lease we quote the prediction interval \((\$2.79\text{M}, \$4.70\text{M})\).
One number: 95% CI for the slope = ($654K, $861K) per star — the magnitude the real-estate team plans around.
One caveat: the model assumes the new site behaves like our 45 stores; the two off-trend 5-star outliers warn that rating alone misses something — which is exactly why we add predictors next.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward (Lecture 2)

Your client (homework): on your team’s simple regression — test the slope (report \(t\), \(p\), and the 95% CI), check the residual plot for assumption violations, and produce a point prediction with the right interval for one new case. State whether you’d trust the forecast.
Next time: sales depend on more than one thing. We add population, income, and end-cap placement to the model — Multiple Regression — and learn why a variable that looks important alone can vanish once others are in the room.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Whole Engagement on One Page
text:
The Whole Engagement on One Page










Step
Question
Tool
Auto-parts result




Fit
What’s the line?
Least squares
\(\hat{y} = 332.8 + 757.5\,x\)


Interpret
What’s a star worth?
Slope \(b_1\)
+$757.5K per Yelp star


Explain
How good is the fit?
\(R^2\)
0.834 — 83% of sales variation

Is it real?
\(t\) test on slope
\(t = 14.7\), \(p < .0001\) → reject \(H_0\)


Quantify
How big, plausibly?

($654K, $861K) per star

Horizontal band → OK (2 outliers)
