Yes





The model is highly significant overall (\(F = 136.7\)) — yet two of its four predictors are individually insignificant.
The lesson: a significant \(F\) does not mean every variable matters. Here all the explanatory power comes from service (Yelp) and merchandising (endcap); the location variables are statistical passengers.
Significance ≠ importance, and importance ≠ significance. Judge each on its own evidence.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Confirming with a Side-by-Side Comparison



Build models step by step and watch adjusted \(R^2\):

Population + Income alone: the demographics explain almost nothing.
Add Yelp: adjusted \(R^2\) leaps.
Add Endcap: it climbs again.


The location variables a manager would buy first are the ones that move the model least.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A Switch, Not a Dial: Encoding “Has an Endcap”
text:
A Switch, Not a Dial: Encoding “Has an Endcap”


endcap isn’t a number you can have more or less of — it is a category: a store either has premium endcap placement or it doesn’t.
We encode it as a dummy (indicator) variable: \(1\) = has an endcap, \(0\) = none. The \(0\) category is the reference (baseline) level.
Excel treats the 0/1 column like any other predictor — no special tool. Its coefficient has a special meaning:

\(b_{\text{endcap}}\) = the average difference in sales between an endcap store and a non-endcap store, holding the other predictors constant.

In other words, the dummy coefficient is the dollar value of an endcap — exactly what the CFO asked for.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: What the Dummy Does to the Equation
text:
What the Dummy Does to the Equation


Split the fitted equation by the two values of the dummy (all else held fixed):


\[
\text{No endcap } (x=0): \quad \widehat{\text{sales}} = (b_0 + \cdots) + 642.3\,\text{yelp}
\]
\[
\text{Endcap } (x=1): \quad \widehat{\text{sales}} = (b_0 + 816.5 + \cdots) + 642.3\,\text{yelp}
\]


Same slope on every other variable — only the intercept shifts up by $816.5K.
A dummy gives the two groups parallel lines: identical response to Yelp, location, income — but a fixed level gap between them.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Endcap Gap, Drawn


Two parallel lines, separated by a constant $816K — the endcap premium, holding population, income, and Yelp fixed.
\(t = 6.98\), p < 0.0001 → this gap is not noise. An endcap is a real, sizeable lever.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Putting a Defensible Number on the Decision
text:
Putting a Defensible Number on the Decision


Scenario: the chain plans a new store with population_3mi = 120,000, income_3mi = 55,000, yelp_rating = 4.0. Should it pay for endcap placement?
Plug into the fitted equation, once with endcap = 0 and once with endcap = 1:





Configuration
Predicted annual sales




Without endcap
$2,816K


With endcap
$3,632K


Lift from the endcap
+$816K / year





The decision rule: negotiate for the endcap if its annual cost is below ~$816K. If the placement leases for, say, $150K/year, that is a clear yes. The dummy turned a vague “merchandising matters” into a number you can put in a budget.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A Note on Categories with More Than Two Levels
text:
A Note on Categories with More Than Two Levels


endcap had two levels → one dummy. The rule generalizes:

A categorical variable with \(k\) levels needs \(\mathbf{k - 1}\) dummy variables (one level is the reference).

Example — three store formats (Standard, Express, Flagship): use two dummies (Express 0/1, Flagship 0/1); “Standard” is the reference. Each dummy coefficient is that format’s sales gap versus Standard.
Do not encode categories as a single 1/2/3 number — that would falsely force the levels to be evenly spaced and ordered. Always use separate 0/1 columns.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Why We Check It


Multicollinearity = two or more predictors are strongly correlated with each other.
When predictors overlap, the model struggles to credit the effect to one versus another. Symptoms:

coefficient standard errors inflate → wide CIs, small \(t\)’s;
coefficients become unstable and can flip sign;
an important predictor can look insignificant — not because it’s useless, but because a correlated partner is absorbing its credit.

It rarely hurts prediction, but it muddies interpretation — and interpretation is exactly what the CFO is buying.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Detecting It: Correlations and VIF




Quick look: the predictor correlation matrix (Data Analysis → Correlation). Flag any pair with \(|r| > 0.7\).
Formal measure: the Variance Inflation Factor,

\[
VIF_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2}
\]
where \(R_j^2\) is from regressing \(x_j\) on all the other predictors. Rule of thumb: investigate when VIF > 5.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Our Model Is Clean — So the Verdict Stands
text:
Our Model Is Clean — So the Verdict Stands


For the auto-parts model every pairwise correlation is small (largest: income–endcap at \(-0.47\)) and every VIF is below 1.7 — far under the threshold of 5.





Predictor
VIF




Population
1.17


Income
1.49


Yelp rating
1.29


Endcap
1.62





So population and income are insignificant for an honest reason — they genuinely add little once service and merchandising are in the model. This is not a multicollinearity artifact; the verdict is trustworthy.
Contrast: had population and income been highly correlated, we’d have to be cautious — their small \(t\)’s could have been an overlap symptom rather than true irrelevance.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Test the drivers & make the call
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Test the drivers & make the call

Decide: which drivers are real, what is an endcap worth, and where should the CFO invest?
Data: data/autoparts.csv · Time: ~15 min

From your regression output, read Significance F (overall test) — is the model useful at all?
In the Coefficients table, mark each predictor significant (\(|t| > 2\), p < 0.05) or not — sort the four drivers into “real” vs. “can’t tell.”
Price the endcap: predict sales for a planned store (pop = 120,000, income = 55,000, yelp = 4.0) with endcap = 0 and with endcap = 1; the difference is the endcap’s dollar value.
State the investment call in three sentences: the lever to fund · the number · the caveat.

Hand in (1 slide): your significant-vs-not verdict, the endcap’s dollar value, and the CFO recommendation.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Debrief — Manager’s Takeaway (Lecture 2)


One sentence: the overall model is decisive (\(F = 136.7\)), but only service (Yelp) and merchandising (endcap) survive their individual \(t\)-tests — so the budget goes there, not into bigger-market real estate.
One number: $816K — an endcap’s value, straight off the dummy coefficient; defensible against any lease under that figure.
One caveat: every coefficient is read “holding the other three constant” and the model is descriptive — pilot the endcap, then re-estimate before betting the chain on it.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Whole Picture on One Page
text:
The Whole Picture on One Page











Driver
Partial effect
\(t\)
Verdict
Investment read




Population (3 mi)
+$0.35K / 1,000 residents
0.88
not significant
bigger market ≠ more sales here


Income (3 mi)
+$4.7K / $1,000 income
1.50
not significant
richer market ≠ more sales here


Yelp rating
+$642K / star
16.5
significant
invest in service / experience


Endcap
+$816K
6.98
significant
buy the endcap placement



Model: \(R^2 = 0.932\), adjusted \(R^2 = 0.925\), overall \(F = 136.7\) (p \(< 10^{-22}\)), \(s = \$308.5\)K, all VIF \(< 1.7\).


Recommendation to the CFO: stop chasing big, rich markets — location demographics don’t move sales in this chain. The money belongs in service quality (every Yelp star ≈ +$642K/yr) and in endcap merchandising (≈ +$816K/yr, well worth a sub-$816K lease). Both effects are statistically rock-solid and not a multicollinearity mirage.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
\(F\) vs. \(t\) — the Lesson That Travels


The \(F\)-test and the \(t\)-tests answer different questions, and you need both:

\(F\) said the model as a whole is useful — but that alone could have tempted us to keep all four variables.
The \(t\)-tests revealed that half the predictors were dead weight, steering the budget toward the two that actually pay.

Significance is conditional. Every coefficient and every \(t\) here means “…holding the other three constant.” Change the set of predictors and the story can change.
Significance ≠ importance. With huge samples trivial effects turn “significant”; with small samples real effects can hide. Always pair the test with the dollar size of the effect — that is the number a manager decides on.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Manager’s Takeaway


One sentence: for this 45-store chain, service and merchandising — not location demographics — drive sales, so invest in Yelp rating (+$642K/star) and endcap placement (+$816K), each statistically decisive.
One number to remember: $816K — the value of an endcap, read straight off a dummy variable’s coefficient, holding everything else constant.
One caveat: the model describes 45 existing stores; it does not prove that buying an endcap will cause +$816K at a brand-new store. Correlation control is the best we can do with observational data — pilot it, then re-estimate.
Practice with the real data: data/autoparts.csv + Excel Regression → reproduce \(R^2 = 0.932\), \(F = 136.7\), the four coefficients, and the prediction. Worked solutions: data/autoparts_regression.xlsx.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Summary
Some key takeaways from this topic:

Multiple regression estimates the effect of each predictor holding the others constant — observational data’s closest move to a controlled comparison.
A partial coefficient can differ wildly from its simple-regression cousin: population and income looked plausible but added nothing once Yelp and endcap were in.
\(R^2\) only ever rises when you add predictors → judge model size with adjusted \(R^2\), which charges a fee per predictor.
\(F\) first, then \(t\): \(F\) asks “is the model useful at all?”; the \(t\)-tests ask “which predictors carry their weight?” A significant \(F\) does not bless every coefficient.
A dummy variable (0/1) prices a category — the endcap coefficient is the dollar value of an endcap; \(k\) levels need \(k-1\) dummies.
Multicollinearity (overlapping predictors; check VIF > 5) muddies interpretation; here every VIF < 1.7, so the verdict holds.
Regression output is an input to a decision: translate coefficients into dollars and weigh them against costs.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward

Your client (homework, part 2): for your team’s case, run the overall \(F\)-test and the individual \(t\)-tests; if your case has a natural category, add a dummy and interpret its coefficient as a group difference. Check VIF for any predictors you suspect overlap. Close with a one-paragraph recommendation: which lever should the client pull, and what is it worth?
Where we’ve been: from comparing two groups (inference) to predicting with one driver (simple regression) to isolating many drivers at once (today) — the analyst’s core toolkit for turning data into a defensible business decision.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Overview



Case Spotlight: A/B Testing at Vungle
Lecture 1 — sampling, point estimation
The sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) and \(\bar{p}\)
The Central Limit Theorem & standard error
How sure can 30 days make us?



Lecture 2 — the interval estimate
Confidence intervals for a mean (\(t\)) and a proportion
The margin of error — and the three levers that set it
How many days for \(\pm\$0.05\)? — sample size
The estimation memo for Vungle

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Brief — 30 Days Is a Sample
text:
The Brief — 30 Days Is a Sample


“We ran algorithm B for 30 days and saw a mean eRPM of about $3.46. But June is over. What is B’s true mean eRPM going forward — and how close can 30 days really get us?”



Until now we have described the 30 days we have. Today we generalize to the days we don’t have — every future impression B will ever serve.
The 30-day mean $3.46 is a single point estimate. By itself it hides the most important question a manager can ask: how much could it be off?
Today’s job: turn “$3.46” into “$3.46, give or take a known margin of error” — and decide whether 30 days is even enough.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
How Today’s Studio Runs


This is a two-lecture topic. Each lecture runs the same loop: I demo → your team sprints on the Vungle data → we debrief the call.










Lecture
The question
The tool




1
Why does a sample mean wobble — and by how much?
Sampling distribution, standard error, the CLT


2
What is B’s true mean eRPM, \(\pm\) how much? How many days do we need?
Confidence intervals, margin of error, sample size





By the end, your team writes Vungle’s estimation memo: the true mean eRPM and conversion rate, each with a 95% interval, plus the days needed for a tighter test.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Brief — Why a Sample Mean Wobbles
text:
The Brief — Why a Sample Mean Wobbles


A different 30 days — say, July — would give a different mean eRPM. So would any other random sample.
That wobble is not a mistake; it is the price of working from a sample instead of the whole population.
Lecture 1’s question: if the sample mean changes every time we sample, how can we trust any one of them? The answer is the sampling distribution — and it is surprisingly well-behaved.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Population, Sample, Inference


Population: every future impression B will serve — its true mean eRPM \(\mu\) is unknown.
Sample: the 30 June days. We use them to estimate \(\mu\) — first with a point, later with an interval.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: From Population Parameter to Sample Statistic
text:
From Population Parameter to Sample Statistic


A parameter is a fixed (usually unknown) number describing the population; a statistic is computed from the sample to estimate it.





Population parameter
Point estimator (statistic)




mean \(\mu\)
sample mean \(\bar{x}\)


standard deviation \(\sigma\)
sample SD \(s\)


proportion \(p\)
sample proportion \(\bar{p}\)





Point estimation uses one sample to compute a single number as our best guess of the parameter.
A different random sample gives a different estimate — so a point estimate alone never tells us how close we are.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: St. Andrew’s College


St. Andrew’s received 900 applications. The Director wants the mean SAT score and the proportion wanting on-campus housing — before the full data are entered.
She draws a simple random sample of \(n = 30\) applicants:





Parameter
True value (later)
Sample estimate (\(n=30\))




Mean SAT \(\mu\)
1697
\(\bar{x} = 1684\)


SD \(\sigma\)
87.4
\(s = 85.2\)


Proportion housing \(p\)
0.72
\(\bar{p} = 0.67\)





Each estimate is close but not exact — and a different 30 applicants would have landed elsewhere. That variability is exactly what the sampling distribution describes.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Vungle Point Estimates


Vungle’s 30-day sample gives the same kind of best guesses:





Parameter (true, going forward)
Point estimate from 30 days




Mean eRPM, Algorithm A
\(\bar{x}_A = \$3.347\)


Mean eRPM, Algorithm B
\(\bar{x}_B = \$3.459\)


Mean daily conversion rate, B
\(\bar{x} = 0.354\%\)





These are our spears. The rest of the topic builds the net — the interval that quantifies how far off the spear might be.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: A Distribution of the Estimates Themselves
text:
A Distribution of the Estimates Themselves


Imagine drawing many random samples of size \(n\), computing \(\bar{x}\) for each. Collect all those \(\bar{x}\) values.
Their distribution is the sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) — the probability distribution of the sample mean.
Do not confuse two distributions:

the distribution of the raw data in one sample (30 daily eRPMs), versus
the distribution of the sample mean across many samples.

The sampling distribution is what lets us say how close our \(\bar{x}\) is likely to be to \(\mu\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Center and Spread of \(\bar{x}\)


Center (unbiased): on average, the sample mean equals the population mean —


\[
E(\bar{x}) = \mu
\]


Spread (the standard error): the standard deviation of \(\bar{x}\) is the standard error of the mean —


\[
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\]


The estimate is unbiased (centered on the truth) and gets more precise as \(n\) grows.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Standard Error: Precision Depends on \(n\), Not \(N\)
text:
Standard Error: Precision Depends on \(n\), Not \(N\)


The standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\) depends on the sample size \(n\), not on how big the population is.
This is the wonderful part: to halve the wobble of your estimate you need four times the data (\(\sqrt{n}\)), and it does not matter whether the population is 900 applicants or billions of impressions.





St. Andrew’s: \(\sigma = 87.4\)
\(\sigma_{\bar{x}} = 87.4/\sqrt{n}\)




\(n = 30\)
\(15.96\)


\(n = 100\)
\(8.74\)





More than triple the sample → the standard error roughly halves. Precision is bought with sample size.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Seeing the Sampling Distribution


Repeatedly averaging 10 Vungle A-days: the individual days are scattered, but their averages pile up tightly in a bell around \(\mu\) — narrower than the raw data, and roughly Normal.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The CLT — Why the Bell Always Appears
text:
The CLT — Why the Bell Always Appears


Central Limit Theorem: for random samples of size \(n\) from any population with mean \(\mu\) and SD \(\sigma\), the sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) is approximately


\[
\bar{x} \;\sim\; \text{approximately } N\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) \quad \text{as } n \text{ grows}
\]


It works regardless of the population’s shape — skewed, uniform, lumpy, it does not matter.
Rule of thumb: \(n \geq 30\) is usually enough; \(n \geq 50\) if the population is highly skewed or has outliers. Vungle’s \(n = 30\) sits right on the line.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The CLT in One Picture


Start with a sharply right-skewed population (\(n = 1\)). Average just a handful of values (\(n = 5\)) and it already symmetrizes; by \(n = 30\) the distribution of \(\bar{x}\) is essentially Normal.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Using the CLT: A Probability Question
text:
Using the CLT: A Probability Question

St. Andrew’s: with \(\sigma = 87.4\) and \(n = 30\), what is the probability the sample mean SAT lands within 10 points of the true \(\mu = 1697\) (i.e., between 1687 and 1707)?

\[
\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{87.4}{\sqrt{30}} = 15.96,
\qquad
z = \frac{10}{15.96} = 0.63
\]


\[
P(1687 \le \bar{x} \le 1707) = P(-0.63 \le Z \le 0.63) = 0.4691
\]

Read it: a single sample of 30 has only about a 47% chance of landing within 10 SAT points of the truth — precision you can now quantify, and improve by raising \(n\). In Excel: =NORM.S.DIST(0.63,TRUE)-NORM.S.DIST(-0.63,TRUE).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Proportions Have a Sampling Distribution Too
text:
Proportions Have a Sampling Distribution Too


For a yes/no outcome (an impression converts or it doesn’t), the sample proportion is \(\bar{p} = x/n\).
It has its own sampling distribution, centered and spread by:


\[
E(\bar{p}) = p
\qquad\qquad
\sigma_{\bar{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}
\]


Normal approximation holds when the sample is large enough:


\[
np \geq 5 \qquad \text{and} \qquad n(1-p) \geq 5
\]


St. Andrew’s: \(n = 30\), \(p = 0.72\) → \(np = 21.6 \geq 5\) and \(n(1-p) = 8.4 \geq 5\) ✓ — the normal approximation is acceptable.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — How Trustworthy Is 30 Days?
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — How Trustworthy Is 30 Days?

Decide: is 30 days a precise enough sample to pin down B’s true mean eRPM — or just a noisy first look?
Data: data/vungle_estimation.xlsx (sheet eRPM_by_day) · Time: ~10 min

With the ToolPak’s Descriptive Statistics (or =AVERAGE / =STDEV.S), get \(\bar{x}\) and \(s\) for erpm_B.
Compute the standard error \(s/\sqrt{30}\) — the typical wobble of a 30-day mean.
Compute what the standard error would be at \(n = 120\) (\(s/\sqrt{120}\)). By how much does it shrink, and why?

Hand in (1 slide): B’s \(\bar{x}\), \(s\), the standard error at \(n=30\) vs. \(n=120\), and a one-line verdict: precise enough, or test longer?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Debrief — Lecture 1 Takeaway


One sentence: a sample mean is an unbiased estimate of the truth, but it wobbles by a standard error of \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\) — and thanks to the CLT, that wobble is Normal for \(n \geq 30\).
One number: B’s standard error is \(s/\sqrt{30} = 0.344/\sqrt{30} \approx \$0.063\) — the typical gap between a 30-day mean and the truth.
One caveat: precision is bought with \(n\), and only as \(\sqrt{n}\) — quadrupling the days only halves the error.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward to Lecture 2

We can now say how much a 30-day mean wobbles. Next we turn that wobble into a confidence interval — a stated range for B’s true mean eRPM — and answer the manager’s real question: $3.46, give or take how much?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Brief — $3.46, Give or Take How Much?
text:
The Brief — $3.46, Give or Take How Much?


“Don’t just tell me B earns $3.46. Tell me the range I can bank on — and tell me how many test-days it would take to cut that range in half.”



A point estimate with no range is half an answer. Today we attach a margin of error and report an interval we are 95% confident contains the truth.
Then we invert the question: pick the precision first (\(\pm\$0.05\)), and solve for the sample size that delivers it.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: From Standard Error to a Margin of Error
text:
From Standard Error to a Margin of Error


An interval estimate adds and subtracts a margin of error \(E\) from the point estimate:


\[
\bar{x} \pm E
\qquad\text{where}\qquad
E = (\text{a multiplier}) \times (\text{standard error})
\]


The multiplier comes from the sampling distribution: about 1.96 standard errors capture the middle 95% of a Normal curve.
Two cases for the multiplier:

\(\sigma\) known (rare) → use \(z\).
\(\sigma\) unknown (almost always) → estimate it with \(s\) and use the \(t\) distribution.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Why \(t\), Not \(z\)


In real data we never know \(\sigma\) — we estimate it with the sample SD \(s\). That extra uncertainty makes the bell fatter in the tails: the \(t\) distribution.
A \(t\) distribution is indexed by its degrees of freedom \(df = n - 1\) (the \(n\) observations, minus one used up estimating \(s\)).
As \(df\) grows, \(t\) converges to \(z\): by \(df = 29\) they are nearly identical, and beyond \(df = 100\) the difference is negligible.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
\(t\) Has Fatter Tails Than \(z\)


Fatter tails → a larger multiplier → a wider interval. That is the honest “tax” for not knowing \(\sigma\). Excel: =T.INV.2T(0.05, df) gives the 95% multiplier.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The \(t\)-Interval Formula


With \(\sigma\) unknown (the usual case), the confidence interval for \(\mu\) is:


\[
\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\,\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\]


The pieces: \(\bar{x}\) the point estimate, \(s/\sqrt{n}\) the standard error, \(t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\) the multiplier for confidence level \(1-\alpha\).
Excel multipliers: =T.INV.2T(0.05, n-1) (95%), =T.INV.2T(0.10, n-1) (90%), =T.INV.2T(0.01, n-1) (99%).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
What “95% Confident” Really Means


Correct: “in repeated sampling, about 95% of intervals built this way contain \(\mu\).” The interval is random; \(\mu\) is fixed.
Wrong: “there is a 95% chance \(\mu\) is in this one interval.” Once computed, an interval either contains \(\mu\) or it doesn’t.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: Apartment Rents

A reporter samples \(n = 16\) one-bedroom apartments near campus: \(\bar{x} = \$750\)/month, \(s = \$55\). Population assumed roughly Normal; \(\sigma\) unknown → \(t\)-interval, \(df = 15\).

\[
t_{0.025,\,15} = 2.131,
\qquad
\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{55}{\sqrt{16}} = 13.75,
\qquad
E = 2.131 \times 13.75 = 29.31
\]


\[
750 \pm 29.31 \;=\; (\$720.69,\; \$779.31)
\]

Read it: we are 95% confident the true mean rent for one-bedroom apartments near campus is between $721 and $779. In Excel: =CONFIDENCE.T(0.05, 55, 16) returns the margin $29.31 directly.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Vungle eRPM Interval — the Five Steps
text:
The Vungle eRPM Interval — the Five Steps

1. Business context. B’s true mean eRPM drives every revenue projection; we need a defensible range, not a single number.
2. Setup. \(n = 30\) days, \(\sigma\) unknown, \(\bar{x}_B = \$3.459\), \(s_B = \$0.344\), \(df = 29\), 95% confidence.
3. Compute.
\[
\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.344}{\sqrt{30}} = 0.0629,
\qquad
t_{0.025,\,29} = 2.045,
\qquad
E = 2.045 \times 0.0629 = 0.1286
\]
4. Interval. \(3.459 \pm 0.129 = (\$3.330,\; \$3.588)\).
5. Manager’s Translation. We are 95% confident B’s true mean eRPM is between $3.33 and $3.59. The $0.13 margin is wide relative to the $0.11 gap over A — a first hint that 30 days may not yet settle the A-vs-B question.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Two Intervals, Side by Side
text:
The Two Intervals, Side by Side


A’s interval is ($3.27, $3.43), B’s is ($3.33, $3.59) — they overlap. Estimation alone cannot yet declare a winner; that is the job of hypothesis testing, next topic.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Vungle eRPM CI in Excel



Two equivalent paths:

Formula cells: =AVERAGE, =STDEV.S, =T.INV.2T(0.05,29), then \(\bar{x}\pm t\cdot s/\sqrt{n}\).
One shot: =CONFIDENCE.T(0.05, s, n) returns the margin \(E\) directly.


The ToolPak’s Descriptive Statistics also prints the 95% margin as the Confidence Level(95.0%) row.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Adequate Sample Size for a \(t\)-Interval


A general guide for the \(t\)-interval to behave well:

\(n \geq 30\) is usually adequate.
\(n \geq 50\) if the population is highly skewed or has outliers.
\(n \geq 15\) suffices if the population is roughly symmetric.
\(n < 15\) only if the population is approximately Normal.



Vungle’s eRPM is roughly symmetric (mean \(\approx\) median in our descriptives), so \(n = 30\) clears the bar — the interval is trustworthy; it is just wide.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Proportion Interval


For a population proportion \(p\), the interval uses \(\bar{p}\) and the \(z\) multiplier (no \(t\) — there is no separate SD to estimate):


\[
\bar{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}
\]


Check the large-sample condition first: \(n\bar{p} \geq 5\) and \(n(1-\bar{p}) \geq 5\).
Excel: =NORM.S.INV(0.975) → 1.96 for the 95% multiplier.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: PSI Election Poll

Political Science Inc. contacts \(n = 500\) registered voters; 220 favor the candidate, so \(\bar{p} = 220/500 = 0.44\). Build a 95% interval for the true share.

\[
\sqrt{\frac{0.44(0.56)}{500}} = 0.0222,
\qquad
E = 1.96 \times 0.0222 = 0.0435
\]


\[
0.44 \pm 0.0435 \;=\; (0.3965,\; 0.4835)
\]

Read it: PSI is 95% confident that between 39.6% and 48.4% of all voters favor the candidate. The interval straddles 50% from below — too close to call a lead.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Vungle Conversion Rate — a Proportion in Practice
text:
Vungle Conversion Rate — a Proportion in Practice


Each impression either becomes an install or not — a proportion. We estimate B’s true mean daily conversion rate from the 30 daily rates (a \(t\)-interval on the daily percentages keeps the unit the day, parallel to eRPM):





Quantity
Algorithm B




mean daily rate
\(0.3538\%\)


\(s\)
\(0.0231\%\)


standard error
\(0.0042\%\)


95% margin (\(t_{29}\))
\(0.0086\%\)


95% CI
\((0.345\%,\; 0.362\%)\)





Read it: we are 95% confident B converts between 0.345% and 0.362% of impressions to installs — a tight interval, because day-to-day conversion is very stable.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Vungle Conversion CI in Excel



Same machinery as the mean eRPM interval — just run it on the daily conversion-rate column:

\(\bar{x}\), \(s\) on conv_B_pct
\(t_{0.025,29} = 2.045\)
\(E = t \cdot s/\sqrt{30}\)


Because conversion is stable across days, \(s\) is tiny → a very tight interval.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Three Levers That Set Interval Width
text:
The Three Levers That Set Interval Width


The margin of error \(E = t_{\alpha/2}\,s/\sqrt{n}\) moves with exactly three things:










Lever
Increase it → interval gets
Manager’s control?




Confidence level (\(1-\alpha\))
wider (bigger multiplier)
choose it


Variability (\(s\))
wider
not directly


Sample size (\(n\))
narrower (as \(\sqrt{n}\))
yes — buy more data





The only lever a manager freely controls is \(n\). Want a tighter interval at the same confidence? Collect more days.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Higher Confidence Costs Width


For B’s eRPM, holding \(n = 30\) fixed, raising confidence widens the interval:





Confidence
\(t^*\) multiplier
Margin \(E\)
Interval




90%
1.699
$0.107
($3.352, $3.566)


95%
2.045
$0.129
($3.330, $3.588)


99%
2.756
$0.173
($3.286, $3.632)





More confidence = a wider net. There is no free lunch: to be more sure the interval contains \(\mu\), you must accept a less precise range.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Sample Size: Pick Precision First, Solve for \(n\)
text:
Sample Size: Pick Precision First, Solve for \(n\)


Invert the margin-of-error formula. Set the target \(E\), use a planning \(s\) and \(z\) (95% → 1.96):


\[
E = z_{\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
n = \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}\,s}{E}\right)^{2}
\]


Always round up — a fractional person or day cannot be sampled, and rounding down would miss the target precision.
Planning value for \(s\): a prior study, a pilot sample, or a best guess. Here we use B’s pilot \(s = \$0.344\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: How Many Days Does Vungle Need?
text:
How Many Days Does Vungle Need?

Question: how many test-days for the mean eRPM margin to hit \(\pm\$0.10\)? \(\pm\$0.05\)? (planning \(s = 0.344\), 95% confidence, \(z = 1.96\))

\[
n_{0.10} = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 0.344}{0.10}\right)^{2} = 45.6 \;\rightarrow\; \mathbf{46 \text{ days}}
\]


\[
n_{0.05} = \left(\frac{1.96 \times 0.344}{0.05}\right)^{2} = 182.3 \;\rightarrow\; \mathbf{183 \text{ days}}
\]

The lesson: halving the margin (\(\$0.10 \rightarrow \$0.05\)) takes four times the days (\(46 \rightarrow 183\)). Precision is expensive — and the manager must decide whether $0.05 of precision is worth six months of testing.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Margin of Error vs. Sample Size
text:
Margin of Error vs. Sample Size


The curve flattens fast: early days buy a lot of precision, later days buy very little. The \(\sqrt{n}\) law is why doubling down on data has diminishing returns.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Sample Size in Excel



One formula cell per target:
=CEILING((NORM.S.INV(0.975)*s/E)^2, 1)


s = planning SD (here B’s $0.344)
E = desired margin ($0.10, then $0.05)
CEILING(...,1) rounds up to whole days

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Sample Size for a Proportion


The proportion version, solving the same way:


\[
n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{2}\,p^*(1-p^*)}{E^{2}}
\]


\(p^*\) is a planning value for the proportion: a prior estimate, a pilot, or — if you know nothing — the conservative \(p^* = 0.5\), which maximizes \(p^*(1-p^*)\) and gives the largest (safest) \(n\).
PSI example: for \(E = 0.03\) at 99% (\(z = 2.576\)): with \(p^* = 0.44\), \(n = 1817\); with the conservative \(p^* = 0.5\), \(n = 1844\). When unsure, plan for the larger.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Vungle’s Estimation Memo

Decide: what is B’s true mean eRPM and conversion rate (each \(\pm\) a margin), and how long must the next test run for \(\pm\$0.05\) precision?
Data: data/vungle_estimation.xlsx · Time: ~14 min

95% CI for mean eRPM (B): \(\bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,29}\,s/\sqrt{30}\) — use =CONFIDENCE.T(0.05, s, 30) for the margin.
95% CI for the daily conversion rate (B): same steps on the conv_B_pct column.
Sample size: how many days for the eRPM margin to reach \(\pm\$0.05\)? Use =CEILING((1.96*s/0.05)^2,1).

Hand in (1 slide): the two intervals (with a one-line plain-English reading of each) and the required number of days — your estimation memo to Vungle.

text:
What the Intervals Tell Vungle

($3.330, $3.588)
true earning power, \(\pm\$0.13\)

The memo in one line: B’s true mean eRPM is $3.46 give or take $0.13, its conversion rate is 0.354% give or take 0.009pt, and reaching \(\pm\$0.05\) on eRPM would take about 183 days of testing.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Honest Limit of Estimation


B’s eRPM interval ($3.33, $3.59) and A’s ($3.27, $3.43) overlap. The $0.11 sample gap is real in this sample, but the intervals do not, by themselves, prove B’s true mean beats A’s.
Estimation answers “how big, and how sure are we about that size?” — it does not directly answer “is B better than A?”
That comparison question — does B beat a benchmark, and does B beat A? — is hypothesis testing, the next two topics. Today’s standard error and \(t\) machinery carry straight over.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Manager’s Takeaway


One sentence: a confidence interval turns a point estimate into a defensible range — B’s true mean eRPM is $3.46 \(\pm\) $0.13 at 95% confidence — and the margin of error is the number a manager should actually negotiate over.
One number to remember: \(E = t_{\alpha/2}\,s/\sqrt{n}\) — and the \(\sqrt{n}\) in the denominator, which makes precision expensive.
One caveat: “95% confident” describes the procedure over many samples, not a probability for this one interval — and overlapping intervals do not settle a comparison.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Summary
Some key takeaways from this session:

A point estimate (\(\bar{x}\), \(\bar{p}\)) is a single best guess; it is unbiased but tells you nothing about how close it is.
The sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\) is centered at \(\mu\) with standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\); the CLT makes it Normal for \(n \geq 30\), whatever the population’s shape.
A confidence interval = point estimate \(\pm\) margin of error; use \(t\) for a mean (\(\sigma\) unknown), \(z\) for a proportion.
The margin of error has three levers: confidence level (up → wider), variability \(s\) (up → wider), sample size \(n\) (up → narrower, as \(\sqrt{n}\)).
Sample size inverts the margin formula: pick the precision, solve for \(n\), round up — halving the margin costs ~4× the data.
“95% confident” is a statement about the method, not a probability for one interval — and overlapping intervals do not prove a difference.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward

Your client (homework): for your team’s case, pick one key mean and one key proportion. Build a 95% confidence interval for each, write a one-sentence plain-English reading, and compute the sample size needed to halve one of the margins. Justify your planning value.
Next time: we stop estimating ranges and start testing claims — Hypothesis Testing: fund B only if its mean eRPM beats a benchmark. Today’s standard error and \(t\) tools become the test statistic.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Overview



Case Spotlight: A/B Testing at Vungle
Two designs: matched (paired) vs. independent samples
Matched samples: the paired \(t\)-test
Inferences About the Difference Between Two Population Means

\(\sigma_1, \sigma_2\) Known
\(\sigma_1, \sigma_2\) Unknown: pooled \(t\) and Welch \(t\)




Same data, different design: why pairing matters
Inferences About the Difference Between Two Population Proportions
Statistical vs. practical significance
The rollout decision: from test results to a recommendation

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: June 30, 2014 — Two Analysts and One Month of Data
text:
June 30, 2014 — Two Analysts and One Month of Data


Vungle is a mobile ad-tech startup: it serves 15-second video ads inside other companies’ apps and earns revenue mainly when viewers install the advertised app (cost-per-install).
Two Darden MBAs, Andrew Kritzer and Hammond Guerin, built a new ad-serving algorithm (B) and ran it head-to-head against the existing one (A) for the entire month of June 2014.
The split was random: an MD5 hash of each user ID sent 1/16 of traffic to B, the rest to A. Same market, same days, same apps — only the algorithm differs.
The metric that pays the bills is eRPM: effective revenue per 1,000 impressions.
Guerin is about to become Vungle’s head of data science. His credibility — and his stock options — ride on this call.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Brief — Today’s Managerial Question


“B’s daily eRPM averaged about $0.11 higher than A’s. Is that a real improvement — or just month-to-month noise? Should Vungle roll out B to all traffic?”



In Topic 8 we tested one population against a benchmark (“is B’s mean eRPM above $3.30?”). Today’s is the canonical business question: is B better than A? — comparing two populations.
How today’s studio runs: I demo each test on a textbook example → your team runs it on the Vungle data (two sprints) → we debrief the rollout call together.
By the end of class, your team makes the rollout recommendation — with the statistics to back it up.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
How Today’s Tools Answer It

Every tool today maps onto the Vungle dataset:







Feature of the Vungle test
The tool




Same 30 days observed under both A and B
Matched samples → paired \(t\)-test


What if we (wrongly) ignored the day pairing?
Independent samples \(t\)-test — a cautionary contrast


Millions of impressions → installs (yes/no)
Two-proportion \(z\)-test


“Roll out or not?”
Test results become decision signals (Decision Analysis, Topic 4)





One dataset, three tests — and they will not all point the same way. That tension is today’s managerial lesson.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Canonical Business Question


“Is treatment B systematically different from A?” appears everywhere in business:

New ad algorithm vs. current one (Vungle)
New web page layout vs. old (conversion A/B tests)
Delivery carrier 1 vs. carrier 2 (cost, speed)
Trained employees vs. untrained (productivity)

The null hypothesis is the skeptic’s position: any observed gap is just sampling noise.
Before any formula, you must answer a design question: how were the two samples collected?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Two Ways to Collect the Comparison
text:
Two Ways to Collect the Comparison

Suppose you run a taste test of two flavors:

Paired test (matched samples): select one random sample and let every person rate both flavors (in random order).

Each sampled unit produces one pair of data values.

Unpaired test (independent samples): select two random samples and let each group rate only one flavor.

No natural link between an observation in group 1 and any observation in group 2.

Which is better? The paired test tends to remove much of the extraneous variation — differences in people, days, and experimental conditions — so the flavor effect stands out.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Two Designs, in Pictures


Paired is preferred when feasible — but it requires that every unit can be measured under both conditions.
When the units come from genuinely separate populations (bankrupt vs. non-bankrupt firms), only the independent design exists.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Hypotheses Are About a Difference
text:
The Hypotheses Are About a Difference


All of today’s tests reuse the Topic 8 logic — the only change is the parameter: it is now a difference.


\[
\begin{aligned}
\text{Two means:} \quad & H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_a: \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0 \; (\text{or } >, <) \\
\text{Two proportions:} \quad & H_0: p_1 - p_2 = 0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_a: p_1 - p_2 \neq 0 \; (\text{or } >, <)
\end{aligned}
\]


Same machinery as before: a test statistic, a rejection region, a \(p\)-value, and a decision at significance level \(\alpha\).
The equality always lives in \(H_0\); the claim we seek evidence for lives in \(H_a\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text: Choosing the Right Test

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Test Selection Cheat Sheet











Situation
Test
Test statistic
Distribution
Excel




Same units, two conditions
Paired \(t\)
\(t = \dfrac{\bar{d} - 0}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}\)
\(t\), \(df = n-1\)
ToolPak: t-Test: Paired Two Sample for Means


Independent, \(\sigma_1,\sigma_2\) known
Two-sample \(z\)
\(z = \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2) - D_0}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}}\)
\(N(0,1)\)
ToolPak: z-Test: Two Sample for Means


Independent, \(\sigma\) unknown, assumed equal
Pooled \(t\)
\(t = \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2) - D_0}{s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}}\)
\(t\), \(df = n_1+n_2-2\)
ToolPak: t-Test: Two-Sample Assuming Equal Variances


Independent, \(\sigma\) unknown, not assumed equal
Welch \(t\) (default)
\(t = \dfrac{(\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2) - D_0}{\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}}\)
\(t\), Satterthwaite \(df\)
ToolPak: t-Test: Two-Sample Assuming Unequal Variances


Two proportions (large samples)
Two-proportion \(z\)
\(z = \dfrac{\bar{p}_1-\bar{p}_2}{\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})(1/n_1+1/n_2)}}\)
\(N(0,1)\)
No built-in tool — formula cells

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: One Idea: Reduce Two Samples to One
text:
One Idea: Reduce Two Samples to One


With a matched-sample design, each sampled unit provides a pair of data values: \((x_{1i}, x_{2i})\).
Key insight: work with the difference for each pair,


\[
d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}
\]


The two-sample problem becomes a one-sample problem about the mean difference \(\mu_d\) — and we can apply exactly the single-population procedures from Topic 8.
Point estimate of \(\mu_d\):


\[
\bar{d} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Paired \(t\) Mechanics



Sample standard deviation of the differences:


\[
s_d = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (d_i - \bar{d})^2}{n - 1}}
\]


Standard error of \(\bar{d}\):


\[
SE(\bar{d}) = \frac{s_d}{\sqrt{n}}
\]


Test statistic (under \(H_0: \mu_d = 0\)), with \(df = n - 1\):


\[
t = \frac{\bar{d} - 0}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Tests, Intervals, and Decision Rules


Hypothesis forms: \(H_0: \mu_d = 0\) vs. \(H_a: \mu_d \neq 0\) (two-tailed), or the one-sided versions \(H_a: \mu_d > 0\) / \(H_a: \mu_d < 0\).
Decision rule (critical value): reject \(H_0\) if \(|t| \geq t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}\) (two-tailed) or \(t \geq t_{\alpha,\,n-1}\) (upper-tail).
Confidence interval for \(\mu_d\):


\[
\bar{d} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \frac{s_d}{\sqrt{n}}
\]


\(p\)-values in Excel:

Two-tailed: =T.DIST.2T(ABS(t), df)
Upper-tail: =T.DIST.RT(t, df)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: Express Deliveries

To compare delivery times of two express services, UPX and INTEX, two reports were sent to each of ten district offices — one carried by UPX, the other by INTEX. Same origin, same destination, same day: a natural pair.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Express Deliveries — the Five Steps

1. Business context. Choosing a carrier; the metric is delivery time (hours); slower deliveries cost client goodwill.
2. Hypotheses (is there any difference? → two-tailed):
\[
H_0: \mu_d = 0 \qquad H_a: \mu_d \neq 0 \qquad \alpha = 0.05
\]
3. Test statistic. From the ten differences:
\[
\bar{d} = 2.70, \quad s_d = 2.908, \quad SE = \frac{2.908}{\sqrt{10}} = 0.920, \quad t = \frac{2.70}{0.920} = 2.94, \quad df = 9
\]
4. Decision. \(p\text{-value} = 0.017 \leq \alpha = 0.05\) → reject \(H_0\). Also: 95% CI for \(\mu_d\) = \((0.620,\; 4.780)\) hours — zero is not a plausible mean difference.
5. Manager’s Translation. UPX averages about 2.7 hours slower than INTEX on identical routes — a real difference, not noise. If speed matters, route the volume to INTEX (then negotiate with UPX).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Express Deliveries in Excel



Data → Data Analysis → t-Test: Paired Two Sample for Means

Variable 1 Range: UPX column
Variable 2 Range: INTEX column
Hypothesized Mean Difference: 0
Alpha: 0.05


Read the output: t Stat = 2.936, P(T<=t) two-tail = 0.017 \(\leq\) 0.05 → reject \(H_0\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Step 1 — Business Context: Why a Paired Design?
text:
Step 1 — Business Context: Why a Paired Design?


Each of the 30 days of June 2014 is a natural experimental unit: on any given day, the same advertising market operates — same advertisers, same user pool, same day-of-week effects.
Users were randomly split within each day: A and B ran side by side.
So each day yields one pair: \((\text{eRPM}_A, \text{eRPM}_B)\) — and one difference


\[
d_i = \text{eRPM}_{B,i} - \text{eRPM}_{A,i}
\]


Pairing subtracts out day-specific noise (weekend dips, late-June seasonality) and isolates the pure algorithm effect.
Data: data/vungle_erpm_paired.csv — 30 rows, columns erpm_A, erpm_B, diff_B_minus_A.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Data: 30 Days of eRPM


B starts below A, then pulls ahead for most of the month. Eyeballing is suggestive — but Guerin needs more than an eyeball to bet his job on.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Differences: \(d = B - A\)


26 of 30 days favor B; the mean difference is +$0.112 per 1,000 impressions.
The paired \(t\)-test asks: could a mean difference this large arise by chance if the true difference were zero?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Step 2 — Hypotheses


\[
H_0: \mu_d \leq 0 \qquad \text{vs.} \qquad H_a: \mu_d > 0 \qquad \text{(upper-tail)} \qquad \alpha = 0.05
\]



Why one-tailed? The business decision is asymmetric: Vungle rolls out B only if it is better. “B is worse” and “B is the same” lead to the identical action — keep A.
\(\mu_d\) = the true mean daily eRPM advantage of B over A.
We will also report the two-tailed \(p\)-value — the more conservative convention — and the conclusion will not change.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Step 3 — Test Statistic

From the 30 daily differences in vungle_erpm_paired.csv:




Quantity
Value




\(n\) (days)
30


\(\bar{d}\) (mean difference)
$0.1119


\(s_d\)
0.1843


\(SE = s_d/\sqrt{30}\)
0.0337


\(t = \bar{d}/SE\)
3.325


\(df\)
29






\[
t = \frac{0.1119}{0.1843/\sqrt{30}} = \frac{0.1119}{0.0337} = 3.325
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Excel: t-Test Paired Two Sample for Means
text:
Excel: t-Test Paired Two Sample for Means



Data → Data Analysis → t-Test: Paired Two Sample for Means

Variable 1 Range: erpm_B column
Variable 2 Range: erpm_A column

Alpha: 0.05


Key rows:

t Stat = 3.325
P(T<=t) one-tail = 0.0012

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Step 4 — Decision


Critical value: \(t = 3.325 \geq t_{0.05,29} = 1.699\) → reject \(H_0\).
\(p\)-value: one-tail \(p \approx 0.0012\) (two-tail \(\approx 0.0024\)) \(\leq \alpha = 0.05\) → reject \(H_0\).
Strong evidence that algorithm B’s mean daily eRPM is higher than A’s.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Confidence Interval Tells You the Size
text:
The Confidence Interval Tells You the Size


The test says the lift is real; the confidence interval says how big it plausibly is:


\[
\bar{d} \pm t_{0.025,29}\frac{s_d}{\sqrt{n}} = 0.1119 \pm 2.045 \times 0.0337 \;=\; (\$0.043,\; \$0.181)
\]


The entire interval is above zero — consistent with rejecting \(H_0\).
Even the lower bound ($0.043 per 1,000 impressions) is a meaningful lift at Vungle’s scale.
In Excel: =T.INV(0.975, 29) → 2.045; bounds with formula cells from \(\bar d\) and \(SE\).

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Step 5 — Manager’s Translation: Back to the Decision
text:
Step 5 — Manager’s Translation: Back to the Decision


“A difference this large would arise by chance only about 1 in 1,000 times if B were no better than A — so we reject the ‘no difference’ story. On a typical day, B earns between $0.04 and $0.18 more per 1,000 impressions than A.”
Scale it: A served ≈ 236.5M impressions in June. Had B served that traffic:


\[
\frac{236{,}459{,}402}{1{,}000} \times \$0.112 \approx \$26{,}500 \text{ per month} \;\approx\; \$318\text{K per year}
\]


Even at the CI lower bound ($0.043), the lift is ≈ $10K/month — material for a startup with $25.5M raised.
Discussion hook: the daily gap is noisy — the first week even favored A, and after two weeks B’s lead was only ≈ $0.04, still well below the full-month $0.11. Stopping the test early could have hidden the effect. (Sequential testing — beyond today’s scope, but exactly what real A/B platforms wrestle with.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: ⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — Is the lift real?
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 1 — Is the lift real?

Decide: does algorithm B truly earn more per 1,000 impressions than A?
Data: data/vungle_erpm_paired.csv · Time: ~8 min

In Excel: Data → Data Analysis → t-Test: Paired Two Sample for Means (Variable 1 = erpm_B, Variable 2 = erpm_A, α = 0.05).
Read off t Stat and P(T<=t) one-tail; build the 95% CI for the mean difference.
Decide at α = 0.05 — is the lift real?

Hand in (1 slide): your \(t\), \(p\), and CI, plus a one-line verdict for Guerin.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Setup and Point Estimate


Two independent random samples: sizes \(n_1, n_2\) from populations with means \(\mu_1, \mu_2\).
Point estimate of \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\): \(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2\).
Sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2\): centered at \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\), with standard error built from both sampling variances:


\[
SE(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}
\]


Which test statistic you use depends on what you know about \(\sigma_1, \sigma_2\) — the three cases that follow.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Case 1 — \(\sigma_1, \sigma_2\) Known: the Two-Sample \(z\)


Rare in practice (you seldom know population standard deviations), but conceptually clean:


\[
z = \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - D_0}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1 + \sigma_2^2/n_2}}
\qquad\qquad
\text{CI:}\;\; (\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}
\]


\(D_0\) = hypothesized difference, usually 0.
Excel: Data Analysis → z-Test: Two Sample for Means (requires the two known variances as inputs); \(p\)-values via =NORM.S.DIST.
Note: the worksheet function =Z.TEST is for one sample only — the two-sample version needs the ToolPak tool or formula cells.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Case 2 — \(\sigma\) Unknown, Assumed Equal: the Pooled \(t\)
text:
Case 2 — \(\sigma\) Unknown, Assumed Equal: the Pooled \(t\)


If it is reasonable to assume \(\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma\), combine both samples into one pooled variance:


\[
s_p^2 = \frac{(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}
\]


\[
t = \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - D_0}{s_p\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}},
\qquad df = n_1 + n_2 - 2
\]


CI: \((\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n_1+n_2-2}\; s_p\sqrt{1/n_1 + 1/n_2}\)
Excel: Data Analysis → t-Test: Two-Sample Assuming Equal Variances.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Case 3 — \(\sigma\) Unknown, Not Assumed Equal: the Welch \(t\)
text:
Case 3 — \(\sigma\) Unknown, Not Assumed Equal: the Welch \(t\)



Drop the equal-variance assumption — each sample keeps its own variance:


\[
t = \frac{(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) - D_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}
\]


Degrees of freedom (Satterthwaite approximation; typically non-integer — round down):


\[
df = \frac{\left(\dfrac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{s_2^2}{n_2}\right)^2}{\dfrac{(s_1^2/n_1)^2}{n_1 - 1} + \dfrac{(s_2^2/n_2)^2}{n_2 - 1}}
\]


CI: \((\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,df}\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1 + s_2^2/n_2}\)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Practical Guidance: Default to Welch


Unless you have a strong theoretical reason to assume equal variances, use Welch. It is the safe default: when variances are equal it loses almost nothing; when they differ it protects you.
Excel: Data Analysis → t-Test: Two-Sample Assuming Unequal Variances.
Reading any ToolPak \(t\)-test output — the same rows every time:

Mean, Variance, Observations — the summary statistics per group
df — degrees of freedom used
t Stat — the test statistic
P(T<=t) one-tail / two-tail — your \(p\)-values
t Critical one-tail / two-tail — the cutoffs for \(\alpha\)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Anchor Example: Start-up Owner’s Equity

Two populations (no pairing is possible — a firm is either bankrupt or it isn’t):

Small business firms that went bankrupt within the first year
Firms that are not bankrupt

Variable of interest: owner’s equity ($K) at founding.




Group
\(n\)
\(\bar{x}\)
\(s\)




Bankrupt
15
5.73
3.33


Non-bankrupt
15
7.87
2.61




Question: is there a difference in average owner’s equity between bankrupt and non-bankrupt firms?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Owner’s Equity — the Five Steps

1. Business context. Does thin capitalization at founding predict first-year failure? (Lender screening, founder advice.)
2. Hypotheses (two-tailed): \(H_0: \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0\) vs. \(H_a: \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0\), \(\alpha = 0.05\). Independent samples, \(\sigma\) unknown, no reason to assume equal variances → Welch \(t\).
3. Test statistic.
\[
t = \frac{(5.73 - 7.87) - 0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3.33^2}{15} + \dfrac{2.61^2}{15}}} = \frac{-2.14}{1.093} = -1.96, \qquad df \approx 26
\]
4. Decision. \(p\)-value (two-tail) \(\approx 0.061 > \alpha = 0.05\) → fail to reject \(H_0\). 95% CI for \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\): \((-4.4,\; 0.1)\) — barely includes zero.
5. Manager’s Translation. Bankrupt firms started with about $2.1K less equity on average, but with only 15 firms per group we cannot rule out chance at the 5% level. A borderline result — worth a bigger sample before building lending policy on it.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Owner’s Equity in Excel



Data → Data Analysis → t-Test: Two-Sample Assuming Unequal Variances

Variable 1 Range: Bankrupt column
Variable 2 Range: Non-bankrupt column
Hypothesized Mean Difference: 0


“Fail to reject” ≠ “the means are equal.” With \(n = 15\) per group the test has low power; a $2K average gap may be economically real yet statistically undetectable here.
(With equal \(n\), the pooled \(t\) gives the same \(t\)-statistic with \(df = 28\) — same conclusion.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: What If We Ignored the Pairing?
text:
What If We Ignored the Pairing?



Treat the 30 A-days and 30 B-days as two independent samples (Welch) — wrong design, same data:

\(\bar{x}_A = 3.347\), \(s_A = 0.217\)
\(\bar{x}_B = 3.459\), \(s_B = 0.344\)
\(t = 1.51\), \(p\) (two-tail) \(\approx 0.138\)


Fail to reject \(H_0\) at \(\alpha = 0.05\). The $0.11 lift “disappears.”
(ToolPak: Variable 1 = erpm_B, Variable 2 = erpm_A — same order as the paired test, so \(t\) is positive.)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
The Lesson: Design Drives Conclusions





Paired \(t\) (correct)
Independent \(t\) (wrong)




Test statistic
3.33
1.51


\(p\)-value (two-tail)
0.0024
0.138


Decision at \(\alpha = .05\)
Reject \(H_0\) — B wins
Fail to reject





Same data. Same question. Different design assumption → opposite conclusion.
Why: day-to-day market swings (weekends, seasonality) inflate the independent-samples noise (\(s \approx 0.22\)–\(0.34\)), while pairing cancels them (\(s_d = 0.18\) on the differences, and the A/B series are correlated at \(r = 0.88\)).
The design decision was made before any data were collected — when Vungle chose to run A and B in parallel within each day. Good analysis starts at design, not at the formula sheet.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Vungle’s Other Metric: Conversion Rate


eRPM is revenue per impression. Advertisers also watch the conversion rate: installs ÷ impressions.
The case: “small improvements in the click-through or conversion rates could have a large effect on Vungle’s revenue.”
June 2014 totals (vungle_daily.csv):





Metric
Algorithm A
Algorithm B




Impressions
236,459,402
15,825,376


Installs
951,666
56,031


Conversion rate \(\bar{p}\)
0.4025%
0.3541%





Question: does B convert impressions to installs at a different rate than A?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Unit of Observation: Days vs. Impressions
text:
Unit of Observation: Days vs. Impressions


Careful — the “sample size” just changed by seven orders of magnitude:

For eRPM, the unit was the day: \(n = 30\) paired days.
For conversion, the unit is the impression: each one is a Bernoulli trial (install / no install), so \(n_1 \approx 236\)M and \(n_2 \approx 16\)M.

Large-sample conditions easily satisfied: \(n\bar{p} \geq 5\) and \(n(1-\bar{p}) \geq 5\) for both groups (e.g., \(n_2\bar{p}_2 = 56{,}031 \gg 5\)).
Also note the traffic imbalance: A got ~15× B’s impressions (the 1/16 hash split). That is exactly why we compare rates, never raw counts — B’s 56K installs vs. A’s 952K says nothing by itself.
Honest caveat: impressions within a day are not perfectly independent (time structure). Beyond today’s scope — but a good question to ask any A/B platform.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Estimating \(p_1 - p_2\)


Two independent large samples; observe \(\bar{p}_1 = x_1/n_1\) and \(\bar{p}_2 = x_2/n_2\).
Point estimate: \(\bar{p}_1 - \bar{p}_2\).
Confidence interval — each group keeps its own variance (do not pool for CIs):


\[
(\bar{p}_1 - \bar{p}_2) \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}_1(1-\bar{p}_1)}{n_1} + \frac{\bar{p}_2(1-\bar{p}_2)}{n_2}}
\]

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Testing \(p_1 - p_2 = 0\): Pool Under \(H_0\)


Under \(H_0\) the two proportions are the same \(p\) — so the best estimate of it uses all the data:


\[
\bar{p} = \frac{x_1 + x_2}{n_1 + n_2}
\qquad\qquad
SE_{pool} = \sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})\left(\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}\right)}
\]


Test statistic:


\[
z = \frac{\bar{p}_1 - \bar{p}_2}{SE_{pool}}
\]


Decision rule and \(p\)-value exactly as in the one-proportion \(z\)-test (Topic 8); Excel: =NORM.S.DIST.
Rule of thumb: pool under \(H_0\) (testing); do not pool for confidence intervals.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: No ToolPak Tool: Build It with Formulas
text:
No ToolPak Tool: Build It with Formulas



The Data Analysis ToolPak has no two-proportion z tool — five formula cells do the job.

This is worth mastering: most real A/B testing metrics (click-through, conversion, churn) are proportions.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Vungle Conversion — the Five Steps

1. Business context. Advertisers pay mostly per install; conversion rate signals how well ads match users.
2. Hypotheses (two-tailed): \(H_0: p_B - p_A = 0\) vs. \(H_a: p_B - p_A \neq 0\), \(\alpha = 0.05\).
3. Test statistic.
\[
\bar{p} = \frac{951{,}666 + 56{,}031}{236{,}459{,}402 + 15{,}825{,}376} = 0.3994\%,
\qquad SE_{pool} = 1.64 \times 10^{-5}
\]
\[
z = \frac{0.003541 - 0.004025}{0.0000164} = \mathbf{-29.56}
\]
4. Decision. \(|z| = 29.56 \gg z_{0.025} = 1.96\); \(p\)-value \(< 0.0001\) → reject \(H_0\) overwhelmingly.
5. Manager’s Translation. B converts impressions to installs at a genuinely lower rate than A — 0.354% vs. 0.403%, a 12% relative drop. This is not noise.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: The Surprise: B Converts Less but Earns More
text:
The Surprise: B Converts Less but Earns More









Metric
Winner
Evidence




eRPM (revenue per 1,000 impressions)
B
paired \(t = 3.33\), \(p = 0.0024\)


Conversion rate (installs per impression)
A
\(z = -29.56\), \(p < 0.0001\)





Both results are statistically decisive — and they point in opposite directions.
This is not a data error. It is the most realistic moment in this course: real decisions face multiple metrics that disagree.
So which metric should drive the rollout?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
⏱️ Team Sprint 2 — Make the call

The conflict: B earns more per impression (paired \(t = 3.33\)) but converts fewer installs (\(z = -29.6\)). Both results are statistically decisive.
Decide (≈12 min): write CEO Jaffer’s rollout recommendation.

Which metric should drive the decision for Vungle’s business model — and why?
What would you monitor after rollout, and why?
State it in three sentences: the call · the number · the caveat.

Hand in (1 slide): your rollout recommendation to the CEO.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Reconciling the Two Metrics


eRPM rewards revenue per impression, not installs per impression.
The case tells us eRPM varied from $2 to $7 per campaign, and the vast majority of Vungle’s ads were CPI — advertisers pay per install, at very different prices.
So B apparently selects higher-value campaigns: fewer installs, but each install is worth more — a richer optimization target than raw conversion.
Analogy: a salesperson who closes fewer deals at much higher margins.
The metric must match the business model. Vungle is a revenue-maximizing platform → eRPM is the right north star; conversion is a health metric to monitor.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Statistical vs. Practical Significance


With \(n\) in the hundreds of millions, the conversion test was guaranteed to be “significant”: at this scale, even a microscopic difference produces a huge \(z\).
\(z = -29.56\) tells you the difference is real; it does not tell you the difference matters.
Always pair the test with the effect size: -0.048 percentage points (a 12% relative drop in conversion) — that is the number a manager should debate.
Flip side from the equity example: small samples can make real effects invisible (low power). Large \(n\): everything is significant. Small \(n\): nothing is. Neither replaces judgment about magnitude.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Three Tests, One Decision









Test
Result
Decision signal




Paired \(t\) — eRPM (B vs. A)
\(t = 3.33\), \(p = 0.0024\)
B earns more per impression. Roll out.


Independent \(t\) — same data, wrong design
\(t = 1.51\), \(p = 0.138\)
(Cautionary: wrong design would have hidden the lift.)


Two-proportion \(z\) — conversion
\(z = -29.56\), \(p < 0.0001\)
B converts less. Monitor.





Recommendation to CEO Jaffer: roll out B — eRPM is the metric aligned with Vungle’s business model, the lift is statistically solid ($0.04–$0.18 per 1,000), and it is worth ≈ $26.5K/month on June’s traffic. Track the install funnel weekly post-rollout in case the lower conversion rate erodes advertiser satisfaction.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
From Inference to Decision Analysis


In Topic 4 you computed expected values for roll out / keep A / test first. Today’s output feeds straight into that framework:

The CI \((\$0.043, \$0.181)\) per 1,000 impressions is the range of payoffs for the “roll out” branch.
The \(p\)-value bounds the probability of the bad state (“B is not actually better”) — the input Bayesian revision needs.

This is the division of labor in analytics:

Inference (Topics 7–9): what is true, and how sure are we?
Decision analysis (Topic 4): given that uncertainty, what should we do?

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
section: Two-Population Tests on One Page — the Vungle Bridge Table
text:
Two-Population Tests on One Page — the Vungle Bridge Table











Test

\(t = 3.33\), \(p = .0024\)

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Manager’s Takeaway


One sentence: Algorithm B earns about $0.11 more per 1,000 impressions than A, and the paired test says that lift is real, not luck (\(p < 0.01\)) — roll out B.
One number to remember: \(t = 3.33\) — and where it came from: \(\bar{d}/(s_d/\sqrt{n})\).
One caveat: B’s conversion rate is lower (\(z = -29.6\)); monitor the install funnel after rollout, because the eRPM advantage depends on high-value campaigns continuing to bid.
Practice with the real data:

data/vungle_erpm_paired.csv + ToolPak → reproduce \(t = 3.325\) (paired) and \(t = 1.51\) (unequal variances).
data/vungle_daily.csv + five formula cells → reproduce \(z = -29.56\).
Worked solutions: data/vungle_ab_analysis.xlsx.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Summary
Some key takeaways from this session:

Design comes first: matched (paired) samples reduce two-sample problems to one-sample problems on the differences — and remove extraneous unit-to-unit variation.
The paired \(t\), pooled \(t\), Welch \(t\), two-sample \(z\), and two-proportion \(z\) all reuse the Topic 8 logic; only the parameter (a difference) and the standard error change.
Default to Welch for independent means; pool only under \(H_0\) for proportion tests.
Same data, different design → different conclusion: Vungle’s lift is decisive paired (\(t = 3.33\)) and invisible unpaired (\(t = 1.51\)).
Statistical significance ≠ practical significance: with 252M impressions everything is “significant” — judge the effect size; with \(n = 15\) per group, real effects can hide — mind the power.
Test results are inputs to decisions, not decisions: the rollout call weighs the metric that matches the business model.

title: QM 6700: Business Analytics
text:
Carry-forward

Your client (homework): find two groups in your team’s case — two segments, two periods, or treatment vs. control — and run the right two-sample test. Justify the design: paired or independent?
Next time: we stop comparing and start predicting — Simple Regression (a new client: an auto-parts retailer choosing store sites).

title: Syllabus
text:
Course Description and Objectives
This course develops the quantitative and data-analysis skills managers need to make data-driven decisions. Coverage includes descriptive statistics, probability and probability distributions, decision analysis, sampling distributions and interval estimation, hypothesis testing, and applied regression analysis (simple and multiple). (Scope to be finalized during the course redesign.)
Our primary goal is to instill in students the quantitative and data analysis skills essential in modern business, where Business Analytics and Data Science are increasingly important. These skills will empower managers to make data-based decisions, perform risk assessments, develop policies, and collaborate effectively with analytics teams to boost business performance.
Because modern data analysis relies heavily on computers and software, we will emphasize practical applications using Microsoft Excel (with exposure to Minitab, R, or Python when relevant).
Course Website: https://davi-moreira.github.io/2026F_business_analytics_qm670/

title: Lecture Guide — T11 · Multiple Regression
text:
Spine case: Auto-parts retail · Data: data/autoparts.csv (45 stores) · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: fit and read a multiple regression, interpret each partial coefficient (“holding the others constant”), tell the overall \(F\)-test apart from the individual \(t\)-tests, price a category with a dummy variable, sanity-check multicollinearity, and turn the output into an investment recommendation.
The one trap that carries the topic: the two drivers a manager expects to matter — population and income — are not significant once Yelp (service) and endcap (merchandising) are in the model. Significance is conditional; bivariate intuition misleads.



Goal of the day: the team fits sales on all four drivers and reads each partial effect + the fit.


Pose: “Invest in location, service, or merchandising — which actually drives sales?” Have teams commit a gut ranking of the four drivers (most will rank population/income at the top — set that up, you’ll overturn it). Remind them last topic’s simple model gave Yelp ≈ +$758K/star in isolation.




From one predictor to many: model \(y=\beta_0+\beta_1x_1+\cdots+\beta_px_p+\epsilon\); the plane picture; least squares minimizes \(\sum(y_i-\hat y_i)^2\) exactly as before — Excel does the matrix algebra.
The partial coefficient — hammer the clause: \(b_j\) = effect of \(x_j\) holding all others constant. This is the concept of the day; everything else hangs on it.
Demo (Excel) on Butler Trucking (anchor, \(n=10\)): Data → Data Analysis → Regression, Y = travel time, X = the two columns (miles, deliveries) together. Read \(\hat y = -0.869 + 0.0611x_1 + 0.923x_2\); interpret each \(b\); predict assignment #1 → 8.94 hr; residual +0.36. Fit: \(R^2=0.904\), adj \(R^2=0.876\).
\(R^2\) vs adjusted \(R^2\): stress that \(R^2\) can only rise when you add variables (even noise) → you need the per-predictor penalty of adjusted \(R^2\) for honest model comparison (\(R^2_a\le R^2\) always).
Spine demo: run the four-variable model on autoparts.csv; show \(R^2=0.932\), adj \(0.925\), \(s=308.5\); surface the surprise (population +$0.35K/1,000 ppl, income +$4.7K/$1,000 — both tiny).
Quick checks: “Why select all four X columns at once?” “Why can’t a bigger \(R^2\) settle which model is better?”




Teams run Regression on autoparts.csv (Y = sales2012_k; X = the four columns together; Labels + 95% CI). Write the equation; record \(R^2\) vs adjusted \(R^2\); translate Yelp & endcap into dollars. - Mentor: make sure they select all four X columns contiguously; have them say the “holding the others constant” clause out loud for at least one coefficient. Push on the population/income surprise. - Pitfalls: selecting only one X column; reading the raw population coefficient (0.00035) without scaling to “per 1,000 residents”; calling a small coefficient “wrong” rather than “small/insignificant.” - Answer key (instructor): - Equation: \(\widehat{\text{sales}} = -52.1 + 0.00035\,\text{pop} + 0.0047\,\text{income} + 642.3\,\text{yelp} + 816.5\,\text{endcap}\). - \(R^2 = 0.932\), Adjusted \(R^2 = 0.925\), \(s = \$308.5\)K, \(n=45\). - Yelp ≈ +$642K per star; endcap ≈ +$816K, each holding the others fixed. - Population/income partial effects are negligible (per-1,000-resident ≈ +$0.35K; per-$1,000-income ≈ +$4.7K).



1–2 teams pitch. Land: 93% explained; the levers that matter are service & merchandising, not the market-size variables intuition favors. Surface the pitfall: a near-zero coefficient means “adds little once the others are in,” not “irrelevant in the world.” Tee up Lecture 2: are these effects statistically real, and what is an endcap worth?



Homework part 1 (team client): fit a multiple regression with ≥2 predictors; report equation, \(R^2\), adjusted \(R^2\); interpret each coefficient with the “holding others constant” clause. Preview: \(F\) vs \(t\), the dummy variable, multicollinearity.





Goal of the day: the team decides which drivers are real, prices an endcap, and makes the CFO’s call.


Pose: “The model fits — but is each driver real? Put a defensible dollar figure on an endcap. Then: location, service, or merchandising?” Note a high \(R^2\) says nothing about which single coefficients to trust.




\(F\) vs \(t\) — different jobs now (in simple regression they agreed). \(F\) first (is the model useful at all?), then the \(t\)’s (which predictors carry weight?).
\(F\)-test: \(H_0:\beta_1=\cdots=\beta_p=0\); \(F=MSR/MSE=(SSR/p)/(SSE/(n-p-1))\); Excel’s Significance F. On the spine: \(F=136.7\), df (4, 40), p \(\approx 3.4\times10^{-23}\) → reject overwhelmingly.
\(t\)-tests: \(t=b_j/s_{b_j}\), df \(=n-p-1=40\); \(|t|>2\) ⇒ significant. Walk the coefficient table: Yelp \(t=16.5\), endcap \(t=6.98\) (both significant); population \(t=0.88\), income \(t=1.50\) (both not). The headline: significant \(F\), yet half the predictors are individually insignificant.
Dummy variable: endcap is a category, encode 0/1, reference = 0. Coefficient = mean sales gap between endcap and non-endcap stores holding others fixed = the dollar value of an endcap = $816.5K. Show the parallel lines picture (same slope, intercept shifted up $816K). Note \(k\) levels → \(k-1\) dummies; never encode categories as a single 1/2/3 number.
Prediction demo: planned store pop=120k, income=55k, yelp=4.0 → without endcap $2,816K, with endcap $3,632K; lift +$816K/yr. Decision rule: buy the endcap if the lease < ~$816K/yr.
Multicollinearity: correlated predictors inflate SEs, destabilize/flip coefficients, can hide a real predictor. Detect via correlation matrix (|r|>0.7) and VIF \(=1/(1-R_j^2)\) (investigate > 5). Auto-parts is clean — every VIF < 1.7 — so population/income are insignificant for an honest reason, not an artifact.
Quick checks: “Significant \(F\) — does that mean every variable matters?” (no) “What does the endcap coefficient mean in dollars?” “If population & income were highly correlated, how would the story change?”




From their output: read Significance F; mark each predictor significant/not (\(|t|>2\)); price the endcap via the prediction (endcap 0 vs 1) for pop=120k/income=55k/yelp=4.0; write a 3-sentence call. - Mentor: push them past “the bigger coefficient wins” to “which are statistically real and worth the money.” Make them attach a caveat (descriptive, not causal; holding-others-constant). - Pitfalls: declaring population/income important because \(F\) is huge; forgetting the prediction is \(b_0+b_1\text{pop}+b_2\text{income}+b_3\text{yelp}(+b_4)\); treating “not significant” as “exactly zero.” - Answer key (instructor): - Overall: \(F=136.7\) (df 4, 40), Significance F \(\approx 3.4\times10^{-23}\) → model highly useful. - Significant: Yelp (\(t=16.5\)), Endcap (\(t=6.98\)). Not significant: Population (\(t=0.88\), p=0.39), Income (\(t=1.50\), p=0.14). - Endcap value: predicted $3,632K (with) − $2,816K (without) = +$816K/yr. - All VIF < 1.7 (pop 1.17, income 1.49, yelp 1.29, endcap 1.62) → no multicollinearity; verdict trustworthy. - Model call: fund service (Yelp, +$642K/star) and endcap placement (+$816K, worth any sub-$816K lease); don’t chase bigger/richer markets. Caveat: descriptive model on 45 existing stores — pilot the endcap and re-estimate before scaling.



1–2 teams pitch the investment call. Land: \(F\) and \(t\) answer different questions and you need both (\(F\) alone would have kept all four variables; the \(t\)’s cut the dead weight); significance is conditional on the other predictors; significance ≠ importance — always pair the test with the dollar effect size. Walk the one-page summary table.



Homework part 2 (team client): run the overall \(F\)-test and individual \(t\)-tests; if the case has a category, add a dummy and interpret it as a group difference; check VIF for suspected overlap; close with a one-paragraph recommendation (which lever, what it’s worth). Preview: this closes the prediction arc — from comparing groups → predicting with one driver → isolating many at once.






data/autoparts.csv in Excel (45 rows; endcap already 0/1). Worked solutions + every number in data/autoparts_regression.xlsx (tabs: Full_Model, Fit_Statistics, VIF, Model_Comparison, Prediction, Butler_Anchor). Analysis ToolPak installed (Data → Data Analysis → Regression / Correlation).
Butler anchor numbers (for the Lecture-1 demo): \(\hat y=-0.869+0.0611x_1+0.923x_2\); \(R^2=0.904\), adj \(0.876\), \(s=0.573\); \(F=32.88\) (df 2,7); miles \(t=6.18\), deliveries \(t=4.18\).
Excel tip for VIF: no built-in tool — run an auxiliary regression of each \(x_j\) on the other X’s and compute \(1/(1-R_j^2)\), or just show the correlation matrix and the precomputed VIFs.





“A bigger \(R^2\) means a better model.” (\(R^2\) only rises with more predictors → use adjusted \(R^2\).)
“The \(F\)-test is significant, so every predictor matters.” (No — \(F\) is the whole-model test; read the \(t\)’s.)
“Population/income coefficients are ~0, so those variables don’t matter at all.” (They add little once Yelp & endcap are in — significance is conditional, not absolute.)
“Bigger coefficient ⇒ more important.” (Compare on units/dollars and on significance; a raw coefficient on a large-scale variable like population looks tiny by construction.)
“We can just code the 3 store formats as 1, 2, 3.” (No — that forces order & equal spacing; use \(k-1\) dummies.)
“Regression proves the endcap causes +$816K.” (Descriptive control on observational data; pilot to confirm.)
