title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv (filter algorithm = "B") · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: model a continuous business metric as Normal, compute a tail probability (NORM.DIST) and a percentile (NORM.INV), check whether the Normal assumption holds (QQ plot / Shapiro-Wilk), and use the Normal approximation of the Binomial as a planning number at scale.



Goal of the day: the team fits a Normal to B’s daily eRPM and reads a typical day off it.


Pose: “Before we can ask ‘how likely is eRPM above $3.50?’, we need a model for a continuous quantity.” Frame the whole topic’s payoff (P(eRPM > 3.50), the 90th-pct day, the assumption check) as next lecture’s; today is the machinery. Get teams to gut-guess: “Is a $3.50 day rare or common?” (Hold the answer — it’s ~45%.)




Continuous ≠ discrete: P(X = x) = 0; probability is area under the density f(x). f(x) is a density, not a probability (it can exceed 1) — only areas are probabilities.
Uniform as the gentlest entry: probability ∝ interval length. Demo Slater’s Buffet (a=5, b=15): E(x)=10, Var=8.33, P(12 ≤ x ≤ 15) = base × height = 3 × 1/10 = 0.30. Probability is literally a rectangle’s area.
Normal: density formula on screen but “don’t tattoo it” — two parameters, μ (location) and σ (spread). Symmetric, bell, mean = median = mode. Walk the three-curve shape figure.
Empirical rule 68 / 95 / 99.7. Tie to Vungle: ~95% of B-days in 3.46 ± 2(0.34) = ($2.77, $4.15).
Standard Normal & z: z = (x − μ)/σ = “how many SDs from the mean.” One curve unlocks all Normals.
Anchor: Pep Zone demand ~ N(15, 6). P(stockout) = P(X > 20): z = (20−15)/6 = 0.83, P(Z > 0.83) = 0.2023. (Tease the inverse: reorder at 25 gal → 5% stockout. Full inverse is L2.)
Bridge to Vungle: fit N(3.459, 0.344) to the 30 B-days; a $3.50 day is z = 0.12 — barely above average.
Quick checks: “Why is P(X = exactly 3.50) zero?” “Does a bigger σ make the curve taller or flatter?” (flatter/wider). “What’s z for a $4.15 day?” (≈ +2 → top ~2.5%).




Teams compute mean/sd of the 30 B-day erpm values, apply the empirical rule, and standardize $3.50. - Mentor: make sure they filter to algorithm = "B" (not A, not all 60 rows). Use STDEV.S (sample), not STDEV.P. Push interpretation: “0.12 SD above average → is that a good day?” (No — basically average.) - Pitfalls: filtering wrong / mixing A and B; using population sd; reading z as a probability (it’s a count of SDs, not an area yet). - Answer key (instructor): - mean 3.459, sd 0.344 (B); A is mean 3.347, sd 0.217 (in case a team grabs A). - 95% empirical interval ≈ ($2.77, $4.15). - z for $3.50 = (3.50 − 3.459)/0.344 = 0.12 → an average day.



1–2 teams report. Land: (a) a continuous metric is described by a distribution, not a single number; standardizing collapses every Normal to N(0,1). (b) $3.50 is ~average (z = 0.12) — set up next lecture’s exact 45%. Surface the caveat: the empirical rule and z only work if the data are Normal — we have assumed it, not checked it. That check is Lecture 2.



Homework (team client): pick a continuous metric (price, time, spend, rating); compute mean/sd and an empirical-rule range; eyeball the histogram for “Normal-ish.” Preview: tail probabilities, percentiles, the normality check, and the Normal-approx-to-Binomial.





Goal of the day: the team turns the fitted Normal into a tail probability, a percentile, a trust check, and a planning number at scale.


Pose: “Tomorrow’s eRPM ~ N(3.459, 0.344). What’s P(eRPM > $3.50)? What defines a top-10% day? And — before we trust any of it — is the Normal assumption fair?” Re-collect gut guesses on P(> $3.50).




Tail probability. Standardize (\(z = 0.12\)) → right-tail area, or go straight in Excel: =1 - NORM.DIST(3.50, 3.459, 0.344, TRUE) = 0.4526. Plain English: ~45 of 100 days clear $3.50. Show the NORM.S.DIST(0.12) route gives the same number (two roads, one answer). Also the bad-day tail: NORM.DIST(3.00, …) = 0.091.
Percentile (inverse). “What value marks the top 10%?” =NORM.INV(0.90, 3.459, 0.344) = $3.90 (by hand: μ + z·σ = 3.459 + 1.282·0.344). Emphasize NORM.DIST ↔︎ NORM.INV are inverses.
Assumption check. QQ / normal-probability plot: points on a line → Normal OK; S-curve / fat tails → suspect. Companion Shapiro-Wilk (H₀: data are Normal); large p = no evidence against Normal. For eRPM_B: W = 0.961, p = 0.33 → do not reject; model is fair.
Normal approximation of the Binomial. Conditions n > 20, np ≥ 5, n(1−p) ≥ 5; then μ = np, σ = √(np(1−p)); continuity correction ±0.5 (a count of 10 → [9.5, 10.5]). Textbook anchor Binomial(100, 0.4): μ = 40, σ = 4.90. Then Vungle installs on a typical B-day.
Quick checks: “Right tail or left tail for P(eRPM > 3.50)?” “Is NORM.INV giving a value or an area?” (a value). “Why ±0.5?” (discrete bar has width 1).




Teams compute P(eRPM > $3.50), the 90th-percentile day, and eyeball the QQ plot. - Mentor: watch the right-tail subtraction (1 - NORM.DIST(...), not just NORM.DIST). Make them say the probability in business English, not just report a decimal. On the QQ plot, ask “any systematic curve, or do points hug the line?” - Pitfalls: forgetting 1 - for the right tail; passing a probability to NORM.DIST or a value to NORM.INV (swapped args); concluding “not Normal” from one or two off points (look for systematic departure). - Answer key (instructor): - P(eRPM > $3.50) = 0.4526 (~45% of days; almost a coin flip). - 90th-percentile day = $3.90 (75th ≈ $3.69; median = mean = $3.46). - P(eRPM < $3.00) = 0.091 (≈ 1 in 11) if asked. - Normality verdict: points hug the line, W = 0.96, p = 0.33 → Normal is a fair working model.



1–2 teams pitch. Walk the “Three Answers, One Model” table: every number came from the single fitted N(3.459, 0.344). Land: (a) z = (x−μ)/σ is the one move behind all of it; (b) NORM.DIST = probabilities, NORM.INV = percentiles, and they invert each other; (c) check the assumption before betting a decision on a Normal probability; (d) at huge n the Binomial is a Normal. Surface the pitfall: a beautiful probability on a wrong distribution is fiction — the QQ plot is not optional.



Homework (team client): compute one P(X > threshold) with NORM.DIST, one meaningful percentile with NORM.INV, and a QQ-plot normality check on the team’s continuous metric; name one decision it informs. Preview: these 30 days are a sample → Sampling & Estimation (estimating the true eRPM ± a margin).






data/vungle_daily.csv in Excel; filter algorithm = "B" for all eRPM work (30 rows).
Worked solutions: data/vungle_normal_analysis.xlsx (tabs: Raw_Data, eRPM_B_summary, Normal_Approx_Binomial, Anchors).
Excel functions to have ready: AVERAGE, STDEV.S, NORM.DIST, NORM.INV, NORM.S.DIST, NORM.S.INV. (No ToolPak needed for the Normal work; ToolPak Descriptive Statistics optional for the mean/sd in Sprint 1.)
Figures (all generated by _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic06_assets.R): uniform_slater.png, normal_shapes.png, empirical_rule.png, standardization.png, pepzone_stockout.png, vungle_pgt350.png, vungle_pct90.png, vungle_qq.png, normal_approx_binomial.png, continuity_correction.png, plus the three Excel panels (excel_normdist_vungle.png, excel_norminv_vungle.png, excel_normapprox_vungle.png).





eRPM_B ~ N(3.459, 0.344), n = 30. (eRPM_A ~ N(3.347, 0.217).)
P(eRPM_B > $3.50) = 0.4526 (z = 0.12). P(eRPM_B < $3.00) = 0.091.
90th percentile = $3.90 (z₀.₉₀ = 1.282). 75th ≈ $3.69. median = mean = $3.46.
Normality: Shapiro-Wilk W = 0.961, p = 0.33 → do not reject Normal.
Slater uniform: E = 10, Var = 8.33, P(12 ≤ x ≤ 15) = 0.30.
Pep Zone N(15, 6): P(X > 20) = 0.2023; reorder for 5% ≈ 25 gal.
Binomial(100, 0.4) → Normal(40, 4.90): P(30 ≤ X ≤ 50) ≈ 0.968 (with continuity correction).
Vungle installs, typical B-day: n = 527,513, p = 0.00354 → μ = 1,867, σ = 43.1; P(installs > 2000) = 0.001.





“f(x) is the probability of x.” (It’s a density; only areas are probabilities; P(X = x) = 0.)
“z is a probability.” (z is the number of SDs from the mean; you still need the area for z.)
“NORM.DIST gives the right-tail probability.” (It gives the left area P(X ≤ x); subtract from 1 for a right tail.)
“A big Shapiro-Wilk p-value proves the data are Normal.” (It only means no evidence against Normal — fine for a working model; you can’t prove Normal, especially at n = 30.)
“Forget the continuity correction.” (For the Binomial→Normal, a discrete count spans a half-unit each side; ±0.5 matters most at moderate n.)
“Higher mean eRPM (B) means B is the safe bet.” (B also has the larger σ — higher upside and higher downside; that variance discussion returns in the inference topics.)

title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
Goal of the day: the team fits a Normal to B’s daily eRPM and reads a typical day off it.


Pose: “Before we can ask ‘how likely is eRPM above $3.50?’, we need a model for a continuous quantity.” Frame the whole topic’s payoff (P(eRPM > 3.50), the 90th-pct day, the assumption check) as next lecture’s; today is the machinery. Get teams to gut-guess: “Is a $3.50 day rare or common?” (Hold the answer — it’s ~45%.)




Continuous ≠ discrete: P(X = x) = 0; probability is area under the density f(x). f(x) is a density, not a probability (it can exceed 1) — only areas are probabilities.
Uniform as the gentlest entry: probability ∝ interval length. Demo Slater’s Buffet (a=5, b=15): E(x)=10, Var=8.33, P(12 ≤ x ≤ 15) = base × height = 3 × 1/10 = 0.30. Probability is literally a rectangle’s area.
Normal: density formula on screen but “don’t tattoo it” — two parameters, μ (location) and σ (spread). Symmetric, bell, mean = median = mode. Walk the three-curve shape figure.
Empirical rule 68 / 95 / 99.7. Tie to Vungle: ~95% of B-days in 3.46 ± 2(0.34) = ($2.77, $4.15).
Standard Normal & z: z = (x − μ)/σ = “how many SDs from the mean.” One curve unlocks all Normals.
Anchor: Pep Zone demand ~ N(15, 6). P(stockout) = P(X > 20): z = (20−15)/6 = 0.83, P(Z > 0.83) = 0.2023. (Tease the inverse: reorder at 25 gal → 5% stockout. Full inverse is L2.)
Bridge to Vungle: fit N(3.459, 0.344) to the 30 B-days; a $3.50 day is z = 0.12 — barely above average.
Quick checks: “Why is P(X = exactly 3.50) zero?” “Does a bigger σ make the curve taller or flatter?” (flatter/wider). “What’s z for a $4.15 day?” (≈ +2 → top ~2.5%).




Teams compute mean/sd of the 30 B-day erpm values, apply the empirical rule, and standardize $3.50. - Mentor: make sure they filter to algorithm = "B" (not A, not all 60 rows). Use STDEV.S (sample), not STDEV.P. Push interpretation: “0.12 SD above average → is that a good day?” (No — basically average.) - Pitfalls: filtering wrong / mixing A and B; using population sd; reading z as a probability (it’s a count of SDs, not an area yet). - Answer key (instructor): - mean 3.459, sd 0.344 (B); A is mean 3.347, sd 0.217 (in case a team grabs A). - 95% empirical interval ≈ ($2.77, $4.15). - z for $3.50 = (3.50 − 3.459)/0.344 = 0.12 → an average day.



1–2 teams report. Land: (a) a continuous metric is described by a distribution, not a single number; standardizing collapses every Normal to N(0,1). (b) $3.50 is ~average (z = 0.12) — set up next lecture’s exact 45%. Surface the caveat: the empirical rule and z only work if the data are Normal — we have assumed it, not checked it. That check is Lecture 2.



Homework (team client): pick a continuous metric (price, time, spend, rating); compute mean/sd and an empirical-rule range; eyeball the histogram for “Normal-ish.” Preview: tail probabilities, percentiles, the normality check, and the Normal-approx-to-Binomial.

title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
Goal of the day: the team turns the fitted Normal into a tail probability, a percentile, a trust check, and a planning number at scale.


Pose: “Tomorrow’s eRPM ~ N(3.459, 0.344). What’s P(eRPM > $3.50)? What defines a top-10% day? And — before we trust any of it — is the Normal assumption fair?” Re-collect gut guesses on P(> $3.50).




Tail probability. Standardize (\(z = 0.12\)) → right-tail area, or go straight in Excel: =1 - NORM.DIST(3.50, 3.459, 0.344, TRUE) = 0.4526. Plain English: ~45 of 100 days clear $3.50. Show the NORM.S.DIST(0.12) route gives the same number (two roads, one answer). Also the bad-day tail: NORM.DIST(3.00, …) = 0.091.
Percentile (inverse). “What value marks the top 10%?” =NORM.INV(0.90, 3.459, 0.344) = $3.90 (by hand: μ + z·σ = 3.459 + 1.282·0.344). Emphasize NORM.DIST ↔︎ NORM.INV are inverses.
Assumption check. QQ / normal-probability plot: points on a line → Normal OK; S-curve / fat tails → suspect. Companion Shapiro-Wilk (H₀: data are Normal); large p = no evidence against Normal. For eRPM_B: W = 0.961, p = 0.33 → do not reject; model is fair.
Normal approximation of the Binomial. Conditions n > 20, np ≥ 5, n(1−p) ≥ 5; then μ = np, σ = √(np(1−p)); continuity correction ±0.5 (a count of 10 → [9.5, 10.5]). Textbook anchor Binomial(100, 0.4): μ = 40, σ = 4.90. Then Vungle installs on a typical B-day.
Quick checks: “Right tail or left tail for P(eRPM > 3.50)?” “Is NORM.INV giving a value or an area?” (a value). “Why ±0.5?” (discrete bar has width 1).




Teams compute P(eRPM > $3.50), the 90th-percentile day, and eyeball the QQ plot. - Mentor: watch the right-tail subtraction (1 - NORM.DIST(...), not just NORM.DIST). Make them say the probability in business English, not just report a decimal. On the QQ plot, ask “any systematic curve, or do points hug the line?” - Pitfalls: forgetting 1 - for the right tail; passing a probability to NORM.DIST or a value to NORM.INV (swapped args); concluding “not Normal” from one or two off points (look for systematic departure). - Answer key (instructor): - P(eRPM > $3.50) = 0.4526 (~45% of days; almost a coin flip). - 90th-percentile day = $3.90 (75th ≈ $3.69; median = mean = $3.46). - P(eRPM < $3.00) = 0.091 (≈ 1 in 11) if asked. - Normality verdict: points hug the line, W = 0.96, p = 0.33 → Normal is a fair working model.



1–2 teams pitch. Walk the “Three Answers, One Model” table: every number came from the single fitted N(3.459, 0.344). Land: (a) z = (x−μ)/σ is the one move behind all of it; (b) NORM.DIST = probabilities, NORM.INV = percentiles, and they invert each other; (c) check the assumption before betting a decision on a Normal probability; (d) at huge n the Binomial is a Normal. Surface the pitfall: a beautiful probability on a wrong distribution is fiction — the QQ plot is not optional.



Homework (team client): compute one P(X > threshold) with NORM.DIST, one meaningful percentile with NORM.INV, and a QQ-plot normality check on the team’s continuous metric; name one decision it informs. Preview: these 30 days are a sample → Sampling & Estimation (estimating the true eRPM ± a margin).

title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
data/vungle_daily.csv in Excel; filter algorithm = "B" for all eRPM work (30 rows).
Worked solutions: data/vungle_normal_analysis.xlsx (tabs: Raw_Data, eRPM_B_summary, Normal_Approx_Binomial, Anchors).
Excel functions to have ready: AVERAGE, STDEV.S, NORM.DIST, NORM.INV, NORM.S.DIST, NORM.S.INV. (No ToolPak needed for the Normal work; ToolPak Descriptive Statistics optional for the mean/sd in Sprint 1.)
Figures (all generated by _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic06_assets.R): uniform_slater.png, normal_shapes.png, empirical_rule.png, standardization.png, pepzone_stockout.png, vungle_pgt350.png, vungle_pct90.png, vungle_qq.png, normal_approx_binomial.png, continuity_correction.png, plus the three Excel panels (excel_normdist_vungle.png, excel_norminv_vungle.png, excel_normapprox_vungle.png).

title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
eRPM_B ~ N(3.459, 0.344), n = 30. (eRPM_A ~ N(3.347, 0.217).)
P(eRPM_B > $3.50) = 0.4526 (z = 0.12). P(eRPM_B < $3.00) = 0.091.
90th percentile = $3.90 (z₀.₉₀ = 1.282). 75th ≈ $3.69. median = mean = $3.46.
Normality: Shapiro-Wilk W = 0.961, p = 0.33 → do not reject Normal.
Slater uniform: E = 10, Var = 8.33, P(12 ≤ x ≤ 15) = 0.30.
Pep Zone N(15, 6): P(X > 20) = 0.2023; reorder for 5% ≈ 25 gal.
Binomial(100, 0.4) → Normal(40, 4.90): P(30 ≤ X ≤ 50) ≈ 0.968 (with continuity correction).
Vungle installs, typical B-day: n = 527,513, p = 0.00354 → μ = 1,867, σ = 43.1; P(installs > 2000) = 0.001.

title: Lecture Guide — T06 · Continuous Probability Distributions
text:
“f(x) is the probability of x.” (It’s a density; only areas are probabilities; P(X = x) = 0.)
“z is a probability.” (z is the number of SDs from the mean; you still need the area for z.)
“NORM.DIST gives the right-tail probability.” (It gives the left area P(X ≤ x); subtract from 1 for a right tail.)
“A big Shapiro-Wilk p-value proves the data are Normal.” (It only means no evidence against Normal — fine for a working model; you can’t prove Normal, especially at n = 30.)
“Forget the continuity correction.” (For the Binomial→Normal, a discrete count spans a half-unit each side; ±0.5 matters most at moderate n.)
“Higher mean eRPM (B) means B is the safe bet.” (B also has the larger σ — higher upside and higher downside; that variance discussion returns in the inference topics.)

title: Lecture Guide — T07 · Sampling Distributions & Interval Estimation
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv, data/vungle_estimation.xlsx · Lectures: 2 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: turn a 30-day point estimate into a confidence interval for a mean (t) and a proportion (z), read the margin of error correctly, and compute the sample size needed for a target precision — then write Vungle’s estimation memo.



Goal of the day: students see why a sample mean wobbles, quantify the wobble (standard error), and trust the bell (CLT) — so “30 days” becomes a number they can reason about, not just a guess.


Pose: “B’s 30-day mean eRPM was ~$3.46. June is over — what is B’s TRUE mean going forward, and how close can 30 days get us?” Get teams to commit to a gut answer: “is 30 days enough?” (most say yes; today complicates that). Frame the two-lecture arc (Lec 1 = wobble; Lec 2 = the interval).




Population vs. sample vs. inference (the population_sample_inference.png schematic). \(\mu\) is fixed and unknown; \(\bar{x}\) is what we compute and it changes sample to sample.
Point estimation: parameter→statistic table (\(\mu\to\bar{x}\), \(\sigma\to s\), \(p\to\bar{p}\)). Anchor = St. Andrew’s College (n=30: \(\bar{x}=1684\) vs true \(\mu=1697\); \(\bar{p}=0.67\) vs 0.72). Then the Vungle point estimates (\(\bar{x}_A=3.347\), \(\bar{x}_B=3.459\)).
Sampling distribution of \(\bar{x}\): drill the distinction — distribution of one sample’s data vs. distribution of the mean across many samples. \(E(\bar{x})=\mu\) (unbiased), \(\sigma_{\bar{x}}=\sigma/\sqrt{n}\). Hammer “precision depends on n, NOT N” (St. Andrew’s: SE drops 15.96→8.74 going n=30→100).
CLT: \(n\ge 30\) → \(\bar{x}\) approx Normal regardless of population shape (clt_population_shapes.png). Worked probability: P(\(\bar{x}\) within 10 of \(\mu\)) = 0.4691 — a 47% chance with n=30 surprises them.
Sampling distribution of \(\bar{p}\): \(\sigma_{\bar p}=\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}\); success-failure check \(np\ge5\), \(n(1-p)\ge5\).
Quick checks: “If I take a different 30 days, does \(\mu\) change? Does \(\bar{x}\)?” (only \(\bar{x}\)). “Which shrinks the standard error — a bigger population or a bigger sample?” (sample).




Teams open vungle_estimation.xlsx (sheet eRPM_by_day), get \(\bar{x}\) and \(s\) for erpm_B, compute SE at n=30, then what SE would be at n=120. - Mentor as they work: make sure they use =STDEV.S (sample SD), not STDEV.P. Push the interpretation of SE: “the typical distance between a 30-day mean and the truth.” Ask why n=120 only halves it (because \(\sqrt{4}=2\)). - Pitfalls: confusing \(s\) (spread of the data) with SE (spread of the mean); expecting SE to halve when n doubles (it doesn’t — needs 4×). - Answer key (instructor): \(\bar{x}_B = \$3.459\), \(s_B = \$0.3444\). SE at n=30 = \(0.3444/\sqrt{30}
  = \$0.0629\). SE at n=120 = \(0.3444/\sqrt{120} = \$0.0314\) — exactly half (4× the data). Verdict: precise-ish but a $0.06 wobble is large next to the $0.11 A-vs-B gap → “test longer” is defensible.



1–2 teams report SE numbers. Land: the sample mean is unbiased but wobbles by \(\sigma/\sqrt{n}\); the CLT makes that wobble Normal for n≥30; precision is bought with n and only as \(\sqrt{n}\). Surface the pitfall: “30 days felt like a lot until you saw the standard error.”



Preview Lec 2: turn the standard error into a confidence interval — “$3.46, give or take how much?”





Goal of the day: the team writes Vungle’s estimation memo — 95% CIs for mean eRPM (t) and the conversion rate, plus the sample size for ±$0.05 precision.


Pose: “Don’t just tell me $3.46 — give me the range I can bank on, and tell me how many test-days it takes to cut that range in half.” This is the manager’s real ask.




Margin of error: interval = point estimate ± E, where E = multiplier × SE. ~1.96 SEs capture the middle 95% of a Normal.
t vs z: σ is never known → estimate with s → fatter tails → t distribution, df = n−1 (t_vs_z.png). By df=29, t≈z. Excel multiplier: =T.INV.2T(0.05, df).
t-interval for a mean: \(\bar{x}\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1}\,s/\sqrt{n}\). Anchor = Apartment Rents (n=16, \(\bar{x}=750\), s=55 → 95% CI = ($720.69, $779.31)). Demo =CONFIDENCE.T(0.05,55,16)=29.31.
CI interpretation drill (ci_mechanics.png): RIGHT = “~95% of intervals built this way contain μ”; WRONG = “95% chance μ is in this interval.” The interval is random, μ is fixed.
Vungle eRPM CI (the five steps): n=30, \(\bar{x}_B=3.459\), \(s_B=0.344\), \(t_{0.025,29}=2.045\), E=0.1286 → ($3.330, $3.588). Show A’s interval too and that they overlap.
Proportion CI: \(\bar{p}\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\bar p(1-\bar p)/n}\) (z, not t). Anchor = PSI poll (n=500, \(\bar p=0.44\) → (0.3965, 0.4835)). Vungle conversion via daily rates (t-interval).
Three levers of width (confidence ↑ wider, s ↑ wider, n ↑ narrower) → sample size: invert the MOE formula, \(n=(z\,s/E)^2\), round UP. Excel: =CEILING((NORM.S.INV(0.975)*s/E)^2,1).
Quick checks: “Why t here but z for the proportion?” (no separate SD to estimate for a proportion). “Does a 99% interval get wider or narrower than 95%?” (wider).




Teams build: (1) 95% CI for mean eRPM (B), (2) 95% CI for daily conversion rate (B), (3) days needed for ±$0.05 eRPM. Use vungle_estimation.xlsx. - Mentor as they work: check they read =CONFIDENCE.T margin (not the half-width of the data); for sample size, make sure they round up with CEILING and use s=0.344 as the planning value (the same SD as the eRPM CI — not A’s 0.217). Push each team to write the plain-English reading. - Pitfalls: using z instead of t for the mean CI (small but tell them why t is correct); using A’s SD for B’s sample size; forgetting to round up; reporting the SE as if it were the margin. - Answer key (instructor): - Mean eRPM (B): \(\bar{x}=3.459\), \(s=0.3444\), SE=0.0629, \(t_{0.025,29}=2.045\), E=0.1286 → 95% CI = ($3.330, $3.588). (A’s, for comparison: \(\bar{x}=3.347\), s=0.2166, E=0.0809 → ($3.266, $3.428).) - Conversion rate (B), daily-rate t-interval: mean=0.3538%, s=0.0231%, SE=0.0042%, E=0.0086% → 95% CI = (0.345%, 0.362%). - Sample size for ±$0.05 (mean eRPM, s=0.344, z=1.96): \(n=(1.96\times0.344/0.05)^2=182.3 \to\) 183 days. (For ±$0.10: \(n=45.6\to\) 46 days — halving E costs ~4× the days.) - PSI sample-size anchor (if asked): E=0.03, 99%, \(z=2.576\): p*=0.44 → 1817; conservative p*=0.5 → 1844. Discount Sounds mean (σ=4500, E=500, 95%) → 312.



1–2 teams pitch the memo. Land: the CI turns a point estimate into a defensible range; the margin of error is what a manager negotiates; “95% confident” describes the procedure, not a probability for one interval. Surface the limit: A’s and B’s eRPM intervals overlap — estimation alone can’t declare a winner; that’s hypothesis testing (next topic). Note that today’s SE and t machinery becomes the test statistic.



Homework (team client): pick one key mean and one key proportion in your case; build a 95% CI for each (one-sentence reading), and compute the sample size to halve one margin — justify the planning value. Preview: Hypothesis Testing — fund B only if its mean eRPM beats a benchmark.






vungle_daily.csv / vungle_daily.xlsx and vungle_estimation.xlsx (sheets: Raw_Data, eRPM_by_day, CI_mean_eRPM, CI_conversion, Sample_Size) in the topic data/ folder.
Analysis ToolPak installed; Descriptive Statistics with “Confidence Level for mean” checked.
Key Excel functions to have ready: =AVERAGE, =STDEV.S, =T.INV.2T, =CONFIDENCE.T, =NORM.S.INV, =CEILING.
Regenerate assets if data changes: Rscript _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic07_assets.R.





“Standard deviation = standard error.” \(s\) describes the data; SE = \(s/\sqrt{n}\) describes the mean. The interval uses SE.
“Precision depends on how big the population is.” No — only on n (and s). A 30-day sample is as precise for billions of impressions as for thousands.
“There’s a 95% chance μ is in this interval.” μ is fixed; the interval is random. 95% refers to the long-run hit rate of the procedure.
“Doubling the sample halves the error.” It takes 4× the sample to halve the margin (\(\sqrt{n}\)).
“Overlapping CIs prove the means are equal / different.” Overlap is suggestive, not a test — that’s what hypothesis testing is for.
“Use t for the proportion too.” Proportions use z — there’s no separately estimated SD.

Push each team to write the plain-English reading. - Pitfalls: using z instead of t for the mean CI (small but tell them why t is correct); using A’s SD for B’s sample size; forgetting to round up; reporting the SE as if it were the margin. - Answer key (instructor): - Mean eRPM (B): \(\bar{x}=3.459\), \(s=0.3444\), SE=0.0629, \(t_{0.025,29}=2.045\), E=0.1286 → 95% CI = ($3.330, $3.588). (A’s, for comparison: \(\bar{x}=3.347\), s=0.2166, E=0.0809 → ($3.266, $3.428).) - Conversion rate (B), daily-rate t-interval: mean=0.3538%, s=0.0231%, SE=0.0042%, E=0.0086% → 95% CI = (0.345%, 0.362%). - Sample size for ±$0.05 (mean eRPM, s=0.344, z=1.96): \(n=(1.96\times0.344/0.05)^2=182.3 \to\) 183 days. (For ±$0.10: \(n=45.6\to\) 46 days — halving E costs ~4× the days.) - PSI sample-size anchor (if asked): E=0.03, 99%, \(z=2.576\): p*=0.44 → 1817; conservative p*=0.5 → 1844. Discount Sounds mean (σ=4500, E=500, 95%) → 312.



1–2 teams pitch the memo. Land: the CI turns a point estimate into a defensible range; the margin of error is what a manager negotiates; “95% confident” describes the procedure, not a probability for one interval. Surface the limit: A’s and B’s eRPM intervals overlap — estimation alone can’t declare a winner; that’s hypothesis testing (next topic). Note that today’s SE and t machinery becomes the test statistic.



Homework (team client): pick one key mean and one key proportion in your case; build a 95% CI for each (one-sentence reading), and compute the sample size to halve one margin — justify the planning value. Preview: Hypothesis Testing — fund B only if its mean eRPM beats a benchmark.

title: Lecture Guide — T07 · Sampling Distributions & Interval Estimation
text:
vungle_daily.csv / vungle_daily.xlsx and vungle_estimation.xlsx (sheets: Raw_Data, eRPM_by_day, CI_mean_eRPM, CI_conversion, Sample_Size) in the topic data/ folder.
Analysis ToolPak installed; Descriptive Statistics with “Confidence Level for mean” checked.
Key Excel functions to have ready: =AVERAGE, =STDEV.S, =T.INV.2T, =CONFIDENCE.T, =NORM.S.INV, =CEILING.
Regenerate assets if data changes: Rscript _adm_stuff/_redesign/scripts/make_topic07_assets.R.

title: Lecture Guide — T07 · Sampling Distributions & Interval Estimation
text:
“Standard deviation = standard error.” \(s\) describes the data; SE = \(s/\sqrt{n}\) describes the mean. The interval uses SE.
“Precision depends on how big the population is.” No — only on n (and s). A 30-day sample is as precise for billions of impressions as for thousands.
“There’s a 95% chance μ is in this interval.” μ is fixed; the interval is random. 95% refers to the long-run hit rate of the procedure.
“Doubling the sample halves the error.” It takes 4× the sample to halve the margin (\(\sqrt{n}\)).
“Overlapping CIs prove the means are equal / different.” Overlap is suggestive, not a test — that’s what hypothesis testing is for.
“Use t for the proportion too.” Proportions use z — there’s no separately estimated SD.

title: Lecture Guide — T09 · Hypothesis Testing II (Two Populations)
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_erpm_paired.csv, data/vungle_daily.csv · Lectures: 1 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: pick and run the right two-sample test (paired t, independent/Welch t, two-proportion z), see how the design changes the conclusion, and write a defensible roll-out recommendation when metrics disagree.


Goal of the day: the team makes the Vungle roll-out call, backed by the right tests.


Pose: “B’s daily eRPM ran ~$0.11 above A’s. Real improvement or noise? Roll out B?” Tell them the plan: I demo each test on a textbook example → your team runs it on Vungle (two sprints) → we debrief the call.




Matched vs independent design first. Paired t = reduce two samples to differences dᵢ = B−A.
Demo the paired t on the Express Deliveries textbook anchor (ToolPak: t-Test: Paired Two Sample for Means), then walk the Vungle paired result.
Quick check: “Why pair the days at all?” (removes day-to-day market noise).




Teams run the paired t on vungle_erpm_paired.csv (Var 1 = erpm_B, Var 2 = erpm_A) + build the CI. - Mentor: make sure Var 1 = B so the sign is positive; have them read P(T<=t) one-tail. - Answer key: t = 3.325, one-tail p ≈ 0.0012 (two-tail ≈ 0.0024), 95% CI ≈ ($0.043, $0.181) → reject H₀, B’s lift is real.




Independent means: pooled vs Welch (default). Show the same Vungle data analyzed as independent → t = 1.51, p = 0.138 (fail to reject): same data, different design, opposite call.
Two-proportion z on conversion (installs/impressions); pool under H₀; no ToolPak tool → formula cells.
Reveal The Surprise: B converts less (z = −29.56) but earns more eRPM.




Teams write CEO Jaffer’s roll-out recommendation given the conflict (3 sentences: call · number · caveat). - Mentor: push them to choose the metric that matches the business model, not just the bigger number. - Answer key / model call: roll out B — eRPM is the revenue-aligned metric; lift is solid ($0.04–$0.18 / 1,000, ≈ $26.5K/month on June traffic); monitor the install funnel because the conversion rate is lower. Acceptable to argue “monitor before full rollout” if justified.



1–2 teams pitch. Land: design drives conclusions (paired vs independent); large n makes everything “significant” — judge effect size; test results are inputs to a decision, not the decision. Walk the bridge table.



Homework (team client): find two groups in your case (two segments / periods / treatment vs control) and run the right two-sample test — justify paired vs independent. Preview: Simple Regression.





vungle_erpm_paired.csv and vungle_daily.csv in Excel; worked solutions in data/vungle_ab_analysis.xlsx. ToolPak installed.





“99.9% confident it’s not luck.” (p-value is P(data | H₀), not P(B is better)) — the deck already rewords this; reinforce it.
“Independent t is fine for the same 30 days.” (ignoring pairing throws away the design’s power)
“z = −29.56 means it matters.” (significance ≠ practical importance at n in the hundreds of millions)

title: Lecture Guide — T03 · Probability
text:
Spine case: Vungle A/B · Data: data/vungle_daily.csv, data/vungle_probability.xlsx · Lectures: 1 · Software: Excel + Analysis ToolPak Students leave able to: read the ad funnel as a chain of conditional probabilities, chain the steps with the multiplication law, run a Bayes update (given an install, which algorithm?), and tell apart the three answers — marginal, conditional, reverse — to the same question.


Goal of the day: the team builds the Vungle funnel as a probability tree and reverses it with Bayes.


Pose all three at once: “Chance a random impression installs? If we know it was clicked, what changes? We just saw one install — how likely was it B?” Get a gut answer to each before any math. Land that these are three different numbers (marginal / conditional / reverse) — the day’s spine.




Experiment → sample space → event, on “serve one impression.” Probabilities in \([0,1]\), sum to 1.
Three sources of numbers: classical / relative frequency (Vungle’s world) / subjective.
Basic relationships: complement, addition law (subtract the overlap), intersection (joint), mutually exclusive.
Demo (anchor): the BestBuy TV/DVD contingency table (100 customers). Read a joint (30/100), a marginal (35/100), and the addition law (\(0.60 + 0.40 - 0.30 = 0.70\)). This is the structural twin of the Vungle funnel table.
Quick check: “Is \(P(\text{Flat Screen})\) a joint or a marginal?” (marginal — it’s a column total).





Conditional probability \(P(A\mid B) = P(A\cap B)/P(B)\) = shrink the sample space to \(B\). Demo on BestBuy: \(P(\text{DVD}\mid\text{HDTV}) = 30/40 = 0.75\).
The Vungle funnel = a chain of conditionals. Walk the tree figure for Algorithm A.
Multiplication law chains the three steps back to the marginal install rate; the product must equal installs/impressions (built-in error check).
Independence = conditioning changes nothing; show the funnel steps are not independent (click rate given completion 5.68% vs. unconditional 5.07%). Warn: independent ≠ mutually exclusive.
Demo (Excel): =SUMIF(algorithm,"A",impressions) etc.; every rate is one cell ÷ another.




Teams build Algorithm B’s funnel from vungle_probability.xlsx (or vungle_daily.csv): three conditional rates, chain them, sketch the tree. - Mentor as they work: make them keep the conditioning straight — denominator is the previous stage’s count, not impressions. Probe: “20× jump from 0.35% to 7.19% — what does conditioning on a click buy you?” Push the “where does B leak most?” read. - Pitfalls: dividing every stage by impressions (gives the marginal path probabilities, not the step conditionals); mixing A and B rows; forgetting completes is the denominator for the click step. - Answer key (Algorithm B, June totals): impressions 15,825,376 · completes 14,048,440 · clicks 779,552 · installs 56,031. - $P() = 14{,}048{,}440/15{,}825{,}376 = $ 88.77% - $P() = 779{,}552/14{,}048{,}440 = $ 5.55% - $P() = 56{,}031/779{,}552 = $ 7.19% - product $= 0.8877 = $ 0.3541% \(= 56{,}031/15{,}825{,}376\) ✓ - Read: B’s biggest leak is the click step (~5.5% click-through, like A); its install-given- click (7.19%) is a touch below A’s (7.93%) — that lower last-step rate is why B’s marginal install rate (0.354%) sits under A’s (0.403%).




Reverse the conditioning: have \(P(\text{install}\mid\text{algo})\), want \(P(\text{algo}\mid\text{install})\).
Prior / likelihood / posterior. The formula = (this joint) ÷ (sum of all joints) = total-probability denominator. A table does the bookkeeping.
Demo (anchor): the test-market Bayes table (priors 0.80/0.15/0.05; likelihoods 0.02/0.24/0.98 for a large test share). Major-success posterior jumps 5% → 49%. (See answer key below for the arithmetic.)
Demo (Vungle): priors = impression shares (A 93.73%, B 6.27%); likelihoods = install rates (A 0.4025%, B 0.3541%). Posterior $P(B) = $ 5.56% — below the 6.27% prior.
The gut-check: an install is weak evidence against B, because B converts slightly less.




Teams reproduce \(P(B\mid\text{install})\) in vungle_probability.xlsx (priors, likelihoods, joints, sum, posterior). If short on time, run this as a guided whole-room walk instead of a team sprint. - Answer key: prior \(P(B) = 6.27\%\) → posterior $P(B) = {0.9373 + 0.0627} = = $ 5.56%. (Shortcut sanity check: \(P(B\mid\text{install}) = x_B/(x_A+x_B) = 56{,}031/1{,}007{,}697 = 5.56\%\).)



1–2 teams pitch their B funnel. Land the Manager’s Trap: marginal (0.40%), conditional given a click (7.93%), and reverse \(P(B\mid\text{install})\) (5.56%) are three different numbers for the same funnel — match the number to the question. Surface: “most installs come from A” is true only because A serves 15× the traffic (base rate), not because A converts better. Bridge: the conditional rates become the parameters of the probability models in the next topics (a click is a Bernoulli trial, \(p\approx5\%\)).



Homework (team client): build a contingency table of two categorical variables; compute one marginal, one conditional, and one Bayes (reverse) probability; say which managerial question each answers. Preview: Decision Analysis — turning these probabilities into payoffs, expected value, and the value of information on the Vungle rollout.





vungle_daily.csv and vungle_probability.xlsx open in Excel (the .xlsx has tabs: vungle_daily, Funnel_Totals, Conditional_Rates, Bayes_Install, BestBuy).
ToolPak not strictly needed today (probabilities are SUMIF ratios), but keep it loaded for continuity.
Figures the deck pulls: funnel_tree_A.png, bayes_install_table.png, bayes_prior_posterior.png, excel_conditional_cells.png, excel_bayes_cells.png (all from make_topic03_assets.R).





Funnel totals. A: impr 236,459,402 · comp 211,110,683 · clk 11,996,954 · inst 951,666. B: impr 15,825,376 · comp 14,048,440 · clk 779,552 · inst 56,031.
Algorithm A conditionals: P(comp|imp) 89.28% · P(click|comp) 5.68% · P(install|click) 7.93% · marginal P(install|imp) 0.4025% (= product, ✓).
Algorithm B conditionals: 88.77% · 5.55% · 7.19% · marginal 0.3541%.
Independence check: P(click|complete) 5.68% vs. P(click|impression) 5.07% → not independent.
Bayes (install → B): prior 6.27%, P(install) 0.3994%, posterior 5.56%. (Click → B: 6.10%.)
Test-market anchor: joints 0.0160 / 0.0360 / 0.0490; P(Large) 0.1010; posteriors 0.158 / 0.356 / 0.485.





Marginal = conditional. “0.40% of impressions install, so a clicked impression installs 0.40% of the time.” No — conditioning on the click jumps it to 7.93%. Reinforce: the denominator changed.
Counts answer rate questions. “B made only 56K installs vs A’s 952K, so A is better.” That’s the traffic split (1/16 to B), not algorithm quality — compare rates.
Posterior = likelihood. Reading \(P(B\mid\text{install})\) off the install rate alone, ignoring the tiny 6.27% prior. Base rates dominate; Bayes forces you to weight by the prior.
Independent = mutually exclusive. Opposite ideas: mutually exclusive (nonzero) events are maximally dependent — one occurring drives the other to zero.
Tree branches don’t have to sum to 1. At each node the branch probabilities are conditionals and do sum to 1 within that node (install + no-install = 100%).
